# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]`

**Explain the key features of Julia that make it suitable for data science applications. List any four with brief descriptions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **High performance via JIT compilation:** Julia uses LLVM-based JIT compilation to convert source code into optimized native machine code at runtime, achieving speeds comparable to C while retaining high-level syntax. *(1 mark)*

2. **Multiple dispatch:** Julia selects which method of a function to execute based on the types of *all* its arguments. This enables polymorphic, extensible code and unlocks compiler optimizations. *(1 mark)*

3. **Dynamic typing with optional static typing:** Variables can be used without declaring types (developer productivity) while functions and structs can be annotated with types (compiler efficiency). *(0.5 mark)*

4. **Built-in package manager and rich ecosystem:** The `Pkg` system and the General Registry give access to mature data science libraries like `DataFrames.jl`, `CSV.jl`, `Plots.jl`, `MLJ.jl` natively integrated. *(0.5 mark)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model QP]`

**Differentiate between a Tuple and an Array in Julia with a suitable example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | `Array` | `Tuple` |
|---|---|---|
| **Mutability** | Mutable — elements can be changed | Immutable — cannot be modified after creation |
| **Element types** | Typically homogeneous | Heterogeneous by design |
| **Syntax** | Square brackets `[1,2,3]` | Parentheses `(1, "x", 3.14)` |
| **Use case** | Numerical data, vectors, matrices | Function args, multiple returns, fixed records |

**Example for Array** *(1 mark)*:
```julia
arr = [10, 20, 30]
arr[1] = 99        # Valid: arr becomes [99, 20, 30]
push!(arr, 40)     # Valid
```

**Example for Tuple** *(1 mark)*:
```julia
tup = (10, "hello", 3.14)
tup[1] = 99        # ERROR: MethodError (immutable)
```

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** *Module 1 — Internal Choice Question. Choose either Question A OR Question B.*

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Module 1 Internal Choice]`

**Sub-part (a) — 7 marks:**
Explain the installation procedure of Julia using `juliaup` for a Linux/Windows system. Verify the installation and demonstrate the use of the REPL for basic arithmetic operations. List any five operators unique to Julia with examples.

**Sub-part (b) — 7 marks:**
With a suitable Julia program, illustrate the creation of a `Vector`, a `Matrix`, and demonstrate indexing, slicing, broadcasting, and the use of `push!`/`pop!` mutation functions.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Installation Steps (4 marks):**

For **Linux/macOS**, open terminal and run:
```bash
curl -fsSL https://install.julialang.org | sh
```
The script installs `juliaup` (the version manager) into `~/.juliaup` and adds it to your shell `PATH`.

For **Windows**, download the `juliaup` installer `.exe` from the official Julia website and run it. Alternatively use `winget install julialang -e` in PowerShell.

Add a Julia channel:
```bash
juliaup add release     # latest stable
juliaup add lts         # long-term support
juliaup default release # set default
```

**Verification (1 mark):**
```bash
$ julia --version
julia version 1.10.x

$ julia
julia> versioninfo()
```

**REPL Arithmetic Demo (1 mark):**
```julia
julia> 5 + 3       # 8
julia> 2^10        # 1024
julia> 7 ÷ 2       # 3  (integer division)
julia> 7 % 2       # 1  (remainder)
julia> 3.14 * 2    # 6.28
```

**Five Julia-Unique Operators (1 mark):**

| Operator | Meaning | Example | Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| `÷` | Integer division | `7 ÷ 2` | `3` |
| `!=` | Not equal | `5 != 3` | `true` |
| `===` | Egal (identity) | `[1,2] === [1,2]` | `false` |
| `=>` | Pair constructor | `:x => 1` | `:x => 1` |
| `.<op>` | Broadcasting | `[1,2] .+ 10` | `[11, 12]` |

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

```julia
# 1. Vector creation (1 mark)
v = [10, 20, 30, 40, 50]
typeof(v)               # Vector{Int64}

# 2. Matrix creation (1 mark)
M = [1 2 3; 4 5 6; 7 8 9]
size(M)                 # (3, 3)

# 3. Indexing and slicing (2 marks)
v[1]                    # 10          (Julia is 1-indexed)
v[end]                  # 50
v[2:4]                  # [20, 30, 40]
M[2, 3]                 # 6  (row 2, col 3)
M[:, 1]                 # [1, 4, 7]   (entire first column)

# 4. Broadcasting (1.5 marks)
v .+ 100                # [110, 120, 130, 140, 150]
v .* 2                  # [20, 40, 60, 80, 100]
M .^ 2                  # element-wise square each entry

# 5. push! and pop! (1.5 marks)
push!(v, 60)            # v = [10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60]
pop!(v)                 # returns 60; v = [10, 20, 30, 40, 50]
```

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Module 1 Internal Choice — Alternative]`

**Sub-part (a) — 7 marks:**
Compare and contrast the four built-in data structures in Julia — Array, Tuple, Dict, and Set — with respect to mutability, ordering, indexing, and use cases. Provide a syntactic example for each.

**Sub-part (b) — 7 marks:**
Write a Julia program that creates a `Dict` to store student names and their marks in three subjects. Use the program to: (i) compute the average marks per student, (ii) find the topper using `maximum`, (iii) use a `Set` to list the unique subjects.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Structure | Mutability | Order | Indexing | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `Array` | Mutable | Preserved | Integer 1-based | Numerical data, matrices |
| `Tuple` | Immutable | Preserved | Integer 1-based | Records, multi-return |
| `Dict` | Mutable | Not guaranteed | By key | Lookup tables |
| `Set` | Mutable | Not guaranteed | Membership only | Uniqueness, set algebra |

**Syntactic Examples (4 marks — 1 mark each):**

```julia
# 1. Array
arr = [1, 2, 3, 4]
arr[1] = 99            # valid (mutable)

# 2. Tuple
tup = (1, "Julia", 3.14, :ok)
name, ver, pi_val, status = tup   # destructuring

# 3. Dict
d = Dict("a" => 1, "b" => 2)
d["c"] = 3
get(d, "x", 0)         # safe access with default

# 4. Set
s = Set([1, 2, 2, 3])  # duplicates removed → Set([1, 2, 3])
push!(s, 4)
intersect(s, Set([3, 4, 5]))   # Set([3, 4])
```

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

```julia
# Step 1: Create the dictionary (2 marks)
marks = Dict(
    "Alice"   => (85, 92, 78),
    "Bob"     => (90, 88, 95),
    "Charlie" => (70, 75, 80)
)

# Step 2: Compute average marks per student (2 marks)
averages = Dict(name => sum(m) / length(m) for (name, m) in marks)
# Result: Dict with "Alice"=>85.0, "Bob"=>91.0, "Charlie"=>75.0

# Step 3: Find the topper using maximum (1.5 marks)
topper = findmax(averages)        # ("Bob", 91.0)
println("Topper: $(topper[1]) with $(topper[2]) average")

# Step 4: Use a Set for unique subjects (1.5 marks)
subjects = Set(["Math", "Physics", "Chemistry", "Math"])
# Duplicates removed → Set(["Math", "Physics", "Chemistry"])
```

**Sample Output:**
```
Topper: Bob with 91.0 average
```

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Off-by-one indexing:** Writing `v[0]` instead of `v[1]` — costs full marks on indexing questions.
> 2. **Forgetting `!` distinction:** Saying `sort(v)` modifies `v` (it does not — only `sort!(v)` does).
> 3. **Tuple single-element trap:** Writing `(42)` thinking it's a tuple — it is just `Int64`. Must use `(42,)`.
> 4. **Dict access without default:** `d["missing"]` throws `KeyError`; use `get(d, key, default)`.
> 5. **Mixing Set and Array syntax:** `s[1]` is illegal for a Set — Sets have no indexing.
> 6. **Broadcasting syntax:** Using `[1,2] + [3,4]` gives an error; must use `[1,2] .+ [3,4]`.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] Julia is a **dynamically typed**, **JIT-compiled** language targeting high-performance numerical and scientific computing.
- [x] **Installation:** Use `juliaup` (official version manager); verify with `julia --version`.
- [x] Julia uses **1-based indexing** — `v[1]` is the first element.
- [x] Variable naming uses **`snake_case`** for variables/functions, **`CamelCase`** for types/modules.
- [x] Unicode math symbols are valid identifiers (type `\delta<TAB>` in REPL).
- [x] **Default numeric types:** `Int64` for integers, `Float64` for floats (on 64-bit systems).
- [x] **`!` suffix** indicates an in-place mutating function (e.g., `push!`, `sort!`).
- [x] **Broadcasting:** prefix any operator with `.` to apply element-wise (e.g., `a .* b`, `sin.(x)`).
- [x] **Chained comparisons** are valid: `0 < x < 1` evaluates as `(0 < x) && (x < 1)`.
- [x] **Array** = mutable, ordered, indexed, homogeneous-ish → numerical workhorse.
- [x] **Tuple** = immutable, ordered, indexed, heterogeneous → records and multi-return.
- [x] **Dict** = mutable, unordered, keyed lookup, O(1) access → mappings.
- [x] **Set** = mutable, unordered, no duplicates, supports set algebra (union, intersect, setdiff).
- [x] **Strings:** single quotes for `Char`, double quotes for `String`; interpolation with `$(expr)`.
- [x] **Type inspection:** use `typeof(x)`, `isa(x, T)`, `supertype(T)`.
- [x] **Comment syntax:** `# single line` and `#= block comment =#` (nestable).
- [x] **REPL shortcuts:** `?` for help, `;` for shell mode, `]` for package mode, `Ctrl+D` to exit.
- [x] **Pkg essentials:** `] add DataFrames`, `] status`, `] rm`, `using Pkg`.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Conditionals and loops, functions, methods and constructors, modules and packages, input and output
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET861 - DATA SCIENCE WITH JULIA
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Conditionals and loops, functions, methods and constructors, modules and packages, input and output

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definitions & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Conditionals in Julia

### Formal Definition
**Conditionals** are control-flow constructs that evaluate a Boolean expression and execute different blocks of code based on whether the condition evaluates to `true` or `false`. In Julia, the primary conditional construct is the `if … elseif … else … end` compound expression, which is a standard piece of **multiple-dispatch-friendly syntax** evaluated top-down.

### Conceptual Analogy
> [!NOTE]
> Think of conditionals like a **railway track switch**. A train (the program counter) approaches a junction; depending on which lever is pulled (the boolean expression), the train follows a different track. In Julia, the `if` statement is that switch, and only **one track** is taken — the first one whose condition is `true`.

The general syntax is:

```julia
if condition_1
    # block A
elseif condition_2
    # block B
else
    # block C (default)
end
```

The `condition` must reduce to a value of type `Bool` (`true` or `false`). Unlike C/Python, Julia **does not** coerce integers to booleans implicitly — `if 1` is a `TypeError`.

---

## 1.2 Loops in Julia

### Formal Definition
**Loops** are iterative control structures that repeatedly execute a block of code. Julia supports two principal loop forms:
- `for` loop — iterates over a **range, array, or any iterable**.
- `while` loop — iterates as long as a Boolean condition remains `true`.

Loop-modification keywords are `break` (terminates the loop) and `continue` (skips to the next iteration).

### Conceptual Analogy
> [!IMPORTANT]
> A `for` loop is like a **teacher taking attendance** — she goes through the roll call list one name at a time, performing the same action (marking "Present") for each. A `while` loop is like **waiting for a kettle to boil** — you keep checking ("Is it boiling?") until the answer becomes true.

```julia
# for-loop
for i in 1:5
    println(i)
end

# while-loop
n = 1
while n <= 5
    println(n)
    n += 1
end
```

---

## 1.3 Functions in Julia

### Formal Definition
A **function** in Julia is a mapping `f: X → Y` that associates a tuple of input arguments with a return value, defined using the `function` keyword, the short-form `=` assignment, or anonymous (`->`) syntax. Functions are **first-class objects** — they can be passed as arguments, returned, and assigned to variables.

### Conceptual Analogy
> [!NOTE]
> A function is like a **vending machine**. You insert inputs (coins + selection code = arguments), the machine performs a hidden process (the body), and dispenses a result (the return value). The machine's contract never changes — different inputs may yield different outputs, but the interface is fixed.

```julia
# Long form
function area(radius)
    return π * radius^2
end

# Short form
area(r) = π * r^2

# Anonymous
area = r -> π * r^2
```

---

## 1.4 Methods and Constructors

### Formal Definition
A **method** is a specific implementation of a function for a particular type-signature (the argument types it accepts). Julia uses **multiple dispatch**: the most specific method matching the argument types is selected at runtime. A **constructor** is a special method of a `struct` (or `mutable struct`) that defines how an instance is created.

### Conceptual Analogy
> [!IMPORTANT]
> Methods are like **polymorphic job descriptions** for a single job title. "Add two things" is the *function* — but how you add two integers, two strings, or two arrays differs, so each gets its own *method*. Constructors are the **factory assembly lines** that produce finished `struct` objects according to a specific blueprint.

```julia
struct Point
    x::Float64
    y::Float64
end

# Outer constructor (default)
Point(3, 4)            # Point(3.0, 4.0)

# Inner constructor (custom)
struct PolarPoint
    r::Float64
    θ::Float64
    function PolarPoint(x::Float64, y::Float64)
        new(sqrt(x^2 + y^2), atan(y, x))
    end
end
```

---

## 1.5 Modules and Packages

### Formal Definition
A **module** in Julia is a separate namespace that encapsulates related definitions (functions, types, constants). A **package** is a reusable collection of one or more modules together with a `Project.toml` and (optionally) a `Manifest.toml` file, distributed via the built-in `Pkg` package manager and the General registry.

### Conceptual Analogy
> [!NOTE]
> A **module** is a *toolbox* — it groups related tools so they don't get mixed up. A **package** is a *boxed, labelled toolbox* you can ship to someone else, complete with a manifest listing exactly which tools (and which versions of dependencies) are inside.

```julia
module MyStats
    export mean, std
    mean(x) = sum(x)/length(x)
    std(x)  = sqrt(sum((x .- mean(x)).^2) / (length(x)-1))
end
```

---

## 1.6 Input and Output (I/O)

### Formal Definition
**I/O** in Julia is the set of operations that read from, or write to, *streams* — abstractions over files, sockets, pipes, or in-memory buffers. The `Base.print`, `Base.println`, `Base.read`, and `Base.write` functions form the core API, and the higher-level `DelimitedFiles` and `CSV` standards give tabular access.

### Conceptual Analogy
> [!IMPORTANT]
> I/O is the **mouth and ears** of a program. `print`/`println` are the mouth (the program speaks to the user or to a file). `read` is the ear (the program listens to a file or keyboard). A *stream* is the *nerve pathway* — an open channel that connects the program to the outside world.

| Stream type | Direction | Default |
|---|---|---|
| `stdin`  | Read  | Keyboard |
| `stdout` | Write | Terminal |
| `stderr` | Write | Terminal (error) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Conditionals — Structural Deep-Dive

- **Boolean Conversion Rule**: only `true` and `false` are accepted as conditions. The expression `if 1` raises a `TypeError: non-boolean (Int64) used in boolean context`.
- **Ternary Operator** `? :` is a one-liner equivalent for simple branches.
- **Short-Circuit Evaluation**: `&&` and `||` evaluate the *right* operand only when needed.
- **Compound `if` is an expression** — it returns the value of the last statement in the chosen branch.

### KTU Reference Table — Conditional Constructs

| Construct | Syntax | Returns a value? | Evaluated when condition is |
|---|---|---|---|
| `if … end` | `if cond; body; end` | Yes | `true` |
| `if … else … end` | Two branches | Yes | `true` / `false` |
| `if … elseif … else … end` | Many branches | Yes | First matching `true` |
| Ternary | `cond ? a : b` | Yes | Inline branch |
| Short-circuit `&&` | `a && b` | Yes | Both true |
| Short-circuit `\|\|` | `a \|\| b` | Yes | Either true |

---

## 2.2 Loops — Theoretical Breakdown

- The `for` loop accepts any **iterable** — ranges (`1:10`), arrays, dictionaries, strings, generators.
- The `while` loop is preferred when the number of iterations is **not known in advance**.
- `break` exits the **innermost** enclosing loop. `continue` jumps to the **next iteration** of the innermost loop.
- Nested loops are fully supported; use **descriptive variable names** to keep readability.

### KTU Reference Table — Loop Cheat Sheet

| Keyword | Effect | Use-Case |
|---|---|---|
| `for x in iter` | Iterate over iterable | Known sequence of items |
| `while cond` | Loop while `cond == true` | Unknown iteration count |
| `break` | Terminate loop | Early exit on condition |
| `continue` | Skip to next iteration | Filter inside loop |
| `enumerate(iter)` | Pair `index, value` | Need both index & value |
| `zip(a, b)` | Iterate in parallel | Multiple sequences |

---

## 2.3 Functions — Argument Semantics

- **Positional arguments** are passed in order.
- **Keyword arguments** use the `;` separator and must be passed by name.
- **Default arguments** are evaluated **once**, at function-definition time (in the *outer* scope).
- **Varargs** (`...`) collect a variable number of positional arguments into a tuple.
- **Return behavior**: the last expression is returned automatically; `return` is optional but recommended for clarity.
- **Operators are functions**: `+(1, 2)` is identical to `1 + 2`.

### KTU Reference Table — Function Anatomy

| Element | Example | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Positional arg | `f(x, y)` | Order matters |
| Default arg | `f(x, y=10)` | Used if not supplied |
| Keyword arg | `f(x; tol=1e-6)` | Caller must name it |
| Varargs | `f(args...)` | Tuple of extra args |
| Splatting | `f(collection...)` | Unpacks collection |
| Anonymous | `x -> x^2` | One-shot lambda |
| Do-block | `f(x) do; …; end` | Anonymous 1st arg |

---

## 2.4 Methods & Constructors — Multiple Dispatch Theory

- **Dispatch** is the process of choosing which method of a function to invoke.
- Julia uses **dynamic multiple dispatch** on the types of **all positional arguments**.
- A `struct` automatically gets two **default constructors**:
  1. The implicit inner constructor that accepts all fields in order.
  2. An outer constructor generated by `struct` syntax: `Type(field1, field2, …)`.

### Constructor Variants

| Type | Defined Where | When Used |
|---|---|---|
| Default inner | Auto-generated by `struct` | All field types match |
| User-defined inner | Inside `struct` block | Custom invariants |
| Outer constructor | Outside `struct` block | Convenience overloads |
| Parametric | `struct T{T<:Number}; …; end` | Type-parameterized data |

---

## 2.5 Modules & Packages — The Ecosystem

- **`module Name … end`** creates a new namespace.
- **`using ModuleName`** brings the module's *exported* names into the current scope.
- **`import ModuleName`** keeps names qualified (`ModuleName.f`).
- **`export :a, :b`** lists the names visible to a `using` statement.
- **`include("file.jl")`** evaluates the file in the current module.
- A **package** = a git repo containing `Project.toml` (dependencies) and a top-level module.

### KTU Reference Table — Module Commands

| Command | Effect |
|---|---|
| `using M` | Imports exported names |
| `import M` | Module name required to qualify |
| `import M: f, g` | Specific names from M |
| `export :a, :b` | Make names visible to `using` |
| `include("file.jl")` | Evaluate file in module |
| `Pkg.add("CSV")` | Install package |
| `Pkg.activate(".")` | Activate local environment |
| `Pkg.status()` | List installed packages |

---

## 2.6 Input/Output — Stream Theory

- `print(io, args...)` writes without newline.
- `println(io, args...)` writes with a trailing `\n`.
- `@printf` (from `Printf`) supports C-style formatted output.
- `read(filename, String)` slurps the entire file.
- `open(filename, "w") do io; …; end` is the idiomatic safe form (auto-closes).
- `readlines(filename)` returns a `Vector{String}`.
- `readdlm(filename, ',')` reads delimited numerical data.

### KTU Reference Table — I/O Modes

| Mode String | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `"r"` | Read (default) |
| `"w"` | Write (truncate) |
| `"a"` | Append |
| `"r+"` | Read & write, no truncate |
| `"w+"` | Read & write, truncate |

### Real-World Engineering Utility
> [!NOTE]
> These six building blocks — **conditionals, loops, functions, methods, modules, and I/O** — are the foundation of *every* data-science pipeline. ETL scripts loop over files, dispatch on column types, log progress with `println`, and read/write CSVs. Mastering them is the prerequisite for DataFrames.jl, MLJ.jl, and Flux.jl.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthroughs

## 3.1 Walkthrough — Nested Conditionals & Loops (Trace)

**Problem.** Determine the output of the following Julia program.

```julia
function classify(n)
    if n < 0
        return "Negative"
    elseif n == 0
        return "Zero"
    else
        if n % 2 == 0
            return "Positive Even"
        else
            return "Positive Odd"
        end
    end
end

for i in [-1, 0, 1, 2, 3]
    println(classify(i))
end
```

### Step 1 — Identify the call sequence
The `for` loop iterates over the array `[-1, 0, 1, 2, 3]`. Five iterations, one call to `classify` per element.

### Step 2 — Trace `classify(-1)`
- Condition `n < 0` → `-1 < 0` → `true` → return `"Negative"`.

### Step 3 — Trace `classify(0)`
- `n < 0` → `false`.
- `n == 0` → `0 == 0` → `true` → return `"Zero"`.

### Step 4 — Trace `classify(1)`
- `n < 0` → `false`.
- `n == 0` → `false`.
- Inner `n % 2 == 0` → `1 % 2 == 1` → `false` → return `"Positive Odd"`.

### Step 5 — Trace `classify(2)`
- Falls to inner branch: `2 % 2 == 0` → `true` → return `"Positive Even"`.

### Step 6 — Trace `classify(3)`
- Inner branch: `3 % 2 == 0` → `false` → return `"Positive Odd"`.

### Final Output
```
Negative
Zero
Positive Odd
Positive Even
Positive Odd
```

---

## 3.2 Walkthrough — Function with Multiple Methods (Multiple Dispatch)

**Problem.** Predict the output.

```julia
foo(x::Int)    = "Int: $x"
foo(x::Float64)= "Float: $x"
foo(x::String) = "String: $x"
foo(x::Any)    = "Generic: $x"

println(foo(5))
println(foo(3.14))
println(foo("Julia"))
println(foo([1, 2, 3]))
```

### Step 1 — Method Table Compilation
When Julia parses these definitions, it builds a *method table* for `foo`:

| Method | Argument Type | Specificity |
|---|---|---|
| `foo(x::Int)` | `Int64` | Most specific |
| `foo(x::Float64)` | `Float64` | Most specific |
| `foo(x::String)` | `String` | Most specific |
| `foo(x::Any)` | `Any` | Catch-all (least specific) |

### Step 2 — Resolution of `foo(5)`
- `5` is `Int64`. The most specific matching method is `foo(x::Int)`. Output: `"Int: 5"`.

### Step 3 — Resolution of `foo(3.14)`
- `3.14` is `Float64`. Selects `foo(x::Float64)`. Output: `"Float: 3.14"`.

### Step 4 — Resolution of `foo("Julia")`
- Argument is `String`. Selects `foo(x::String)`. Output: `"String: Julia"`.

### Step 5 — Resolution of `foo([1, 2, 3])`
- Argument is `Vector{Int64}`, which is **not** `Int`, `Float64`, or `String`. Falls through to the catch-all `foo(x::Any)`. Output: `"Generic: [1, 2, 3]"`.

### Final Output
```
Int: 5
Float: 3.14
String: Julia
Generic: [1, 2, 3]
```

---

## 3.3 Walkthrough — Custom Constructor with Validation

**Problem.** Build a `Temperature` struct that stores values in Kelvin but accepts Celsius input and rejects sub-absolute-zero readings.

```julia
struct Temperature
    K::Float64
    function Temperature(c::Float64)
        k = c + 273.15
        k >= 0 || throw(ArgumentError("Below absolute zero!"))
        new(k)
    end
end

t1 = Temperature(25.0)        # OK
println(t1.K)

t2 = Temperature(-300.0)      # Throws
```

### Step 1 — Invariant definition
We need `K ≥ 0` (absolute zero in Kelvin).

### Step 2 — Convert Celsius to Kelvin
$$
K = C + 273.15
$$

### Step 3 — Validate with short-circuit OR
`k >= 0 || throw(ArgumentError("Below absolute zero!"))` — the right-hand `throw` runs only when `k >= 0` is `false`, guaranteeing the invariant.

### Step 4 — `new(k)` creates the struct
The inner constructor calls the special `new` function which builds the immutable instance.

### Step 5 — Trace
- `Temperature(25.0)` → `k = 25.0 + 273.15 = 298.15` → `k >= 0` is `true` → `new(298.15)` is created. `t1.K` prints `298.15`.
- `Temperature(-300.0)` → `k = -26.85` → `k >= 0` is `false` → `throw` raises `ArgumentError`.

### Final Output
```
298.15
ERROR: ArgumentError: Below absolute zero!
```

---

## 3.4 Walkthrough — Module + `using` Chain

**Problem.** Demonstrate how to organize two modules and import names from one to the other.

```julia
# File: Geometry.jl
module Geometry
    export area_circle, area_square
    area_circle(r) = π * r^2
    area_square(s) = s^2
end

# File: Main.jl
include("Geometry.jl")
using .Geometry
println(area_circle(2))   # 12.566370…
println(area_square(3))   # 9
```

### Step 1 — Module `Geometry` is defined
- `area_circle` and `area_square` are added to the module's namespace.
- `export` registers them as publicly accessible names.

### Step 2 — `include("Geometry.jl")` evaluates the file
The module `Geometry` is created in the current namespace. Note the leading `.` in `using .Geometry` — the dot says *"the module I just defined in this file"*.

### Step 3 — `using .Geometry` imports the exported names
After this line, `area_circle` and `area_square` are available as bare names in `Main`.

### Step 4 — Computation
- `area_circle(2) = π × 2² = 4π ≈ 12.566370`.
- `area_square(3) = 9`.

### Final Output
```
12.566370614359172
9
```

---

## 3.5 Walkthrough — Reading a File Line-by-Line

**Problem.** Count how many lines of a file start with the letter `#` (comment lines).

```julia
count = 0
open("data.txt", "r") do io
    for line in eachline(io)
        if startswith(line, "#")
            count += 1
        end
    end
end
println("Comment lines: $count")
```

### Step 1 — Open the file
`open("data.txt", "r") do io … end` opens the file in read mode and binds the resulting stream to `io`. The do-block ensures the file is closed even if an error is thrown.

### Step 2 — Iterate with `eachline`
`eachline(io)` is a memory-efficient generator that returns one line at a time — no need to load the whole file.

### Step 3 — Test each line
`startswith(line, "#")` returns `true` only when the line's first character is `#`.

### Step 4 — Increment counter
`count += 1` is shorthand for `count = count + 1`. The variable `count` is captured by the do-block via Julia's closure semantics.

### Step 5 — Print
After the do-block ends, the file is automatically closed, and we print the total.

This pattern is the idiomatic Julia replacement for Python's `with open(...) as f:` block.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Control-Flow of `if … elseif … else … end`

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start]) --> B[Evaluate condition 1]
    B -->|true| C[Execute Branch 1]
    B -->|false| D[Evaluate condition 2]
    D -->|true| E[Execute Branch 2]
    D -->|false| F[Execute Else branch]
    C --> G([Continue program])
    E --> G
    F --> G
```

## 4.2 Loop Flow with `break` and `continue`

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Loop Start]) --> B[Get next item from iterable]
    B --> C{Item available?}
    C -->|no| D([Exit loop])
    C -->|yes| E{continue triggered?}
    E -->|yes| B
    E -->|no| F{break triggered?}
    F -->|yes| D
    F -->|no| G[Execute loop body]
    G --> B
```

## 4.3 Function Call Stack — Positional, Keyword, and Default Arguments

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Caller([Caller]) --> A[Function entry]
    A --> B[Bind positional args in order]
    B --> C[Bind keyword args by name]
    C --> D{Fill missing from defaults}
    D --> E[Unpack varargs into tuple]
    E --> F[Execute function body]
    F --> G[Last expression returned]
    G --> Caller
```

## 4.4 Multiple Dispatch Method Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Call([Call foo arg]) --> Q{arg is Int64?}
    Q -->|yes| M1[Method 1: Int]
    Q -->|no| R{arg is Float64?}
    R -->|yes| M2[Method 2: Float64]
    R -->|no| S{arg is String?}
    S -->|yes| M3[Method 3: String]
    S -->|no| M4[Method 4: Any fallback]
    M1 --> Out([Return value])
    M2 --> Out
    M3 --> Out
    M4 --> Out
```

## 4.5 Struct Constructor Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Call([Temperature celsius]) --> V1[Convert C to K]
    V1 --> V2{K greater or equal 0?}
    V2 -->|no| ERR[Throw ArgumentError]
    V2 -->|yes| NEW[new K - allocate struct]
    NEW --> RET([Return Temperature instance])
    ERR --> STOP([Program halted])
```

## 4.6 Module / Package Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Pkg[Package Project toml] --> Mod1[Module Geometry]
    Pkg --> Mod2[Module Algebra]
    Mod1 --> F1[area circle]
    Mod1 --> F2[area square]
    Mod2 --> G1[dot product]
    Mod2 --> G2[cross product]
    Mod1 -->|export| Scope[Public namespace]
    Mod2 -->|export| Scope
    Scope -->|using| User([User script])
```

## 4.7 Stream-Based I/O Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Keyboard --> Stdin
    Stdin -->|read| Prog[Program]
    Prog -->|print| Stdout
    Prog -->|print| Stderr
    File1[File data txt] -->|read / write| Prog
    File2[File out csv] -->|read / write| Prog
    Net[Network socket] -->|read / write| Prog
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between `break` and `continue` in Julia loops with an example.**

**Model Answer:**
- `break` **terminates** the entire loop immediately, transferring control to the statement after the loop.
- `continue` **skips the remainder of the current iteration** and proceeds to the next iteration of the loop.

```julia
for i in 1:5
    if i == 3
        break          # stops at i=3
    end
    println(i)
end
# Output: 1 2

for i in 1:5
    if i == 3
        continue       # skips i=3 only
    end
    println(i)
end
# Output: 1 2 4 5
```

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is multiple dispatch? How does it differ from method overloading in C++?**

**Model Answer:**
**Multiple dispatch** is a feature in which the method to invoke is selected at runtime based on the **types of *all* positional arguments**, not just the receiver. Julia supports it natively via the method table. In contrast, **C++ method overloading** is resolved at *compile time* and only considers the receiver's static type, plus the static types of arguments — it cannot dispatch on the dynamic type of the receiver or on combinations of argument types at runtime. Julia's dispatch is *dynamic* and *symmetric* across arguments.

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module-Internal Choice Pattern)

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the various conditional constructs available in Julia with suitable examples. Demonstrate the use of the ternary operator and short-circuit evaluation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write a Julia program that reads integers from standard input until the user enters `0`, stores the entered numbers in a vector, and computes (i) the sum, (ii) the mean, (iii) the maximum. Use a `while` loop and conditional logic.

---

#### Model Solution to (a)

The conditional constructs in Julia are:

1. **`if … end`** — single-branch decision.
2. **`if … else … end`** — two-branch decision.
3. **`if … elseif … else … end`** — multi-branch decision.
4. **Ternary operator `cond ? a : b`** — inline one-liner.
5. **Short-circuit `&&` and `||`** — Boolean logic operators that return values, not just booleans.

**Example — All four patterns:**

```julia
x = 7

# 1. if-else
if x > 0
    println("Positive")
else
    println("Non-positive")
end

# 2. if-elseif-else
if x % 3 == 0
    println("Divisible by 3")
elseif x % 3 == 1
    println("Remainder 1")
else
    println("Remainder 2")
end

# 3. Ternary
sign = x >= 0 ? "non-negative" : "negative"
println(sign)

# 4. Short-circuit
(x > 0) && println("x is positive")
(x == 0) || println("x is non-zero")
```

**Key features:**
- Conditions must be of type `Bool`. Julia does not coerce integers.
- `if … end` is an **expression** that returns a value:
  ```julia
  result = if x > 0 "yes" else "no" end
  ```
- Short-circuit operators return the **deciding operand**, not always `true`/`false`:
  ```julia
  julia> 0 || 42        # 42
  julia> false && 7     # false
  ```

---

#### Model Solution to (b)

```julia
function summarize()
    nums = Int[]
    println("Enter integers (0 to stop):")
    while true
        n = parse(Int, readline())
        n == 0 && break
        push!(nums, n)
    end

    isempty(nums) && return "No data entered."

    s  = sum(nums)
    μ  = s / length(nums)
    mx = maximum(nums)

    println("Sum = $s")
    println("Mean = $μ")
    println("Max = $mx")
end

summarize()
```

**Explanation of steps:**
- `Int[]` creates an empty vector of integers.
- `readline()` reads a single line from `stdin` as a `String`.
- `parse(Int, …)` converts it to `Int`. (A `try/catch` can guard invalid input.)
- `n == 0 && break` uses **short-circuit evaluation** to exit the infinite `while` loop when the user types `0`.
- `push!(nums, n)` appends to the vector (the `!` is Julia's *in-place* mutation convention).
- `sum`, `mean` (computed manually), and `maximum` are all Base-library functions that operate on any iterable of numbers.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the concept of multiple dispatch in Julia. Define a `Shape` hierarchy using abstract types and write methods for an `area` function that dispatches on concrete shape types.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain modules and packages in Julia. Create a custom module `StatsTools` exporting `mean` and `median`, and demonstrate importing it into a separate script.

---

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Concept of Multiple Dispatch:** Julia's central design idea. When a function `f` is called, Julia inspects the types of **all positional arguments** at runtime and selects the **most specific method** from a global method table. This is fundamentally different from:
- Single dispatch (OOP, e.g., C++ virtual functions), which uses only the receiver's type.
- Compile-time overloading (C++), which uses static types.

**Code — Abstract type + multiple methods:**

```julia
abstract type Shape end

struct Circle    <: Shape; r::Float64; end
struct Rectangle <: Shape; w::Float64; h::Float64; end
struct Triangle  <: Shape; b::Float64; h::Float64; end

# Methods of the same function `area`, dispatched on the concrete type
area(c::Circle)    = π * c.r^2
area(r::Rectangle) = r.w * r.h
area(t::Triangle)  = 0.5 * t.b * t.h
```

**Usage:**
```julia
shapes = Shape[Circle(2.0), Rectangle(3.0, 4.0), Triangle(5.0, 6.0)]
total = sum(area.(s) for s in shapes)   # broadcast + generator
println("Total area = $total")
```

When `area(s)` is called inside the generator, Julia's dispatcher:
- For `s::Circle` → invokes `area(c::Circle)`.
- For `s::Rectangle` → invokes `area(r::Rectangle)`.
- For `s::Triangle` → invokes `area(t::Triangle)`.

The `Shape[]` vector holds a heterogeneous collection, but each element still carries its concrete type, so dispatch works correctly.

**Output:**
```
Total area = 33.566370614359172
```

---

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Modules vs Packages:**
- A **module** is a Julia namespace that groups related definitions. Created with `module … end`.
- A **package** is a *reusable* unit of Julia code: a directory containing a `Project.toml` (and usually a `src/<PackageName>.jl` with the top-level module), distributed through the General registry.

**Module — `StatsTools.jl`:**
```julia
module StatsTools

export mean, median

mean(x::AbstractArray) = sum(x) / length(x)

function median(x::AbstractArray)
    s = sort(vec(x))
    n = length(s)
    return n % 2 == 1 ? s[(n+1)÷2] : (s[n÷2] + s[n÷2 + 1]) / 2
end

end # module
```

**Main script — `analysis.jl`:**
```julia
include("StatsTools.jl")
using .StatsTools

data = [4.0, 1.0, 7.0, 3.0, 9.0]
println("Mean   = ", mean(data))
println("Median = ", median(data))
```

**Explanation:**
- `export mean, median` registers the two functions as public symbols of the module.
- `using .StatsTools` (note the **leading dot**!) imports *this file's* module — equivalent to a relative import in Python.
- If the module were installed as a registered package, the line would simply be `using StatsTools` (no dot) after `Pkg.add("StatsTools")`.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Module Imports**
> Students frequently lose 2–3 marks by:
> 1. **Forgetting the leading dot** in `using .MyModule` when the module is defined in the same file.
> 2. Trying to use an exported name *without* calling `using` or `import`.
> 3. Mistaking `import` (qualified access) for `using` (unqualified access). The two are NOT interchangeable.
> 4. Writing `export mean` *after* defining `mean` — order does not actually matter inside the module, but students sometimes place `export` outside the module block by mistake.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Conditionals** in Julia require `Bool` conditions — no integer coercion.
- ✅ The `if … end` block is an **expression** that returns a value.
- ✅ `for` loops iterate over **any iterable**; `while` loops are for unknown iteration counts.
- ✅ `break` exits the loop; `continue` skips the current iteration.
- ✅ `function … end`, the short `=` form, and `->` are all valid ways to define functions.
- ✅ Functions can have **positional, keyword, default, and varargs** arguments — splat with `...`.
- ✅ Julia uses **multiple dispatch** based on the types of *all* positional arguments.
- ✅ A `struct` gets **default constructors** automatically; custom constructors use `new()`.
- ✅ **`module` creates a namespace**; **`using` vs `import`** differ in name resolution.
- ✅ **`export`** lists which names are visible to `using` statements.
- ✅ A **package** is a `Project.toml` + top-level module; managed with the `Pkg` stdlib.
- ✅ **`open(fn, mode) do io … end`** is the safe, idiomatic file-IO pattern.
- ✅ `print` vs `println` — only difference is the trailing newline.
- ✅ `readlines`, `eachline`, `readdlm` cover most tabular / line-based I/O needs.
- ✅ Forgetting the **dot** in `using .LocalModule` is the most common module mistake.
- ✅ Use **`abstract type`** to define shared supertypes for method dispatch hierarchies.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Object oriented programming and functional programming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET861 - DATA SCIENCE WITH JULIA
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Object oriented programming and functional programming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) in Julia

### Formal KTU Definition
**Object-Oriented Programming (OOP)** is a programming paradigm centered on the concept of *objects* — data structures that bundle together **fields (state/attributes)** and **methods (behaviour)** — and the four classical pillars: **Encapsulation**, **Abstraction**, **Inheritance**, and **Polymorphism**.

In Julia, the OOP model is unconventional: Julia is **not** a class-based OOP language. Instead, it implements an OOP-like model through:
- **Abstract types** (for hierarchy and abstraction)
- **Concrete composite types / structs** (for state / fields)
- **Functions and Methods** (for behaviour, attached to types via **multiple dispatch**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Insight:** Julia replaces the traditional "class owns the method" model with the "**function + multiple dispatch**" model. Multiple dispatch is the *Julia equivalent* of polymorphism — it selects the method to execute based on the types of **all** arguments, not just the receiver.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Restaurant Order" Model
Imagine a traditional class-based OOP kitchen as a **specialist chef** who only knows how to cook one cuisine. You tell *the chef* what to do ("chef, cook pasta"). In Julia's kitchen, the philosophy is different: you place an **order ticket** describing the *ingredients* (argument types), and the **dispatcher** (Julia's runtime) routes the order to the correct specialist cook. The dish is selected purely by what is on the ticket, not by which chef is "called first". This routing by argument-type is **multiple dispatch**.

### 1.1.1 The Four OOP Pillars Mapped to Julia

| Pillar | Classical OOP (Java/C++) | Julia Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| **Encapsulation** | `private` fields, getters/setters | `struct` with internal fields; composition |
| **Abstraction** | Abstract classes / interfaces | **Abstract types** (`abstract type`) |
| **Inheritance** | `extends`, `implements` | **Type hierarchy** via `<:` (subtyping) |
| **Polymorphism** | Method overriding, virtual methods | **Multiple dispatch** via method definitions |

> [!NOTE]
> Julia deliberately omits traditional class-based inheritance. The language designers argue this yields more composable, performant code. KTU examiners frequently test this conceptual difference.

---

## 1.2 Functional Programming (FP) in Julia

### Formal KTU Definition
**Functional Programming (FP)** is a programming paradigm where computation is treated as the **evaluation of mathematical functions**, avoiding mutable state and side effects. Its core tenets are:

1. **First-class and higher-order functions** — functions can be passed as arguments and returned as values.
2. **Pure functions** — output depends only on input; no side effects.
3. **Immutability** — data structures are not modified in place.
4. **Function composition** — small functions combine to form larger ones.
5. **Expressions over statements** — every construct returns a value.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Assembly Line of Mathematics"
Think of FP as a **factory assembly line** where each station takes in raw material (input), performs *one* pure transformation, and passes the result to the next station. There are no warehouses where you stash half-finished products and mutate them — every station is a *pure mathematical function*. If you give the line the same input, you always get the same product, regardless of the time of day or who is operating the machine.

> [!NOTE]
> Julia is a **multi-paradigm** language. It is *not* purely functional (mutable state exists, e.g., `Vector{T}`), but it provides *first-class* functional tools. This hybrid nature is a frequently-tested KTU point.

### 1.2.1 Why OOP and FP Together in Data Science?

| Paradigm | Data Science Use Case |
|---|---|
| **OOP (structs + dispatch)** | Model a `DataFrame`, `LinearModel`, or `NeuralNetwork` with structured fields and custom methods. |
| **FP (map/filter/reduce)** | Vectorized, parallelizable transformations on massive datasets (ETL pipelines). |
| **Multiple Dispatch** | Specialise algorithms on numeric types (`Float64`, `Float32`, `BigFloat`) without code duplication. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In data science with Julia, FP is used for **data transformation pipelines** (`map`, `filter`, broadcasting), while the type/struct system provides **structured data containers**. Mastering both is mandatory for the End-Semester Evaluation (ESE).

---

## 1.3 Quick Vocabulary Anchor (Mandatory KTU Glossary)

- **Type** — A classification of values that dictates legal operations (`Int64`, `Float64`, `String`).
- **Abstract Type** — A node in the type hierarchy; cannot be instantiated (`abstract type Shape end`).
- **Composite Type / Struct** — A user-defined concrete type bundling fields (`struct Point ... end`).
- **Method** — A specific implementation of a function for a particular type signature.
- **Multiple Dispatch** — Selection of a method based on the types of *all* positional arguments.
- **Closure** — A function that captures variables from its enclosing lexical scope.
- **Anonymous Function** — A function with no name, written inline (`x -> x^2`).
- **Higher-Order Function** — A function that accepts or returns another function.
- **Broadcasting** — Applying an operation element-wise across arrays via the dot syntax (`f.(x)`).
- **Piping Operator** — The `|>` operator that chains function calls left-to-right.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference

## 2.1 The Julia Type System (Type Hierarchy)

Julia's type system is the foundation of OOP-style programming in the language. It is **nominal** (types have names) and **partially ordered** (form a lattice via `<:`).

```
        Any
         |
    +----+----+
    |         |
 Number    AbstractString
    |         |
    ...      ...
```

- `Any` is the **supertype** of every type.
- Concrete types have no subtypes; abstract types cannot be instantiated.
- A type `T2` is a *subtype* of `T1` if every value of `T2` is also a value of `T1`, written `T2 <: T1`.

### 2.1.1 The Subtype Operator `<:`

$$ T_{\text{child}} \prec T_{\text{parent}} \iff T_{\text{child}} <: T_{\text{parent}} $$

This reads as "**$T_{\text{child}}$ is a subtype of $T_{\text{parent}}$**". The `<:` operator is fundamental to defining the type lattice.

### 2.1.2 Struct Definition Syntax

```julia
struct Point
    x::Float64
    y::Float64
end
```

A `struct` is *immutable by default* in Julia — you cannot reassign `p.x = 5` after construction. To allow mutation, use `mutable struct Point ... end`.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Trap:** Students often forget that a default `struct` is immutable. Marks are deducted for trying to mutate a non-`mutable struct` field.

---

## 2.2 Methods and Multiple Dispatch — The Heart of Julia OOP

A **function** in Julia is a name; a **method** is a concrete implementation tied to a type signature.

### 2.2.1 Method Definition

```julia
area(p::Point) = p.x * p.y          # method 1: specific
area(s::Shape) = ...                 # method 2: general (abstract)
```

When `area(p)` is called, Julia inspects the types of *all* arguments and selects the **most specific** applicable method.

### 2.2.2 The Multiple Dispatch Decision Rule

For a call `f(x1, x2, ..., xn)` with argument types `(S1, S2, ..., Sn)`:

$$ \text{method chosen} = \arg\max_{m \in M} \; \text{specificity}(m.\text{sig}, (S_1, S_2, \dots, S_n)) $$

where $M$ is the set of all methods of $f$ whose signature is a *supertype* match for the argument tuple, and specificity is determined by the type lattice.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **single most testable KTU concept** in Module 2. Memorize: dispatch uses **all** arguments, not just the first (unlike C++/Java).

### 2.2.3 Method Ambiguities

If two methods are equally specific, Julia raises a `MethodError: ambiguous`. Resolve with an explicit method:

```julia
f(x::Number, y::Number) = "numbers"   # tie-breaker
```

---

## 2.3 Functional Programming Constructs — High-Yield Reference Table

| Construct | Syntax | Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Anonymous function** | `x -> x^2` | Inline function with no name |
| **Named function** | `f(x) = x^2` | Standard function definition |
| **Higher-order** | `map(f, collection)` | Function taking/returning a function |
| **`map`** | `map(f, xs)` | Apply `f` to each element of `xs` |
| **`filter`** | `filter(p, xs)` | Keep elements where `p(x) == true` |
| **`reduce`** | `reduce(op, xs)` | Fold `op` cumulatively over `xs` |
| **Broadcasting** | `f.(xs)` | Element-wise application (dot syntax) |
| **Pipe operator** | `x \|> f` | Equivalent to `f(x)` |
| **Composition** | `f ∘ g` | Apply `g` first, then `f` |
| **Closure** | `x -> y -> x + y` | Function capturing outer-scope variable |
| **`do` block** | `map(xs) do x; x^2; end` | Inline lambda for HOFs |

### 2.3.1 Map, Filter, Reduce — Mathematical Form

For a collection $X = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)$ and a function $f: A \to B$:

$$ \text{map}(f, X) = (f(x_1), f(x_2), \dots, f(x_n)) $$

$$ \text{filter}(p, X) = (x_i \in X \mid p(x_i) = \text{true}) $$

$$ \text{reduce}(op, X) = op(op(\dots op(x_1, x_2)\dots), x_n) \quad (\text{left-fold}) $$

where $p$ is a **predicate** (a function returning `Bool`).

### 2.3.2 Purity and Side Effects

A function $f$ is **pure** iff for all inputs $x$:

$$ f(x) = f(x) \quad \text{(referential transparency)} $$

i.e., output depends only on input, and evaluating $f$ produces **no observable side effect** (no I/O, no mutation of global state, no printing).

> [!WARNING]
> KTU examiners often ask: *"Is `sort!()` a pure function?"* Answer: **No**, because the `!` denotes in-place mutation. A pure function does not mutate its arguments.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Multiple dispatch** powers Julia's entire scientific ecosystem (DifferentialEquations.jl, Plots.jl). Algorithms are written generically and specialised for GPU/CPU/mixed precision without code duplication.
- **Functional pipelines** (`map`/`filter`/`reduce`) align with **Spark/MapReduce** data-parallel paradigms used in big-data engineering.
- **Struct-based OOP** lets data scientists build **domain models** (e.g., a `RegressionModel` struct containing weights, bias, and predict/train methods).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Building an OOP-Style Hierarchy in Julia

### Problem
Model a 2D shape system with `Circle` and `Rectangle` types, both subtypes of abstract `Shape`. Provide an `area` function that works polymorphically.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Declare the abstract supertype**

```julia
abstract type Shape end
```

This declares `Shape` as an abstract node in the type lattice. It cannot be instantiated; its sole purpose is to act as a parent for related concrete types.

**Step 2 — Define the concrete subtypes**

```julia
struct Circle <: Shape
    radius::Float64
end

struct Rectangle <: Shape
    width::Float64
    height::Float64
end
```

Note the `<: Shape` syntax — every `Circle` and `Rectangle` is automatically a `Shape` via subtyping.

**Step 3 — Define methods via multiple dispatch**

```julia
area(c::Circle)    = π * c.radius^2
area(r::Rectangle) = r.width * r.height
```

**Step 4 — Invoke polymorphism**

```julia
shapes = [Circle(2.0), Rectangle(3.0, 4.0)]
total  = sum(area(s) for s in shapes)
```

Julia's `sum` calls `area(s)` once per element. The dispatch system routes each call to the correct method based on the runtime type of `s`.

### Mathematical Expansion

For the `Circle(2.0)` call:

$$ \text{area}(c) = \pi \cdot (2.0)^2 = 4\pi \approx 12.566 $$

For the `Rectangle(3.0, 4.0)` call:

$$ \text{area}(r) = 3.0 \times 4.0 = 12.0 $$

Total:

$$ \text{total} = 4\pi + 12.0 \approx 24.566 $$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Functional Pipeline on a Dataset

### Problem
Given a list of sensor readings `[12, -5, 7, -3, 18, 0, 9]`, use a pure FP pipeline to:
1. Remove negative values (`filter`).
2. Square each remaining value (`map` / broadcast).
3. Compute the sum (`reduce`).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Filter negatives**

```julia
data = [12, -5, 7, -3, 18, 0, 9]
pos  = filter(x -> x >= 0, data)
# pos == [12, 7, 18, 0, 9]
```

Mathematically:

$$ \text{pos} = \{ x_i \in \text{data} \mid x_i \geq 0 \} = [12, 7, 18, 0, 9] $$

**Step 2 — Square each element via broadcasting**

```julia
sq = pos .^ 2
# sq == [144, 49, 324, 0, 81]
```

Broadcasting applies `^2` element-wise:

$$ \text{sq}_i = (\text{pos}_i)^2 $$

**Step 3 — Sum via reduce**

```julia
total = reduce(+, sq)
# total == 598
```

Mathematically:

$$ \text{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{5} \text{sq}_i = 144 + 49 + 324 + 0 + 81 = 598 $$

**Bonus — One-line composition using the pipe operator**

```julia
total = data |> x -> filter(y -> y >= 0, x) .|> y -> y^2 |> sum
# or, equivalently:
total = data |> x -> filter(>(0), x) .|> abs2 |> sum
```

Each `|>` passes the LHS as the *first* argument to the RHS. This is left-to-right composition, which KTU examiners often contrast with mathematical right-to-left composition $f \circ g$.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Closure Construction

### Problem
Build a function `make_counter()` that returns a closure which increments an internal counter on every call.

### Step-by-Step Solution

```julia
function make_counter()
    n = 0
    return () -> (n += 1; n)
end

c1 = make_counter()
c1()   # 1
c1()   # 2
c1()   # 3

c2 = make_counter()
c2()   # 1   (independent counter)
```

**Lexical scoping rule:** The anonymous function `() -> (n += 1; n)` captures the binding `n` from `make_counter`'s scope. Each call to `make_counter()` creates a *new* `n` binding, so `c1` and `c2` are independent.

$$ \text{state of } c_k = n_k \quad \text{where } n_k \text{ is unique to closure } k $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Trap:** Students often confuse closures with `mutable struct`. A closure's *captured variable* is mutable in the sense that the closure can update it, but the variable is still scoped to the closure's environment, not exposed as a public field.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4: Multiple Dispatch Resolution (Trace)

### Problem
Given the following methods, predict the output of `f(2, 3.0)` and `f(2.0, 3)`.

```julia
f(x::Int, y::Int)    = "Int, Int"
f(x::Number, y::Any) = "Number, Any"
f(x::Any, y::Number) = "Any, Number"
```

### Step-by-Step Trace

**Call 1: `f(2, 3.0)`**
- Argument types: `(Int64, Float64)`.
- Candidate methods:
  1. `f(::Int, ::Int)` — second arg does not match (Float64 ≠ Int). **Excluded.**
  2. `f(::Number, ::Any)` — `Int64 <: Number` ✓, `Float64 <: Any` ✓. **Applicable.**
  3. `f(::Any, ::Number)` — `Float64 <: Number` ✓, but this method is **equally specific** to method 2 in a different position. **Ambiguity!**

**Result:** Julia raises a `MethodError: f(::Int64, ::Float64) is ambiguous`.

> [!NOTE]
> This is a classic KTU question. The fix is to add a tie-breaker method `f(x::Number, y::Number) = "Number, Number"`.

**Resolution after fix:**
- `f(2, 3.0)` → `"Number, Number"` (most specific).
- `f(2.0, 3)` → `"Number, Number"` (same).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Table — OOP vs FP in Julia

| Dimension | OOP Style (struct + dispatch) | FP Style (map/filter/reduce) |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary unit** | Type / struct | Function |
| **State** | Encapsulated in fields (mutable if needed) | Avoided; immutable data preferred |
| **Extension** | Add new type or new method | Compose new functions |
| **Parallelism** | Naturally safe for immutable structs | Trivially parallel (map, reduce) |
| **Debuggability** | State-tracing can be complex | Pure functions are referentially transparent |
| **Data Science fit** | Model representation | ETL / transformation pipelines |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Julia Type Hierarchy (OOP Foundation)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeAny["Any"]
    nodeNumber["Number"]
    nodeReal["Real"]
    nodeInt["Int64"]
    nodeFloat["Float64"]
    nodeAbstract["AbstractType Shape"]
    nodeCircle["Struct Circle"]
    nodeRect["Struct Rectangle"]
    nodeMethod1["Method area Circle"]
    nodeMethod2["Method area Rectangle"]

    nodeAny --> nodeNumber
    nodeAny --> nodeAbstract
    nodeNumber --> nodeReal
    nodeReal --> nodeInt
    nodeReal --> nodeFloat
    nodeAbstract --> nodeCircle
    nodeAbstract --> nodeRect
    nodeMethod1 -. dispatches on .-> nodeCircle
    nodeMethod2 -. dispatches on .-> nodeRect
```

**Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows denote `<:` (subtype) relationships. Dashed arrows denote dispatch (a method is selected for a type). Note that `Any` is the universal supertype.

---

## 4.2 Multiple Dispatch Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Function call f x1 x2 xn] --> B[Collect argument types S1 S2 Sn]
    B --> C[Find all methods whose signature is supertype of S tuple]
    C --> D{Multiple applicable methods?}
    D -- No --> E[Invoke the single method]
    D -- Yes --> F[Compute specificity score per method]
    F --> G{Unique most specific?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Invoke most specific method]
    G -- No --> I[Raise MethodError Ambiguous]
```

---

## 4.3 Functional Pipeline Architecture (ETL Pattern)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeRaw["Raw Data X"] --> nodeFilter["filter predicate p"]
    nodeFilter --> nodeClean["Cleaned Data Y"]
    nodeClean --> nodeMap["map transform f"]
    nodeMap --> nodeTrans["Transformed Data Z"]
    nodeTrans --> nodeReduce["reduce op plus"]
    nodeReduce --> nodeOut["Aggregate Result R"]
```

**Reading the diagram:** This is a left-to-right data pipeline. Each box is a **pure higher-order function**. The same pipeline can be distributed across cluster nodes (MapReduce paradigm).

---

## 4.4 Block-Level OOP Architecture in Julia

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["AbstractType Shape"]
    nodeB["Struct Circle radius Float64"]
    nodeC["Struct Rectangle width height Float64"]
    nodeD["Function area returns Float64"]
    nodeE["Method area Circle"]
    nodeF["Method area Rectangle"]
    nodeG["Caller code shapes array"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeD --> nodeF
    nodeE -. dispatches when arg .-> nodeB
    nodeF -. dispatches when arg .-> nodeC
    nodeG --> nodeD
```

**Reading the diagram:** Notice the architectural difference from class-based OOP: the *function* `area` lives at the top level; *methods* `area(::Circle)` and `area(::Rectangle)` are attached to it. The caller never says "shape, do your area"; it says "area, given this shape".

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Multiple Dispatch. How does it differ from Single Dispatch? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Multiple Dispatch** is a feature of Julia where the method to be executed is selected based on the **types of all positional arguments** of the function call, not just the receiver. In single dispatch (used by C++, Java, Python), method selection depends only on the type of the first argument (the `this`/`self` object).

In Julia, when a function `f(a, b, c)` is called, the runtime examines the tuple of types `(typeof(a), typeof(b), typeof(c))` and chooses the **most specific** applicable method from the method table. This makes code more compositional and avoids the "visitor pattern" gymnastics required in single-dispatch OOP.

**Key differentiators (1 mark each):**
1. **All arguments participate** in dispatch (vs. only the first).
2. **No classes needed** — dispatch is on Julia's *type lattice* using abstract/concrete types.
3. **Performance** — Julia's JIT compiles a specialised version for each type signature, often matching C speed.

---

### Question 2: What is a Closure? Illustrate with a Julia example. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **closure** is a function value that **captures variables from its enclosing lexical scope**. The captured variables remain accessible even after the outer function has returned, giving the closure *persistent state*.

```julia
function power_generator(n)
    return x -> x^n      # closure capturing n
end

square = power_generator(2)
cube   = power_generator(3)

square(5)   # 25
cube(2)     # 8
```

Here, `square` and `cube` are distinct closures, each capturing a different value of `n` from their respective calls to `power_generator`. This satisfies the KTU requirement of *one code example* (1 mark) plus the definition (1 mark) and the explanation of state (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - For OOP questions, students *lose marks* by treating Julia like Java — **do not write `class`**. Use `struct` and `abstract type`.
> - For FP questions, mixing up `map` (returns collection) with `reduce` (returns scalar) costs 2 marks.
> - In multiple-dispatch traces, **forgetting to exclude non-applicable methods** loses 1 mark per error.
> - Always show method signatures and runtime type arguments side-by-side when tracing dispatch.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): Object-Oriented Modelling with Multiple Dispatch `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Design an abstract type hierarchy for an Employee management system with `Employee` as the abstract supertype, and two concrete subtypes `PermanentEmployee` and `ContractEmployee`. Each employee has a `name::String` and a `base_salary::Float64`. Permanent employees have a `bonus` field; contract employees have an `hourly_rate` and `hours_worked`. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Define a function `calculate_salary(e::Employee)::Float64` using **multiple dispatch** so that the salary formula differs for each employee type. Show the output of calling `calculate_salary` on one of each. Also explain why this design would be cumbersome in a single-dispatch language. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) — Type Hierarchy Definition (7 marks)**

```julia
# Step 1: abstract supertype
abstract type Employee end

# Step 2: concrete subtype for permanent employees
struct PermanentEmployee <: Employee
    name::String
    base_salary::Float64
    bonus::Float64
end

# Step 3: concrete subtype for contract employees
struct ContractEmployee <: Employee
    name::String
    base_salary::Float64
    hourly_rate::Float64
    hours_worked::Float64
end
```

*Breakdown of marks:*
- Abstract type declaration — **1 mark**
- `PermanentEmployee` struct with all three fields — **2 marks**
- `ContractEmployee` struct with all four fields — **2 marks**
- Correct use of `<: Employee` (subtyping) — **2 marks**

---

**(b) — Multiple Dispatch Salary Calculation (7 marks)**

```julia
# Method for permanent employees: base + bonus
function calculate_salary(e::PermanentEmployee)
    return e.base_salary + e.bonus
end

# Method for contract employees: base + (rate * hours)
function calculate_salary(e::ContractEmployee)
    return e.base_salary + e.hourly_rate * e.hours_worked
end
```

**Demonstration:**

```julia
p = PermanentEmployee("Alice", 50000.0, 10000.0)
c = ContractEmployee("Bob", 20000.0, 500.0, 80.0)

calculate_salary(p)   # 60000.0
calculate_salary(c)   # 20000.0 + 500.0 * 80.0 = 60000.0
```

Mathematical verification:

$$ \text{salary}(\text{Alice}) = 50000.0 + 10000.0 = 60000.0 $$

$$ \text{salary}(\text{Bob}) = 20000.0 + 500.0 \times 80.0 = 60000.0 $$

**Why cumbersome in single-dispatch? (3 of the 7 marks):**
In a single-dispatch language (e.g., Java), `calculate_salary` would have to be a *virtual method* overridden in each subclass, and the call site would have to use polymorphism on the receiver only. To handle new behaviour based on *combinations* of argument types (e.g., `calculate_salary(e::Employee, tax_regime::TaxType)`), one would need the **visitor pattern** or chained `instanceof` checks, leading to brittle, non-compositional code. Julia's multiple dispatch handles this with a single, clean method definition per type combination.

*Breakdown of marks:*
- Two correct method definitions — **2 marks**
- Mathematical verification of results — **1 mark**
- Demo calls producing correct output — **1 mark**
- Comparison with single-dispatch (visitor pattern) — **3 marks**

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Functional Programming Pipeline `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Define a higher-order function in Julia that takes a predicate `p` and a vector `v` and returns a new vector containing only the elements of `v` that satisfy `p`. Implement it two ways: (i) using the built-in `filter`, and (ii) using a manual `map` + comprehension approach. Compare both implementations. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Given the dataset `temperatures = [22, 35, 18, 41, 29, 12, 33, 27]`, construct a single Julia expression using `|>`, broadcasting, and `filter` to:
  1. Keep only temperatures between 20°C and 35°C (inclusive).
  2. Convert them to Fahrenheit using the formula $F = C \times \frac{9}{5} + 32$.
  3. Compute their mean.
Show the full pipeline and the final numeric result. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) — Higher-Order Filter (7 marks)**

**Implementation (i) — built-in `filter`:**

```julia
keep_if(p::Function, v::Vector) = filter(p, v)

# Usage
keep_if(x -> x > 0, [-1, 2, -3, 4])   # [2, 4]
```

The built-in `filter` iterates once, calling `p(x)` on each element and collecting those returning `true`.

**Implementation (ii) — manual comprehension approach:**

```julia
keep_if_manual(p::Function, v::Vector) = [x for x in v if p(x)]
```

Both yield the same result, but the built-in `filter` is **more idiomatic and JIT-optimised** in Julia. Comprehensions are arguably more readable for nested cases.

**Comparison (3 of the 7 marks):**
| Aspect | `filter` | Comprehension |
|---|---|---|
| Performance | C-optimised, allocates minimally | Slightly slower for very large vectors |
| Readability | Concise | More familiar to Python users |
| Flexibility | Limited to one predicate | Can embed multiple `if` conditions |
| Composability | Pairs naturally with `\|>` | Needs careful parentheses |

*Breakdown of marks:*
- Built-in implementation — **1 mark**
- Manual implementation — **1 mark**
- Working example with output — **1 mark**
- Comparison table / discussion — **3 marks**
- Function and predicate typing — **1 mark**

---

**(b) — Functional Pipeline with `|>` (7 marks)**

```julia
temperatures = [22, 35, 18, 41, 29, 12, 33, 27]

result = temperatures |>
    x -> filter(t -> 20 <= t <= 35, x) |>
    x -> (x .* 9 ./ 5) .+ 32 |>
    x -> sum(x) / length(x)

# Step-by-step:
# 1) filter  → [22, 35, 29, 33, 27]
# 2) convert → [71.6, 95.0, 84.2, 91.4, 80.6]
# 3) mean    → (71.6 + 95.0 + 84.2 + 91.4 + 80.6) / 5
#            = 422.8 / 5
#            = 84.56
```

**Mathematical derivation of Step 2 (Fahrenheit conversion):**

For each Celsius value $C_i$:

$$ F_i = C_i \times \frac{9}{5} + 32 $$

Numerically:

$$ F_1 = 22 \times 1.8 + 32 = 39.6 + 32 = 71.6 $$

$$ F_2 = 35 \times 1.8 + 32 = 63.0 + 32 = 95.0 $$

$$ F_3 = 29 \times 1.8 + 32 = 52.2 + 32 = 84.2 $$

$$ F_4 = 33 \times 1.8 + 32 = 59.4 + 32 = 91.4 $$

$$ F_5 = 27 \times 1.8 + 32 = 48.6 + 32 = 80.6 $$

**Step 3 (mean):**

$$ \bar{F} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} F_i = \frac{71.6 + 95.0 + 84.2 + 91.4 + 80.6}{5} = \frac{422.8}{5} = 84.56 $$

*Breakdown of marks:*
- Correct use of `|>` (pipe operator) — **2 marks**
- Correct filter predicate `20 <= t <= 35` — **1 mark**
- Broadcasting for F conversion — **2 marks**
- Correct mean computation — **1 mark**
- Final numeric answer `84.56` — **1 mark**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Julia is multi-paradigm**: it supports OOP-like patterns via `struct` + multiple dispatch, and FP via first-class functions.
- **No `class` keyword** in Julia. Use `struct` (immutable) or `mutable struct`.
- **Abstract types** model hierarchy; they cannot be instantiated. Concrete types can.
- **Multiple dispatch** uses **all** argument types to choose a method. This is Julia's *defining feature*.
- **Method ambiguity** arises when two methods are equally specific; resolve with a more specific method.
- **Default structs are immutable**. Use `mutable struct` only when mutation is required.
- **`map` returns a collection**; **`reduce` returns a scalar**; **`filter` returns a subset**.
- **Broadcasting** is the dot syntax `f.(x)` — it is a *syntactic shortcut for `map(f, x)`*.
- **Closures** capture enclosing-scope variables. Each call to the outer function creates a fresh captured environment.
- **The pipe operator** `|>` passes the LHS as the *first* argument of the RHS — it is left-to-right composition.
- **Pure functions** yield the same output for the same input and produce no side effects; a function ending in `!` (e.g., `sort!`) is generally *impure*.
- **KTU 2024 favourite questions**: tracing multiple dispatch, designing small type hierarchies, and writing FP pipelines on toy datasets.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Parallel programming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEIET861 - DATA SCIENCE WITH JULIA > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Parallel programming # ⚡ PARALLEL PROGRAMMING IN JULIA — KTU 2024 SCHEME ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)** > **Parallel Programming** is a computational paradigm in which a problem is decomposed into discrete sub-problems that can be solved **concurrently** by multiple processing units (cores, threads, processes, or distributed machines) cooperating through shared memory or message passing to achieve faster execution, scalability, and improved resource utilization. ### 🍕 Real-World Intuitive Analogy — The "Multi-Chef Pizza Kitchen" Imagine a single chef (serial processor) trying to bake **100 pizzas** for a stadium crowd. He must: 1. Roll dough → 2. Add sauce → 3. Add cheese → 4. Bake → repeat **100 times**. That chef can only do **one operation at a time**. He will take ~10 hours. Now, imagine hiring **10 chefs** and giving each one the responsibility of making **10 pizzas end-to-end**. The total time drops to ~1 hour. This is **Parallel Programming** — dividing the **workload** (data) among **multiple workers** (CPU cores/threads/processes) to finish the job faster. | KTU Term | Pizza Kitchen Analogy | |---|---| | **Task** | One chef's full job | | **Worker / Core** | A single chef | | **Channel** | A shared counter where chefs put completed pizzas | | **Distributed Memory** | Each chef works in a separate kitchen (no shared stove) | | **Shared Memory** | All chefs share the same kitchen counter (data race possible) | | **Speedup (S)** | Time(1 chef) ÷ Time(N chefs) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Distinction for the Board Exam** > - **Concurrency** = *Dealing with* many things at once (logical). > - **Parallelism** = *Doing* many things at once (physical, simultaneous). > Julia supports **both**, but **Parallel Programming** specifically refers to true simultaneous execution on multiple hardware units. ### 🎯 Types of Parallelism (High-Yield for KTU) 1. **Task Parallelism** — Different *tasks* run on different cores. 2. **Data Parallelism** — Same *task* operates on different chunks of data (Julia's `@distributed` strength). 3. **Bit-level Parallelism** — Wider registers (32-bit → 64-bit). 4. **Instruction-level Parallelism (ILP)** — Pipelining inside CPU. > [!TIP] > Julia's slogan — *"walk like Python, run like C, parallel like Fortran"* — refers to the fact that **MPI, OpenMP, and CUDA-style parallelism** is built into the language with minimal boilerplate. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 🧠 2.1 The Julia Concurrency & Parallelism Stack Julia organizes its parallelism into **four clearly separated layers** (this is a frequently-tested structural question): ``` Layer 4: GPU Computing (CUDA.jl, AMDGPU.jl, KernelAbstractions.jl) Layer 3: Distributed Computing (Distributed.jl) ← Across machines / processes Layer 2: Multi-Threading (Base.Threads) ← Inside ONE process Layer 1: Coroutines / Tasks (Base.Task, Channel) ← Cooperative, single-threaded ``` Each layer is **independent and composable** — you can run a `@distributed` loop across 8 worker processes, where each process itself uses 4 threads, and each thread launches GPU kernels. ### 🧩 2.2 Layer 1 — Coroutines (Tasks & Channels) #### 🔹 Tasks (Cooperative Concurrency) A **Task** in Julia is a *suspendable* computation unit — the official term is **coroutine**. Unlike a thread, a task is **not** preempted by the OS; it voluntarily yields control via `I/O`, `sleep`, `wait`, or explicit `yieldto`. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Definition:** *"A `Task` is a control structure that allows computations to be suspended and resumed cooperatively without the use of multiple operating-system threads."* #### 🔹 Channels (Message Passing) A **Channel** is a FIFO queue that lets tasks **communicate and synchronize** without locks. - `put!(ch, value)` — pushes a value (blocks if full for bounded channels). - `take!(ch)` — pops a value (blocks if empty). ### 🧵 2.3 Layer 2 — Multi-Threading (Base.Threads) Julia's `Base.Threads` module provides **shared-memory parallelism** within a single process. Each thread gets its own **stack** but shares the **heap** (and therefore all Julia objects). **Key macros / functions:** - `Threads.nthreads()` — query the thread count. - `Threads.@threads for ...` — parallel `for` loop (static partition). - `Threads.@spawn` — dynamic task on a thread. - `Threads.Atomic{T}` — atomic variable (lock-free primitive). - `Threads.SpinLock` / `Threads.Mutex` — explicit locks. **⚠ Thread Safety Caveat:** Most Julia code is **not thread-safe by default** because of the global interpreter state. You must protect mutations to shared arrays/objects with locks or use `Threads.Atomic`. ### 🌐 2.4 Layer 3 — Distributed Computing (Distributed.jl) Each Julia process is identified by an integer **`WorkerID`** starting from `1` (the master is process `1`). All workers communicate via **TCP messages** (the implementation lives in `cluster_manager.jl`). | Function | Purpose | |---|---| | `addprocs(n)` | Spawn `n` **local** worker processes. | | `addprocs([hosts])` | Spawn workers on **remote machines** (SSH cluster). | | `@everywhere expr` | Evaluate `expr` on **every** process. | | `remotecall(f, pid, args...)` | Asynchronous call to worker `pid`. | | `fetch(::Future)` | Block until the result is ready. | | `@spawnat pid expr` | Shorthand for `remotecall` + auto-`fetch`. | | `@distributed` | Parallel reduction / `for` loop across workers. | | `pmap(f, collection)` | Parallel `map` with built-in load balancing. | | `SharedArray{T, N}` | Array shared across workers (one copy in memory). | | `DistributedArray` (DArrays) | Array **partitioned** across workers (each holds a chunk). | ### 🚀 2.5 Layer 4 — GPU Computing Julia can dispatch array operations to a GPU through `CUDA.jl` (NVIDIA), `AMDGPU.jl`, or `oneAPI.jl`. Functions are **kernelized** via `@cuda` or the `KernelAbstractions` package. ### 📐 2.6 Performance Theory — KTU Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following **6 formulas are the single most-tested analytical block** for this module. Memorize them with units and boundary conditions. | # | Formula | Name | Meaning of Terms | Valid Range | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $S_p = \dfrac{T_1}{T_p}$ | **Speedup** | $T_1$ = serial time, $T_p$ = parallel time on $p$ processors | $1 \le S_p \le p$ | | 2 | $E_p = \dfrac{S_p}{p}$ | **Efficiency** | Fraction of theoretical peak | $0 < E_p \le 1$ | | 3 | $S_p^{Amdahl} = \dfrac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{p}}$ | **Amdahl's Law** | $f$ = parallel fraction, $1-f$ = serial fraction | $0 \le f \le 1$ | | 4 | $S_p^{Gustafson} = p - f \cdot (p - 1)$ | **Gustafson's Law** | Problem size **scales** with $p$ | $0 \le f \le 1$ | | 5 | $C_p = p \cdot T_p - T_1$ | **Cost / Work** | Total CPU-time used | $C_p \ge T_1$ | | 6 | $T_{comm} = \alpha + \beta \cdot m$ | **Communication Time** | $\alpha$ = latency, $\beta$ = per-byte cost, $m$ = message size | — | > [!TIP] > **Memory Aid:** Amdahl's Law = *Fixed problem size* (weak). Gustafson's Law = *Scaled problem size* (strong). They give *different* speedups for the same $f$ and $p$. ### ⚙️ 2.7 Engineering Utility Parallel programming in Julia powers: - **Large-scale ML training** (Flux.jl + Distributed + GPU). - **Genomic & bioinformatics pipelines** (parallel sequence alignment). - **Real-time financial Monte-Carlo simulations**. - **Climate/weather modeling** (PETSc.jl, MPI.jl). - **Distributed hyperparameter sweeps** in AutoML. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 🧮 3.1 Full Derivation of Amdahl's Law (Board-Exam Favourite) > [!IMPORTANT] > This derivation is asked in Part B almost every KTU cycle. Show **every line**. **Assumption:** A program has a *parallel fraction* $f$ (time spent on parts that can be parallelized) and a *serial fraction* $(1 - f)$ (parts that cannot). **Step 1 — Write the serial execution time.** The total serial time is normalized to **1 unit** for mathematical convenience: $$T_1 = 1$$ This is the standard KTU normalization. It means we measure all times as a *fraction of the serial time*. **Step 2 — Decompose serial time into parallel and serial portions.** $$T_1 = f \cdot T_1 + (1 - f) \cdot T_1 = f + (1 - f) = 1$$ By definition, the **parallel portion** takes time $f$ on one processor, and the **serial portion** takes time $(1 - f)$ on one processor. **Step 3 — Apply $p$ processors to the parallel portion only.** The serial portion $(1 - f)$ **cannot** be split — it still needs 1 processor. The parallel portion $f$ is divided among all $p$ processors equally, giving: $$T_{parallel} = \frac{f}{p}$$ **Step 4 — Add the times (parallel + serial) for the new total time.** $$T_p = \frac{f}{p} + (1 - f)$$ **Step 5 — Combine the two terms over a common denominator.** $$\begin{aligned} T_p &= \frac{f}{p} + (1-f) \\ &= \frac{f + (1-f)\cdot p}{p} \\ &= \frac{f + p - fp}{p} \\ &= \frac{p - f(p - 1)}{p} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 6 — Compute speedup $S_p = T_1 / T_p$.** $$\begin{aligned} S_p &= \frac{T_1}{T_p} = \frac{1}{\frac{p - f(p-1)}{p}} \\ &= \frac{p}{p - f(p-1)} \\ &= \frac{p}{p(1-f) + f} \\ &= \frac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{p}} \end{aligned}$$ ✅ This is the **final boxed form of Amdahl's Law**. **Step 7 — Verify boundary conditions.** - $f = 0$ (purely serial): $S_p = 1/1 = 1$ ✔ (no speedup possible) - $f = 1$ (purely parallel): $S_p = 1/(1/p) = p$ ✔ (perfect linear speedup) - $p \to \infty$: $S_p \to 1/(1-f)$ ✔ (hard ceiling = serial bottleneck) ### 🧮 3.2 Full Derivation of Gustafson's Law **Assumption:** The *problem size* scales linearly with $p$ such that the parallel workload takes time $f \cdot p$ (i.e., the same per-processor effort as the serial run). **Step 1 — New parallel time on $p$ processors.** $$T_p = f \cdot p + (1 - f)$$ The serial part still takes $(1 - f)$. The parallel part now scales: each of the $p$ processors handles a unit of work, total $p$ units. **Step 2 — Speedup using the new $T_p$ as denominator and a scaled baseline.** Since the problem has *grown*, the equivalent serial time on one processor would be $f \cdot p + (1 - f)$.
