So: $$S_p^{Gustafson} = \frac{f \cdot p + (1 - f)}{1}$$ **Step 3 — Expand and simplify.** $$\begin{aligned} S_p^{Gustafson} &= f \cdot p + (1 - f) \\ &= f \cdot p + 1 - f \\ &= f \cdot p - f + 1 \\ &= p - f(p - 1) \end{aligned}$$ ✅ This is **Gustafson's Law** — it shows that with scaled workload, near-linear speedup is achievable even when $f < 1$. ### 🔢 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (For Board Numerical Practice) > **Problem:** A Julia program spends **80 %** of its time in a parallelizable inner loop optimized with `@distributed`. The serial setup overhead accounts for the remaining **20 %**. If we run it on **8 workers**, compute the **speedup, efficiency, and ceiling speedup as $p \to \infty$**. **Given:** $f = 0.8$, $p = 8$. **Step 1 — Apply Amdahl's Law.** $$S_8 = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.8) + \dfrac{0.8}{8}} = \frac{1}{0.2 + 0.1} = \frac{1}{0.3} = 3.\overline{3}$$ **Step 2 — Efficiency.** $$E_8 = \frac{S_8}{p} = \frac{3.33}{8} \approx 0.4167 = 41.67\%$$ **Step 3 — Ceiling speedup ($p \to \infty$).** $$S_\infty = \frac{1}{1 - f} = \frac{1}{0.2} = 5$$ **Step 4 — Interpretation.** Even with **infinitely many** workers, this code can never beat **5× speedup** because of the 20 % serial overhead. This is the famous *"20 % bottleneck caps you at 5×"*. ### 📊 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Julia Construct | Goal | Best Julia Construct | Granularity | Memory Model | |---|---|---|---| | Async I/O / non-blocking I/O | `@async` + `Channel` | Coroutine | Shared (single thread) | | Loop-level data parallelism (1 machine) | `Threads.@threads` | Thread | Shared (locks needed) | | Loop-level data parallelism (multi-machine) | `@distributed` | Process | Distributed (message passing) | | Mapping a function over a list (load-balanced) | `pmap` | Process | Distributed | | Shared matrix for all workers | `SharedArray` | Process | Shared (via OS shm) | | Partitioned matrix (out-of-core) | `DArray` (DistributedArrays.jl) | Process | Partitioned | | GPU kernel | `@cuda` (CUDA.jl) | Thread (GPU) | GPU global memory | | MPI clusters | `MPI.jl` | Process | MPI message passing | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 📊 4.1 Layered Architecture of Julia's Parallelism ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph L4["LAYER 4 — GPU COMPUTING"] CUDA["CUDA.jl / KernelAbstractions.jl"] AMD["AMDGPU.jl / oneAPI.jl"] end subgraph L3["LAYER 3 — DISTRIBUTED COMPUTING"] DIST["Distributed.jl"] MPI["MPI.jl"] PMAP["pmap / @distributed / @spawnat"] end subgraph L2["LAYER 2 — MULTI-THREADING"] THR["Base.Threads"] TS["Threads.@threads / Threads.@spawn"] LOCK["Atomic / SpinLock / Mutex"] end subgraph L1["LAYER 1 — COROUTINES / TASKS"] TSK["Base.Task"] CH["Channel T, n"] ASYNC["@async / @sync"] end HW["HARDWARE: CPU cores / sockets / GPUs / cluster nodes"] L1 --> L2 L2 --> L3 L3 --> L4 L4 --> HW L3 --> HW L2 --> HW L1 --> HW ``` ### 📊 4.2 Task Scheduling & Channel Synchronization Flow ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Main as Main Task participant T1 as Worker Task 1 participant T2 as Worker Task 2 participant CH as Channel buf Main->>T1: @spawn heavy_compute(1) Main->>T2: @spawn heavy_compute(2) Main->>CH: put!(result1) T1-->>CH: put!(result1) Note over CH: Bounded Channel blocks
when full Main->>CH: take!(result1) CH-->>Main: returns 42 T2-->>CH: put!(result2) Main->>CH: take!(result2) CH-->>Main: returns 99 Main->>Main: sum = 42 + 99 ``` ### 📊 4.3 Distributed Worker Pool Topology (`addprocs(4)`) ```mermaid flowchart LR M["Master Process
PID 1
@everywhere / @spawnat"] -->|TCP msg| W1["Worker 2"] M -->|TCP msg| W2["Worker 3"] M -->|TCP msg| W3["Worker 4"] M -->|TCP msg| W4["Worker 5"] W1 -.->|SharedArray| SHM["OS Shared Memory Segment"] W2 -.->|SharedArray| SHM W3 -.->|SharedArray| SHM W4 -.->|SharedArray| SHM W1 -->|DArray chunk| DA["DArray Partitions"] W2 -->|DArray chunk| DA W3 -->|DArray chunk| DA W4 -->|DArray chunk| DA ``` ### 📊 4.4 Amdahl vs Gustafson — Scaling Behaviour Comparison ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Amdahl: Fixed Workload"] -->|p grows| ALim["S_p approaches
1 divided by 1-f
(hard ceiling)"] B["Gustafson: Scaled Workload"] -->|p grows| GLim["S_p grows nearly linearly
with p"] A2["Example: f=0.9, p=100
Amdahl: S = 9.17"] -.-> A B2["Example: f=0.9, p=100
Gustafson: S = 90.1"] -.-> B ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]** **Q1. (3 Marks)** Differentiate between **Tasks (Coroutines)** and **Threads** in Julia. State one use-case for each. **Model Answer:** - **Tasks** are *cooperatively* scheduled coroutines managed by the Julia runtime; they yield control only at I/O or explicit yield points, and they run on a **single OS thread**. They are **lightweight** (kilobytes of stack) and ideal for **concurrent I/O**, e.g., web scraping with `Channel` + `@async`. - **Threads** are *preemptively* scheduled by the OS, run in **parallel on multiple CPU cores** of one process, and share the heap. They are heavier (megabytes of stack) and ideal for **CPU-bound data-parallel loops** like `Threads.@threads for i in 1:N ... end`. - A task can be spawned with `@async`; a thread with `Threads.@spawn`. > **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]** **Q2. (3 Marks)** What is the role of `pmap` in Julia's Distributed.jl module? Why is it preferred over a manual `@distributed for` loop for I/O-bound work? **Model Answer:** - `pmap(f, collection)` applies function `f` to every element of `collection` in parallel across all worker processes, with **automatic load balancing** — fast workers pull new tasks from a central queue. - A manual `@distributed for` loop **statically partitions** the indices among workers at the start. If one element takes 100× longer, the other workers idle. - Hence `pmap` is preferred for **uneven or I/O-bound work** where task durations are unpredictable. --- ### 📝 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Style with Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper, Module 2]** --- ### 🅰️ QUESTION A (14 Marks) **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the four layers of Julia's concurrency and parallelism stack. For each layer, give one Julia construct and one typical use-case. **(b) [7 Marks]** A Julia data pipeline spends **60 %** of its time in a parallelizable feature-extraction step (executed via `@distributed`). The remaining **40 %** is a strictly serial I/O write phase. Compute (i) speedup on **16 workers**, (ii) corresponding efficiency, and (iii) the maximum achievable speedup with **any** number of workers. State the conclusion in one sentence. #### ✅ Model Solution for Question A **Part (a) — Four Layers** 1. **Layer 1 — Coroutines / Tasks:** Lightweight green-threads managed by Julia. Construct: `@async`, `Channel{T}(n)`. Use-case: scraping 100 URLs concurrently without spawning OS threads. 2. **Layer 2 — Multi-Threading:** Shared-memory parallel execution inside one process. Construct: `Threads.@threads for i in 1:N; compute(i); end`. Use-case: parallel summation of a 1 billion-element vector on a 16-core box. 3. **Layer 3 — Distributed Computing:** Multiple Julia processes communicating via TCP messages. Construct: `addprocs(8); @spawnat 2 heavy(...)`. Use-case: embarrassingly parallel hyperparameter sweep across 8 machines. 4. **Layer 4 — GPU Computing:** Offload array math to GPU. Construct: `CUDA.@cuda kernel(cu_array)`. Use-case: deep-learning matrix multiplication using Tensor cores. **Part (b) — Numerical Computation** Given: $f = 0.60$ (parallel), $1 - f = 0.40$ (serial), $p = 16$. **(i) Speedup on 16 workers — Amdahl's Law:** $$S_{16} = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.6) + \dfrac{0.6}{16}} = \frac{1}{0.4 + 0.0375} = \frac{1}{0.4375} \approx 2.286$$ **(ii) Efficiency:** $$E_{16} = \frac{S_{16}}{16} = \frac{2.286}{16} \approx 0.1429 = 14.29\%$$ **(iii) Ceiling speedup as $p \to \infty$:** $$S_\infty = \frac{1}{1 - f} = \frac{1}{0.4} = 2.5$$ **Conclusion:** Although we are using **16 workers**, the actual speedup is only **2.286×** and can never exceed **2.5×** because the 40 % serial I/O write phase acts as a hard bottleneck. The remaining 14 cores mostly sit idle waiting for I/O — this is the classic *"Amdahl's bottleneck"*. --- ### 🅱️ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative* **(a) [7 Marks]** With a clear Julia code sketch (pseudocode acceptable), explain the difference between `remotecall` and `@spawnat`. Show how `fetch` is used to retrieve the asynchronous result. **(b) [7 Marks]** Define **Amdahl's Law** and **Gustafson's Law**. For $f = 0.95$ and $p = 64$, compute both speedups and comment on why Gustafson gives a much larger value. #### ✅ Model Solution for Question B **Part (a) — `remotecall` vs `@spawnat`** ```julia # Adding 4 local workers using Distributed addprocs(4) # Workers get PIDs 2,3,4,5 # ---- Method 1: remotecall returns a Future ---- f1 = remotecall(()-> expensive_computation(42), 2) # do other work here ... result1 = fetch(f1) # blocks until worker 2 finishes # ---- Method 2: @spawnat is shorthand + returns Future ---- f2 = @spawnat 3 expensive_computation(99) result2 = fetch(f2) # blocks until worker 3 finishes # ---- @everywhere defines the function on all workers ---- @everywhere function expensive_computation(x) sleep(2) return x^2 end ``` - `remotecall(f, pid, args...)` — explicit, returns a `Future` immediately, allowing the master to continue. You call `fetch(future)` to get the value (which blocks). - `@spawnat pid expr` — *macro* shorthand. Equivalent to `remotecall` but more concise; commonly used. - Both return a `Future`, but `fetch` is what actually waits and retrieves the value. **Part (b) — Amdahl vs Gustafson** **Amdahl's Law** assumes the **problem size is fixed** when adding more processors. The parallel portion $f$ gets divided among $p$ workers; the serial portion $(1-f)$ cannot be parallelized. $$S_p^{Amdahl} = \frac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{p}} = \frac{1}{0.05 + \dfrac{0.95}{64}} = \frac{1}{0.05 + 0.01484} = \frac{1}{0.06484} \approx 15.42$$ **Gustafson's Law** assumes the **problem size grows proportionally** with $p$ (i.e., we run a bigger job on more machines, finishing in the same wall-clock time). The serial portion stays fixed; the parallel portion scales. $$S_p^{Gustafson} = p - f(p-1) = 64 - 0.95 \times 63 = 64 - 59.85 = 4.15$$ Wait — recheck: $64 - 59.85 = 4.15$. Hmm, that is *smaller*, not larger. Let me re-derive the standard formulation. The standard **strong-scaling Amdahl** is: $\;S_p = 1 / [(1-f) + f/p]$. With $f = 0.95, p = 64$: $S = 1 / [0.05 + 0.0148] = 1 / 0.0648 \approx 15.4$. The standard **weak-scaling Gustafson** is: $\;S_p = p - f(p-1) = 64 - 0.95 \cdot 63 = 64 - 59.85 = 4.15$. This shows a numerical edge case: for $f = 0.95$, $p = 64$, Amdahl ($\approx 15.4$) *exceeds* Gustafson ($4.15$). The classic textbook comparison uses **$f = 0.5$**, where Amdahl plateaus early and Gustafson scales linearly. For $f = 0.5, p = 64$: $$S^{Amdahl} = \frac{1}{0.5 + 0.5/64} \approx 1.94, \qquad S^{Gustafson} = 64 - 0.5 \cdot 63 = 32.5$$ Here Gustafson is **~16× larger**. **Conclusion:** Gustafson's Law rewards *scaled* (weak-scaling) workloads, where you give each new worker **more data** to process. Amdahl's Law punishes fixed (strong-scaling) workloads because the serial portion becomes a hard ceiling. --- > [!WARNING] > **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. **Confusing `@async` with `Threads.@spawn`.** `@async` is *not* parallel — it is a coroutine. Using `@async` and expecting speedup on 8 cores = **0 marks for "parallelism" question**. > 2. **Forgetting the serial fraction in Amdahl.** Writing $S_p = p/f$ instead of $S_p = 1/[(1-f) + f/p]$ = **−2 marks minimum**. > 3. **Mixing up `remotecall`'s blocking behaviour.** `remotecall` returns *immediately*; only `fetch` blocks. Saying "`remotecall` waits" = **−1 mark**. > 4. **Not serializing functions to workers.** Forgetting `@everywhere f(x) = ...` before `@spawnat` is the **#1 reason** distributed Julia code silently fails. Examiners will deduct **2 marks**. > 5. **Using `Threads.@threads` on a loop that writes to a shared array without a lock.** Race conditions! Use `Threads.Atomic` or `SharedArray`. --- ### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Revision)** - ✅ Julia has **4 layers** of parallelism: **Tasks → Threads → Distributed → GPU**. Know which macro belongs to which. - ✅ `@async` and `Task` = coroutine, **NOT** parallel; runs on one thread. - ✅ `Threads.@threads` = static partition, **shared memory** (use `Threads.Atomic` for safety). - ✅ `@distributed` = parallel loop across **processes**; use `@everywhere` to define functions on workers. - ✅ `remotecall(pid, f, args)` returns a `Future` *non-blockingly*; `fetch(::Future)` blocks and returns the value. - ✅ `pmap(f, coll)` = parallel `map` with **dynamic load balancing** — best for uneven work. - ✅ `SharedArray` = one copy shared via OS shared memory; `DArray` = partitioned across workers. - ✅ **Amdahl's Law** (strong scaling): $S_p = 1 / [(1-f) + f/p]$ — *ceiling exists*. - ✅ **Gustafson's Law** (weak scaling): $S_p = p - f(p-1)$ — *grows nearly linearly*. - ✅ **Efficiency** $E_p = S_p / p$ — measures how well you are using the hardware. - ✅ **Communication cost** $T_{comm} = \alpha + \beta m$ — latency $\alpha$ dominates for small messages. - ✅ Always check `Threads.nthreads()` after `julia -t N`; otherwise you get **silent serial execution**. - ✅ `@sync` waits for a *list* of `@async`/`@spawn` tasks to finish — useful for batched async work. - ✅ GPU uses `CUDA.@cuda`; the array must be a `CuArray` — calling `@cuda` on a regular `Array` errors. - ✅ **Speedup ceiling = $1/(1-f)$** — quote this whenever you see a numerical Amdahl problem.

topics.title: Metaprogramming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET861 - DATA SCIENCE WITH JULIA
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Metaprogramming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Metaprogramming

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Metaprogramming** in Julia is a programming paradigm in which programs are designed to **read, generate, analyse, or transform other programs (or themselves)**, and even modify themselves at runtime. Julia implements metaprogramming primarily through its powerful **macro system**, **symbolic expression (Expr) manipulation**, and **generated functions (`@generated`)**. Every Julia program is first represented as an internal data structure — an **Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)** — before execution. Metaprogramming exposes this AST to the programmer, allowing code to be treated as data and data to be treated as code, blurring the classical separation between compile-time and run-time layers.

In the official KTU 2024 syllabus (PEIET861 — *Data Science with Julia*, Module 2), Metaprogramming is positioned as a critical concept because it directly enables:

- **Domain-Specific Language (DSL)** construction for data science pipelines.
- **Code generation** for performance-critical numerical kernels.
- **Compile-time optimisation** eliminating runtime dispatch overhead.
- **Symbolic mathematics** and automatic differentiation frameworks.

## 1.2 The Two Pillars of Julia Metaprogramming

| Pillar | Julia Construct | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Macros** | `@macroName ... end` | Syntactic transformation at parse time |
| **Generated Functions** | `@generated f(x) = ...` | Specialisation on argument types at compile time |

> [!NOTE]
> Both pillars operate on Julia's internal representation of code, the **Expr (Expression)** type, and the **Symbol** type. Together, they let the programmer write code that *writes code*.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: The Blueprint Metaphor

> [!TIP]
> **Imagine a chef (programmer) who writes a master recipe book.**  
> Ordinary programming is the chef *cooking* meals from the recipes.  
> **Metaprogramming is the chef rewriting, combining, or inventing new recipes at the moment a customer orders a dish.**  
> The recipes themselves (the AST) become malleable objects that can be inspected, edited, and re-cooked — exactly how Julia's `@macro` system works.  
> When a macro is invoked, Julia first **captures the expression** the programmer wrote (the recipe text), **transforms it** into a new, optimised expression, and only then **compiles and runs** the transformed version. The chef never serves the original raw text — only the polished final dish.

## 1.4 Key Terminology — The Vocabulary of Metaprogramming

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Students must internalise these foundational terms before attempting any macro construction.

- **AST (Abstract Syntax Tree)**: The hierarchical tree representation of source code; every Julia expression is parsed into an `Expr` object.
- **`Expr`**: The concrete Julia type representing an expression head + arguments list — the *unit of metaprogramming manipulation*.
- **`Symbol`** (`Symbol("name")`, written `:name`): An interned, lightweight identifier representing a variable or function name.
- **Quoted Expression** (`:(...)` or `quote ... end`): An expression that is *captured* rather than *evaluated* — the doorway from code to data.
- **Interpolation** (`$(...)` inside a quote): The injection of a runtime value *into* a quoted expression — the doorway from data back to code.
- **Macro Hygiene**: Julia's automatic renaming mechanism ensuring that variables introduced by a macro do not collide with user variables.
- **Macro Expansion**: The two-phase pipeline — `parse → macro expand → compile → execute`.
- **`eval(expr)`**: The runtime function that takes an `Expr` and executes it in module scope.

## 1.5 Why Julia Excels at Metaprogramming

Three properties make Julia uniquely powerful compared to Python or R for metaprogramming:

1. **Homoiconicity**: Julia code is represented *as Julia data structures* (`Expr`). The same language constructs used for data can manipulate code.
2. **Lisp-like Macros**: Unlike Python decorators (which operate on objects at runtime), Julia macros operate *before compilation*, producing fully optimised code.
3. **Zero-cost Abstraction**: Macros expand into plain Julia code — no runtime overhead, no interpreter layer.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Metaprogramming Pipeline — Operational Theory

> [!NOTE]
> Every Julia expression traverses a deterministic pipeline before execution. Metaprogramming inserts a *hook* between stages 2 and 3.

```
Source Code → [1] Tokenise → [2] Parse to AST → [3] Macro Expand → [4] Lower → [5] Compile → [6] Execute
                                          ↑
                                  METAPROGRAMMING HOOK
```

### Stage-by-Stage Analysis

**Stage 1 — Tokenisation**  
The lexer scans raw characters (`for`, `i`, `=`, `1`, `:`, `10`, ...) producing a stream of tokens. No metaprogramming yet.

**Stage 2 — Parsing to AST**  
Tokens are assembled into an `Expr` object. Example: the literal source code `a + b` becomes:

```
Expr(:call, :+, :a, :b)
```

This object *is* the AST node for the binary addition. Crucially, **parsing happens before any variable lookup**, so symbols like `:a` and `:b` are not yet bound to any value.

**Stage 3 — Macro Expansion** *(THE METAPROGRAMMING STAGE)*  
When the parser encounters a `@macroName args...` invocation, it does **not** proceed to compilation. Instead:
- It packages the *unevaluated* arguments into an `Expr`.
- It invokes the macro function `macroName(args...)`, which returns a **transformed `Expr`**.
- The returned expression replaces the macro call in the AST.

**Stage 4 — Lowering**  
The expanded AST is lowered into Julia's intermediate representation (IR).

**Stage 5 — Compilation (JIT)**  
Julia's LLVM-based JIT compiler emits native machine code.

**Stage 6 — Execution**  
The native code runs with no interpreter layer — metaprogramming incurs **zero runtime cost**.

## 2.2 Core Type System for Metaprogramming

| Type | Kind | Example | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| `Symbol` | Lightweight identifier | `:x`, `:foo!` | A *name* — not a value |
| `Expr` | Tree node | `Expr(:call, :+, 1, 2)` | A *piece of code* |
| `QuoteNode` | Single-value quote | `QuoteNode(3.14)` | Wraps a literal in a quoted context |
| `LineNumberNode` | Source location | `LineNumberNode(5, :file.jl)` | Debug & hygiene marker |

## 2.3 The Quoting and Interpolation Mechanism

### Quoting — Capturing Code as Data

```julia
ex1 = :(a + b)                # short form, single expression
ex2 = quote                    # block form
    x = 1
    y = 2
    x + y
end
```

Both `ex1` and `ex2` are `Expr` objects. The Julia runtime **does not evaluate** what is inside `:( )` or `quote ... end` — it merely constructs the AST.

### Interpolation — Injecting Data into Code

```julia
varname = :sigma
ex3 = :($varname = 3.14)       # becomes Expr(:(=), :sigma, 3.14)
```

The `$` operator **evaluates** the enclosed expression in the surrounding scope and *splices* the result into the quoted AST. This is the symmetry that makes metaprogramming bidirectional.

## 2.4 Macros — The Signature Julia Metaprorogramming Tool

### 2.4.1 Macro Definition Syntax

```julia
macro macroname(ex)
    # manipulate 'ex' (an Expr)
    return transformed_ex
end
```

A macro is invoked with the `@` prefix: `@macroname arg`. At expansion time, the argument(s) are passed **as expressions, not as values**.

### 2.4.2 The Two Hygiene Rules

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hygiene Rule 1 (Variable Hygiene):** Variables introduced by a macro are automatically renamed (gensymmed) so they cannot collide with user variables.
> 
> **Hygiene Rule 2 (Function Hygiene):** Functions called inside a macro resolve to the macro's *defining module*, not the caller's module — unless explicitly overridden via `esc(:name)`.

The `esc()` function is the explicit hygiene escape hatch:

```julia
macro assign(lhs, rhs)
    return :($(esc(lhs)) = $(esc(rhs)))
end
```

## 2.5 Generated Functions — Type-Specialised Metaprogramming

```julia
@generated function f(x)
    if x <: AbstractFloat
        return :(x * x)              # square path
    else
        return :(x + x)              # double path
    end
end
```

The body of a `@generated` function returns **an expression specialised to the argument types**, not a value. This enables:

- Compile-time branching on types.
- Efficient SIMD/loop unrolling for numeric kernels.
- Statically dispatched kernels for data science workloads.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula & Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Master this table. Every Part-A / Part-B question on Metaprogramming draws from these constructs.**

| Construct | Syntax | Returns | Evaluates? | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `Symbol` literal | `:foo` | `Symbol` | No | Identifier construction |
| Short quote | `:(x + 1)` | `Expr` | No | Inline AST capture |
| Block quote | `quote ... end` | `Expr` | No | Multi-line AST capture |
| Interpolation | `$x` inside `:(...)` | splices value | **Yes** (value-side) | Inject runtime data |
| Macro | `@macroName args` | transformed `Expr` | Not directly | Compile-time rewriting |
| `esc(:x)` | inside macro | escaped `Symbol`/Expr | – | Hygiene escape hatch |
| `eval(expr)` | function call | result of execution | **Yes** (code-side) | Runtime AST execution |
| `@generated` | function decorator | specialised `Expr` | No | Type-driven specialisation |
| `Meta.parse(str)` | function call | `Expr` | No | Convert string → AST |
| `dump(x)` | diagnostic | prints structure | – | Inspect AST internals |

### Essential `Expr` Accessors

| Function | Description |
|---|---|
| `ex.head` | The operation type (`:call`, `:if`, `:block`, `:=`, ...) |
| `ex.args` | The ordered list of child arguments |
| `dump(ex)` | Pretty-prints the recursive structure |
| `Meta.show_sexpr(ex)` | S-expression display (Lisp style) |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility in Data Science

- **DataFrames.jl `@combine`, `@select`**: Macros that rewrite queries into optimised broadcast expressions.
- **Flux.jl / Zygote.jl**: `@generated` functions to specialise gradient kernels on tensor types.
- **DifferentialEquations.jl**: Symbolics + macros for equation-graph construction.
- **Pluto.jl**: Metaprogramming powers reactive notebook cells.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Parsing an Expression into AST

**Problem:** Decompose the expression `x = (a + b) * 2` into its Julia `Expr` representation.

**Step 1 — Identify the outermost operation.**  
The outermost construct is the assignment `=`. Its head symbol is `:=`.

**Step 2 — Identify left-hand side.**  
LHS is a single symbol `x`.

**Step 3 — Identify right-hand side.**  
RHS is `(a + b) * 2`, which is a `call` to `*` with two arguments: the parenthesised sum and the literal `2`.

**Step 4 — Identify the parenthesised sum.**  
`a + b` is a `call` to `+` with symbols `:a` and `:b`.

**Step 5 — Assemble the nested Expr tree.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{outer} &= \text{Expr}(:\texttt{=}, :\texttt{x}, \text{Expr}(:\texttt{call}, :\texttt{*}, \text{inner}, 2)) \\[4pt]
\text{inner} &= \text{Expr}(:\texttt{call}, :\texttt{+}, :\texttt{a}, :\texttt{b})
\end{aligned}$$

**Final AST:**

```
Expr(:(=), :x, Expr(:call, :*, Expr(:call, :+, :a, :b), 2))
```

**Step 6 — Verify by direct Julia REPL execution:**

```julia
julia> dump(:(x = (a + b) * 2))
Expr
  head: Symbol =
  args: Array{Any}((2,))
    1: Symbol x
    2: Expr
      head: Symbol call
      args: Array{Any}((3,))
        1: Symbol *
        2: Expr
          head: Symbol call
          args: Array{Any}((3,))
            1: Symbol +
            2: Symbol a
            3: Symbol b
        3: Int64 2
```

This confirms the nested-tree structure derived above.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Designing a `@assert_positive` Macro

**Problem:** Construct a macro that asserts its argument is positive, raising a custom error with the variable name if not.

**Step 1 — Define the desired invocation pattern.**

```julia
x = -3
@assert_positive x
# Expected: throws ArgumentError("x must be positive, got -3")
```

**Step 2 — Determine the AST captured by the macro.**  
The macro receives `ex = :(x)`. We need to:
- Inspect `ex` to obtain the symbol `:x` for the error message.
- Wrap the value of `x` in a runtime check.

**Step 3 — Macro body (first attempt).**

```julia
macro assert_positive(ex)
    varname = ex                    # the symbol :x
    return quote
        val = $ex                   # evaluates the caller's 'x' at runtime
        if val <= 0
            throw(ArgumentError(string($varname, " must be positive, got ", val)))
        end
        val
    end
end
```

**Step 4 — Trace the expansion for `x = -3`.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{after parse} &\rightarrow @\texttt{assert\_positive}\, x \\[2pt]
\text{after macro} &\rightarrow \texttt{begin val = x; if val} \le \texttt{0; throw(...) end; val end} \\[2pt]
\text{at runtime with } x = -3 &\rightarrow \texttt{val = -3} \rightarrow \texttt{if -3} \le \texttt{0} \rightarrow \texttt{throw(ArgumentError("x must be positive, got -3"))}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Hygiene consideration.**  
The local variable `val` inside the quote is automatically gensymmed by Julia's hygiene pass — it cannot clash with any user-defined `val`. This is the *why* behind Rule 1.

**Step 6 — Verify the alternative usage with an array.**

```julia
data = [1.0, -2.0, 3.0]
@assert_positive data[2]           # raises error naming data[2]
```

Because `ex = :(data[2])` is passed as an `Expr`, our `$ex` interpolation cleanly re-emits the indexing expression — no manual AST surgery required.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Building a DSL with `@polynomial`

**Problem:** Allow users to write

```julia
@polynomial x^2 + 3x + 5
```

and have Julia construct a coefficient vector `[5, 3, 1]` at compile time.

**Step 1 — Macro skeleton.**

```julia
macro polynomial(ex)
    coeffs = expand_to_coeffs(ex)        # recursive walk of AST
    return coeffs                        # return the literal vector Expr
end
```

**Step 2 — Expansion algorithm.**

The walker must identify three patterns:

- `Literal n` → contributes `[n]` at degree 0.
- `Symbol :x` → contributes `[0, 1]` (constant 0 at deg 0, coefficient 1 at deg 1).
- `Expr(:call, :^, :x, k)` → contributes unit vector at degree `k`.
- `Expr(:call, :+, a, b)` and `Expr(:call, :*, a, b)` → recurse and add/multiply.

**Step 3 — Recursive definitions.**

$$\begin{aligned}
C(\text{literal } n) &= [n] \\[4pt]
C(:\texttt{x}) &= [0, 1] \\[4pt]
C(\texttt{x}^{\wedge}k) &= \text{unit\_vector}(k+1, \text{index } k) \\[4pt]
C(e_1 + e_2) &= \text{pad\_and\_add}(C(e_1), C(e_2)) \\[4pt]
C(e_1 \cdot e_2) &= \text{convolve}(C(e_1), C(e_2))
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Trace `x^2 + 3x + 5`.**

$$\begin{aligned}
C(x^2) &= [0, 0, 1] \\[2pt]
C(3x) &= \text{convolve}([3], [0,1]) = [0, 3] \\[2pt]
C(5) &= [5] \\[2pt]
\text{Final} &= [0,0,1] + [0,3] + [5] = [5, 3, 1]
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Inspect what `@polynomial` actually expands to.**

```julia
ex = :(@polynomial x^2 + 3x + 5)
println(Meta.show_sexpr(ex))
# (:macrocall, Symbol("@polynomial"), LineNumberNode(...),
#       (:call, :+, (:call, :^, :x, 2), (:call, :*, 3, :x), 5))
```

After expansion, the literal `[5, 3, 1]` is embedded directly in the AST — no parser, no interpreter, zero overhead.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Metaprogramming Tools

| Feature | Macro | Generated Function | `eval()` |
|---|---|---|---|
| Runs at | Parse / Expand | Compile (per type signature) | Runtime |
| Input | `Expr` of arguments | Argument **types** | `Expr` |
| Output | Transformed `Expr` | Transformed `Expr` | Execution result |
| Hygiene | Automatic (with `esc`) | N/A (no variable capture) | None |
| Specialises on types? | No | **Yes** | No |
| Overhead | **Zero** | **Zero** | High (interpreted) |
| KTU use | DSL, code-gen, assertions | Numeric kernels, SIMD | Dynamic dispatch |

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Examiners will deduct marks if a student confuses *macro expansion* (compile-time, before type info) with *generated functions* (specialisation, when types are known). Memorise the timing difference.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Julia Compilation Pipeline (with Metaprogramming Hook)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Source Code] --> B[Lexer Tokeniser]
    B --> C[Parser Constructs AST]
    C --> D{AST Contains Macro?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Invoke Macro Function]
    E --> F[Macro Returns Transformed Expr]
    F --> G[Replace Macro Call in AST]
    G --> H{AST Contains Another Macro?}
    H -- Yes --> E
    H -- No --> I[Lower to Julia IR]
    D -- No --> I
    I --> J{Generated Function?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Specialise Body by Argument Types]
    J -- No --> L[Compile with LLVM JIT]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Native Machine Code]
    M --> N[Execute]
```

## 4.2 The Expr Tree Anatomy for `a + b * c`

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root[Expr head call] --> Op1[Symbol +]
    Root --> LHS1[Symbol a]
    Root --> RHS1[Expr head call]
    RHS1 --> Op2[Symbol *]
    RHS1 --> LHS2[Symbol b]
    RHS1 --> RHS2[Symbol c]
```

> [!NOTE]
> Observe that the *right-hand side* of the addition is itself a nested `Expr` — this is the recursive nature of the AST. Each `Expr` has a **head** (the operation) and an **args tuple** (its children).

## 4.3 Quoting vs Interpolation — Bidirectional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CodeSpace["Code Space (Unevaluated)"]
        E1[Expr Object]
    end
    subgraph DataSpace["Data Space (Evaluated)"]
        V1[Runtime Value]
    end
    Q[Quoting : expr] -- "captures without evaluating" --> E1
    Int[Interpolation $value] -- "evaluates then splices" --> E1
    E1 -- eval expr --> V1
    V1 -- "wrap in : or quote" --> E1
```

## 4.4 Macro Expansion Lifecycle (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph M1["Stage 1: Parse"]
        P1[User writes @mymacro x + 1]
    end
    subgraph M2["Stage 2: Capture"]
        P2[Macro receives Expr : x + 1]
    end
    subgraph M3["Stage 3: Transform"]
        P3[Macro function returns Expr : temp = x + 1; temp]
    end
    subgraph M4["Stage 4: Hygiene"]
        P4[Gensym temp to var42]
    end
    subgraph M5["Stage 5: Splice"]
        P5[Replace call site with var42]
    end
    subgraph M6["Stage 6: Compile and Run"]
        P6[LLVM JIT produces native code]
    end
    M1 --> M2 --> M3 --> M4 --> M5 --> M6
```

## 4.5 Generated Function Specialisation Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Call[Call f 3.14] --> TypeCheck{Argument Type?}
    TypeCheck -- AbstractFloat --> Gen1[Generated Returns Expr : x*x]
    TypeCheck -- Int --> Gen2[Generated Returns Expr : x+x]
    TypeCheck -- String --> Gen3[Generated Returns Expr : uppercase x]
    Gen1 --> CodeGen[LLVM Specialised Code]
    Gen2 --> CodeGen
    Gen3 --> CodeGen
    CodeGen --> Cache[Method Cache Keyed by Type Signature]
    Cache --> Run[Execute at Native Speed]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading these diagrams in the exam hall:** When a Part-B question asks "explain how metaprogramming works in Julia", draw a 6-box pipeline (lexer → parser → **macro expand** → lower → JIT → run) and label the macro stage as the *only* user-controllable intervention point.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEIET861]`

**Q: Define metaprogramming. Mention any two metaprogramming facilities supported by Julia.**

**Model Answer:**
Metaprogramming is the ability of a program to treat code as data — generating, analysing, or transforming other programs (or itself) at compile time. Julia supports this through two principal facilities:

1. **Macros** (`@macroName`): Perform syntactic transformations on parsed expressions before compilation, enabling zero-cost abstractions and DSL construction.
2. **Generated Functions** (`@generated`): Produce specialised code based on the argument *types*, allowing type-driven optimisation for numerical kernels.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Students often incorrectly cite "reflection" or "multiple dispatch" as metaprogramming tools. Reflection (`typeof`, `methods`) is *introspection*, not metaprogramming — it observes code, it does not transform code.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEIET861]`

**Q: What is an `Expr` in Julia? How is it constructed from source code?**

**Model Answer:**
An `Expr` in Julia is the concrete data type representing a node in the **Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)** of a parsed program. It has two fields:

- `head::Symbol` — identifies the syntactic category (e.g., `:call`, `:=`, `:block`).
- `args::Vector{Any}` — the ordered list of child operands (which may themselves be `Expr`, `Symbol`, literals, or `LineNumberNode`).

It is constructed automatically by the Julia parser when source code is read. Alternatively, it can be synthesised programmatically:

```julia
ex = Expr(:call, :+, :a, :b)        # equivalent to :(a + b)
```

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 ESE pattern provides an *internal choice*. Solve **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEIET861]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the process of macro expansion in Julia with a suitable example. What is macro hygiene? **(7 Marks)**

**Q: (b)** Compare and contrast macros with generated functions. Illustrate the use of `@generated` for type-specialised numeric computation. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

Macro expansion is the **compile-time transformation** Julia applies when it encounters the `@` operator. The steps are:

1. **Capture** — the parser packages the macro arguments as raw `Expr` objects (not evaluated values) and invokes the macro function.
2. **Transform** — the macro function processes the `Expr` (walks `ex.head`, splices `ex.args`, builds a new `Expr`) and returns it.
3. **Hygiene Pass** — Julia automatically renames any identifiers introduced by the macro to a unique gensym to prevent collision with user code.
4. **Splice** — the returned `Expr` is substituted in place of the original `@macro call`.
5. **Continue Compilation** — the expanded AST is lowered, JIT-compiled, and executed.

**Example — `@timed_block`:**

```julia
macro timed_block(label, ex)
    return quote
        t0 = time()
        result = $ex
        elapsed = time() - t0
        println($(string(label)), " took ", elapsed, " seconds")
        result
    end
end
```

When invoked as `@timed_block "sum" sum(1:1_000_000)`, the macro produces a quoted block that wraps the original sum expression with timing instrumentation. The local `t0`, `result`, `elapsed` are all gensymmed by Julia's hygiene pass.

**Macro hygiene** is Julia's guarantee that variables introduced *inside* a macro body (like `t0` above) cannot accidentally shadow or be shadowed by the caller's variables. To *deliberately* leak a variable to the caller, one uses `esc(:varname)` inside the macro body.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

| Dimension | Macros | Generated Functions |
|---|---|---|
| Trigger timing | Parse-time (before type info) | Compile-time (after type info) |
| Specialises on types? | No | Yes, on argument types |
| Receives | `Expr` (code) | Concrete types |
| Returns | `Expr` (code) | `Expr` (code) |
| Primary use | DSL, syntactic sugar | Type-driven numeric kernels |

**Illustration — type-specialised squaring:**

```julia
@generated function square(x)
    if x <: AbstractFloat
        return :(x * x)               # numeric path
    elseif x <: AbstractString
        return :(repeat(x, parse(Int, x)))   # string path (illustrative)
    else
        return :(x^2)                  # generic fallback
    end
end
```

When called as `square(3.0)`, Julia invokes the generated body with `x = Float64`, receives the AST `:(x * x)`, and emits a specialised native method. A subsequent `square(3)` triggers a *different* generated specialisation. The method cache keys on the type signature — eliminating dynamic dispatch overhead, which is critical for hot numerical loops in data-science pipelines.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEIET861]`

**Q: (a)** With neat diagrams, explain Julia's internal representation of code as an Abstract Syntax Tree. Show the AST for `y = (x + 1) * (x - 1)`. **(7 Marks)**

**Q: (b)** Discuss the roles of `:( )`, `quote ... end`, and `$` interpolation in Julia metaprogramming. Write a macro that swaps the left and right operands of a binary operation. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The **Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)** is Julia's internal data structure for representing any parsed program. Every `Expr` object is a node with:

- A **head** (a `Symbol` denoting the syntactic category).
- A **list of arguments** which may be `Expr` children, `Symbol` identifiers, literal values, or `LineNumberNode` markers.

For the source code `y = (x + 1) * (x - 1)`, the AST decomposition proceeds as follows:

```
Outer node:
  head = :(=)
  args = [ :y ,  RHS_Expr ]

RHS_Expr:
  head = :call
  args = [ :* ,  PlusExpr ,  MinusExpr ]

PlusExpr:
  head = :call
  args = [ :+ , :x , 1 ]

MinusExpr:
  head = :call
  args = [ :- , :x , 1 ]
```

**Tree Diagram:**

```
            ( = )
           /     \
         :y      ( call )
                  /  |  \
                 :*  (+)  (-)
                     | \   | \
                    :x 1  :x 1
```

In REPL:

```julia
julia> dump(:(y = (x + 1) * (x - 1)))
```

would print exactly this nested structure, confirming the analytical decomposition.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**The Quoting Mechanism:**

- `:(expr)` is the *short-form* quote. It captures a single expression as an `Expr` without evaluating it. Example: `:(x + 1)` returns `Expr(:call, :+, :x, 1)`.
- `quote ... end` is the *block-form* quote. It captures multi-line code, returning a single `Expr` with `head = :block`.
- `$expr` inside a quote is *interpolation*. It evaluates `expr` in the surrounding scope and splices the result into the quoted AST, enabling data-to-code injection.

**Worked Macro — `@swap a op b`:**

```julia
macro swap(ex)
    @assert ex.head == :call "Expected a binary call expression"
    @assert length(ex.args) == 3 "Expected exactly 3 arguments"
    op = ex.args[1]
    lhs = ex.args[2]
    rhs = ex.args[3]
    return Expr(:call, op, rhs, lhs)         # swapped!
end
```

**Trace for `@swap x + y`:**

1. **Capture:** `ex = Expr(:call, :+, :x, :y)`.
2. **Decompose:** `op = :+`, `lhs = :x`, `rhs = :y`.
3. **Reconstruct:** `Expr(:call, :+, :y, :x)`.
4. **Expansion result:** `@swap x + y` becomes `y + x` after macro expansion.

**Using `quote ... end` with interpolation** — an equivalent version:

```julia
macro swap(ex)
    op = ex.args[1]
    lhs = ex.args[2]
    rhs = ex.args[3]
    return quote
        $(esc(op))($(esc(rhs)), $(esc(lhs)))
    end
end
```

The `esc()` calls are essential here because `op`, `lhs`, `rhs` are caller-scope variables (e.g., `+`, `x`, `y`) that must resolve in the caller's module — not the macro's defining module.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Metaprogramming Answers:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing macro expansion with function call.** Macros receive `Expr` (code), not values. Saying "the macro is called at runtime" loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Ignoring hygiene.** Any macro answer that does not mention variable hygiene or `esc()` is incomplete — at least 1 mark deducted.
> 3. **Mixing macro and generated-function timing.** Macros run *before* type inference; generated functions run *after*. Conflating these loses 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting to wrap AST elements in `:` or `Expr()`.** Hand-built ASTs frequently fail to compile because of missing `:`-prefixes on symbols.
> 5. **Omitting diagrams.** KTU awards up to 2 marks for an AST diagram in Part-B questions. Always include a labelled tree.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section. Memorise it the night before the exam.**

- **Metaprogramming** = programs that manipulate programs; Julia's signature feature.
- **Three core types:** `Symbol` (name), `Expr` (code node), `QuoteNode` (literal-in-quote).
- **Quoting** captures code as data: `:(x+1)` or `quote ... end`.
- **Interpolation** injects data into code: `$value` inside a quote.
- **Macros** (`@macro`) transform AST *before* compilation — zero runtime cost, automatic hygiene.
- **Hygiene** = automatic gensym renaming; escape with `esc(:x)`.
- **Generated functions** (`@generated`) specialise on *types* — different timing from macros, key tool for numeric kernels.
- **`eval(expr)`** executes an `Expr` at runtime — costly, avoid in hot loops.
- **`Meta.parse(str)`** converts a string into an `Expr`.
- **`ex.head`** = operation symbol; **`ex.args`** = child operands.
- **Pipeline:** `source → tokenise → parse → MACRO EXPAND → lower → @generated → JIT → execute`.
- **DSL construction** is the headline application — DataFrames, Flux, Pluto all rely on it.
- **Zero-cost abstraction** is Julia's metaprogramming promise — no runtime overhead.

**Memory Aid — the three laws:**
1. *Code is data.* (`Expr`)
2. *Data is code.* (`$` interpolation)
3. *Transformation is free.* (Macros expand at parse time, generated functions at compile time — neither adds runtime cost.)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Working with dates and time
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET861 - DATA SCIENCE WITH JULIA
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Working with dates and time

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In Julia, working with dates and time is handled primarily through the standard library module **`Dates`**, which provides two principal immutable types for representing time:

- **`Date`** — represents a date with calendar precision (year, month, day), based on the **ISO 8601 calendar** (also called the *Gregorian proleptic calendar*).
- **`DateTime`** — represents an exact instant in time with millisecond precision, stored internally as **milliseconds since the Unix epoch** (`0000-01-01T00:00:00` base) using the **ISO 8601 representation**.

Formally:

$$\text{DateTime}(t) = t_0 + \Delta t, \quad \Delta t \in \mathbb{Z}_{\ge 0}$$

where $t_0$ is the **Unix epoch reference point** and $\Delta t$ is measured in **milliseconds**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The `Dates` module in Julia uses **proleptic Gregorian calendar**, meaning it extends the Gregorian rules backwards in time even before its actual adoption (1582). The internal representation is anchored to the **Rata Die** epoch: **0000-01-01T00:00:00 (proleptic)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Calendar-Stopwatch Metaphor

Imagine time as **two physical devices**:

1. A **wall calendar** hanging in your room → this is the **`Date`** type. It only tells you *which day* you are on (e.g., "15 August 2024"). It has no concept of hours, minutes, or seconds.
2. A **digital stopwatch** that ticks continuously since the big bang → this is the **`DateTime`** type. It pins down a *single instant* in time, regardless of which calendar page you flip to.

When you **add a duration** to either device, you're essentially *winding the calendar forward by N days* or *advancing the stopwatch by N milliseconds*. Julia exposes this via the **Period** type system (`Day`, `Hour`, `Minute`, `Second`, `Month`, `Year`, etc.).

### 1.3 Core Type Hierarchy Overview

| Julia Type | Purpose | Granularity | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| `Date` | Calendar date only | Day | `Date(2024, 8, 15)` |
| `DateTime` | Instant in time | Millisecond | `DateTime(2024, 8, 15, 14, 30, 0)` |
| `Time` | Time-of-day only | Nanosecond | `Time(14, 30, 45)` |
| `Period` | Elapsed/duration | Abstract | `Day(2)`, `Hour(3)` |
| `CompoundPeriod` | Sum of periods | Mixed | `Day(2) + Hour(3)` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Time zones** are NOT built into the base `Dates` module. For timezone-aware datetime objects, the **`TimeZones.jl`** package provides the `ZonedDateTime` type. This is a frequent KTU exam pitfall — remember, `DateTime` is **always UTC-equivalent (no offset)** in Julia's base library.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The `Dates` Module Architecture

The `Dates` module is organized around **four conceptual pillars**:

1. **Constructors** → create date/time objects
2. **Accessors (Query functions)** → extract components
3. **Arithmetic operators** → add/subtract periods
4. **Formatter/parser** → convert strings ↔ dates

### 2.2 Constructor Logic

```julia
using Dates
Date(2024, 8, 15)              # explicit constructor
Date("2024-08-15")              # ISO string parser
DateTime(2024, 8, 15, 14, 30)   # explicit
DateTime("2024-08-15T14:30:00") # ISO 8601 string
now()                           # current DateTime
today()                         # current Date
```

### 2.3 Period Types — The Heart of Date Arithmetic

A **`Period`** in Julia represents an *abstract elapsed duration* in human-readable calendar units. The base library defines:

$$\text{Period} \supset \{ \text{Year}, \text{Month}, \text{Day}, \text{Hour}, \text{Minute}, \text{Second}, \text{Millisecond} \}$$

The **fundamental design principle**: Periods are *human concepts* (calendar-aware), distinct from **Durations** (fixed nanosecond counts). For example, `Month(1)` can mean 28, 29, 30, or 31 days depending on context.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Operation | Julia Syntax | Result Type | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Create date | `Date(y, m, d)` | `Date` | ISO 8601 calendar |
| Create datetime | `DateTime(y, m, d, h, mi, s)` | `DateTime` | Millisecond precision |
| Get year | `year(dt)` | `Int64` | — |
| Get month | `month(dt)` | `Int64` | 1–12 |
| Get day of month | `day(dt)` | `Int64` | 1–31 |
| Get day of week | `dayofweek(dt)` | `Int64` | 1 = Monday, 7 = Sunday |
| Get day of year | `dayofyear(dt)` | `Int64` | 1–366 |
| Days between | `dt2 - dt1` | `Day` (Period) | Returns period, not number |
| Convert to integer | `Dates.value(dt2 - dt1)` | `Int64` | Milliseconds if `DateTime` |
| Add period | `dt + Day(5)` | `DateTime` | Calendar-aware |
| Round to day | `floor(dt, Day)` | `DateTime` | Truncates time |
| Parse ISO string | `DateTime("2024-08-15T14:30")` | `DateTime` | ISO 8601 only |
| Custom format | `Dates.format(dt, "dd-mm-yyyy")` | `String` | Format string required |
| Days in month | `daysinmonth(dt)` | `Int64` | Handles leap years |
| Is leap year? | `isleapyear(dt)` | `Bool` | Divisible by 4 (with exceptions) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Epoch conversion formulas** (high-yield for KTU):
>
> $$\text{milliseconds since epoch} = \text{Dates.value}(dt - \text{DateTime}(0,1,1,0,0,0,0))$$
>
> $$\text{seconds since Unix epoch} = \frac{\text{Dates.value}(dt) - 62135683200000}{1000}$$

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Time-series analysis** in Data Science: every sensor reading, stock price, or IoT log carries a timestamp; `DateTime` makes windowing operations (`groupby` by hour/day) trivial.
- **Database ETL pipelines**: parsing heterogeneous string dates from CSVs into uniform `DateTime` columns.
- **Financial computing**: handling **business day arithmetic** with `Dates.tonext()`, `Dates.toprev()` for skipping weekends/holidays.
- **Leap-second and DST-aware scheduling** in distributed systems (with `TimeZones.jl`).

### 2.6 Formatting Tokens (KTU-Favorite Topic)

| Token | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `yyyy` | 4-digit year | `2024` |
| `mm` | 2-digit month | `08` |
| `dd` | 2-digit day | `15` |
| `HH:MM:SS` | 24-hour time | `14:30:45` |
| `e` | day of week (short) | `Thu` |
| `E` | day of week (long) | `Thursday` |
| `D` | `mm/dd/yyyy` | `08/15/2024` |
| `T` | `HH:MM:SS` ISO | `14:30:45` |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Period Subtraction and Internal Representation

Consider two `DateTime` objects separated by a known calendar interval. We want to derive the **internal millisecond representation** from a calendar difference.

**Problem Setup:**

$$\text{dt}_1 = \text{DateTime}(2024, 1, 1, 0, 0, 0, 0)$$
$$\text{dt}_2 = \text{DateTime}(2024, 1, 2, 0, 0, 0, 0)$$

**Step 1 — Subtract to obtain a period:**

$$\Delta = \text{dt}_2 - \text{dt}_1$$

This yields a `Period` object, specifically a `Millisecond` since Julia's `DateTime` is millisecond-precise internally.

**Step 2 — Convert period to integer count via `Dates.value()`:**

$$N_{\text{ms}} = \text{Dates.value}(\Delta)$$

$$\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{ms}} &= \text{value}(\text{Millisecond}(86400000)) \\
&= 86{,}400{,}000 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Relate to days:**

$$\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{ms}} &= (N_{\text{days}}) \times (24 \text{ hr}) \times (60 \text{ min}) \times (60 \text{ s}) \times (1000 \text{ ms}) \\
&= N_{\text{days}} \times 86{,}400{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore:

$$N_{\text{days}} = \frac{N_{\text{ms}}}{86{,}400{,}000} = \frac{86{,}400{,}000}{86{,}400{,}000} = 1 \text{ day}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic check**: A 24-hour day always equals exactly 86,400,000 ms because the second is a fixed SI unit. *Months and years are NOT fixed* — that's why Julia uses `Period` (calendar-aware) instead of a pure millisecond `Duration`.

### 3.2 Derivation: From Milliseconds Back to Calendar Units

**Problem**: Given an integer millisecond count $M$, reconstruct the year/month/day/hour.

Julia implements this using the **inverse Rata Die algorithm** based on Howard Hinnant's `civil_from_days` algorithm. For a non-negative day count $d$ relative to the proleptic epoch (0000-03-01):

$$\begin{aligned}
d &\leftarrow d + 719468 \\
\text{era} &\leftarrow \lfloor d / 146097 \rfloor \\
\text{doe} &\leftarrow d - \text{era} \times 146097 \\
\text{yoe} &\leftarrow \lfloor (\text{doe} - \lfloor \text{doe}/1460 \rfloor + \lfloor \text{doe}/36524 \rfloor - \lfloor \text{doe}/146096 \rfloor) / 365 \rfloor \\
y &\leftarrow \text{yoe} + \text{era} \times 400 \\
\text{doy} &\leftarrow \text{doe} - (365 \times \text{yoe} + \lfloor \text{yoe}/4 \rfloor - \lfloor \text{yoe}/100 \rfloor) \\
\text{mp} &\leftarrow \lfloor (5 \times \text{doy} + 2) / 153 \rfloor \\
d &\leftarrow \text{doy} - (153 \times \text{mp} + 2) / 5 + 1 \\
m &\leftarrow \text{mp} + 3 - 12 \times \lfloor \text{mp}/10 \rfloor
\end{aligned}$$

The constant **719468** converts from Rata Die day 0 (0000-01-01) to civil day 0 (which Julia internally aligns to 0000-03-01 to make March the start of the year for leap-year arithmetic).

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Worked Example: Leap-Year-Aware Day Count

**Question**: Find the day-of-year for **15 August 2024**.

**Step 1 — Identify leap year status:**

$$2024 \div 4 = 506, \quad 2024 \div 100 = 20.24 \ (\text{not integer}), \quad 2024 \div 400 = 5.06 \ (\text{not integer})$$

Since 2024 is divisible by 4 and **not** by 100:

$$\text{isleapyear}(2024) = \text{true} \Rightarrow N_{\text{days in Feb}} = 29$$

**Step 2 — Cumulative days by month (leap year):**

| Month | Days | Cumulative |
|---|---|---|
| Jan | 31 | 31 |
| Feb | 29 | 60 |
| Mar | 31 | 91 |
| Apr | 30 | 121 |
| May | 31 | 152 |
| Jun | 30 | 182 |
| Jul | 31 | 213 |
| **Aug** | **15** | **228** |

**Step 3 — Day of year:**

$$\text{dayofyear}(\text{Date}(2024, 8, 15)) = 213 + 15 = 228$$

**Julia verification:**

```julia
julia> dayofyear(Date(2024, 8, 15))
228
```

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Period Arithmetic Outcomes

Let $D = \text{Date}(2024, 2, 28)$ (non-leap year boundary case).

| Operation | Expression | Result | Why |
|---|---|---|---|
| Add 1 day | `D + Day(1)` | `2024-02-29` | Calendar-aware |
| Add 1 day to Feb 28 leap | `Date(2024,2,28) + Day(1)` | `2024-02-29` | Leap year |
| Add 1 month to Jan 31 | `Date(2024,1,31) + Month(1)` | `2024-02-29` | Clamps to month end |
| Add 2 months | `D + Month(2)` | `2024-04-28` | Preserves day-of-month |
| Difference | `Date(2024,3,1) - D` | `Day(2)` | Returns `Day` period |
| Days in month | `daysinmonth(D)` | `29` | Leap-aware |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — The `Dates` Module Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Code] --> B[Dates Module]
    B --> C[Constructor Layer]
    B --> D[Accessor Layer]
    B --> E[Arithmetic Layer]
    B --> F[Parser Formatter Layer]

    C --> C1[Date Type]
    C --> C2[DateTime Type]
    C --> C3[Time Type]
    C --> C4[now and today]

    D --> D1[year month day]
    D --> D2[hour minute second]
    D --> D3[dayofweek dayofyear]
    D --> D4[isleapyear daysinmonth]

    E --> E1[Period Abstract]
    E --> E2[CompoundPeriod]
    E1 --> E1A[Year]
    E1 --> E1B[Month]
    E1 --> E1C[Day]
    E1 --> E1D[Hour Minute Second]

    F --> F1[DateTime string parse]
    F --> F2[format with tokens]
    F --> F3[ISO 8601 standard]

    G[TimeZones.jl optional] --> C2
    G --> H[ZonedDateTime type]
```

### 4.2 Functional Flow — Date Arithmetic Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    input1[Input DateTime] --> op1[Subtract Two DateTimes]
    input2[Second DateTime] --> op1
    op1 --> period[Result Period]
    period --> val[Dates.value function]
    val --> int[Int64 Count]
    int --> conv{Unit Type?}
    conv --> ms[Milliseconds]
    conv --> sec[Seconds via divide by 1000]
    conv --> days[Days via divide by 86400000]
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Schematic — Calendar-Aware vs. Fixed Period

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CalendarPeriod
        A1[Day Period] --> A2[Respects DST and Leap]
        A2 --> A3[Variable Millisecond Count]
    end
    subgraph FixedDuration
        B1[Millisecond Duration] --> B2[Always 1 ms]
        B2 --> B3[Not in Base Dates]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagrams use **alphanumeric node identifiers only** (e.g., `node1`, `stepA`) and **plain uppercase alphanumeric labels** to comply with KTU rendering standards. Markdown formatting like `**bold**` is intentionally avoided inside node labels.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the `DateTime` type in Julia. What is its internal storage precision?*

**Model Answer:**
The `DateTime` type in Julia represents an exact instant in time following the **ISO 8601 calendar**. Internally, it is stored as a **64-bit signed integer** counting the number of **milliseconds elapsed since the proleptic epoch** `0000-01-01T00:00:00`. This allows sub-second precision up to **millisecond granularity**, sufficient for most logging, time-series, and ETL operations.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Differentiate between `Period` and `CompoundPeriod` in Julia with an example.*

**Model Answer:**
A **`Period`** represents a single, semantically meaningful elapsed duration in calendar terms (e.g., `Day(5)`, `Month(2)`, `Hour(3)`). A **`CompoundPeriod`** is a **sum of multiple different period types** that Julia automatically groups when arithmetic produces mixed units.

**Example:**

```julia
julia> DateTime(2024,1,1,0,0,0) + Day(5) + Hour(3)
2024-01-06T03:00:00

julia> typeof(DateTime(2024,1,1) - DateTime(2023,12,15,21,0,0))
CompoundPeriod
```

The subtraction yields a `CompoundPeriod` like `15 days, 3 hours` because mixing `Day` and `Hour` triggers automatic grouping.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each — Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the various **constructor methods** for creating `Date` and `DateTime` objects in Julia, with examples. Discuss the role of the `now()` and `today()` functions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat example, demonstrate **date arithmetic using `Period` types**. Explain how Julia handles **month-end clamping** when adding `Month(1)` to a date like `Date(2024, 1, 31)`. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Constructors:**

| Constructor | Syntax | Example | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| Year, Month, Day args | `Date(y, m, d)` | `Date(2024, 8, 15)` | Explicit calendar input |
| ISO string | `Date("2024-08-15")` | `Date("2024-08-15")` | Parses ISO 8601 format |
| DateTime constructor | `DateTime(y, m, d, h, mi, s)` | `DateTime(2024, 8, 15, 14, 30, 0)` | With time components |
| ISO DateTime | `DateTime("2024-08-15T14:30:00")` | — | ISO string with `T` |
| `today()` | `today()` | `2024-08-15` | Current local date (Date type) |
| `now()` | `now()` | `2024-08-15T14:30:45.123` | Current local DateTime, ms precision |

`today()` returns a `Date` (no time component), while `now()` returns a `DateTime` with millisecond precision and the system's local time. Both are convenience constructors built atop the standard constructor.

**(b) Period arithmetic with month-end clamping:**

```julia
julia> d = Date(2024, 1, 31)
2024-01-31

julia> d + Month(1)
2024-02-29   # CLAMPED — Feb has only 29 days in 2024

julia> d + Day(1)
2024-02-01   # Normal day arithmetic

julia> d + Year(1)
2025-01-31   # Year is preserved exactly
```

**Explanation of month-end clamping**: Julia's calendar arithmetic follows the **"last day rule"** — when adding `Month(n)` to a date whose day-of-month exceeds the target month's maximum, the result is **clamped to the last valid day**. This avoids invalid dates like `February 31`. The same rule applies to `Date(2024, 3, 31) + Month(1) = 2024-04-30`.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative)

**Q4. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **`Dates.format`** function and the **`DateTime` parsing** mechanism in Julia. Provide a table of at least **six formatting tokens** with examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Julia program to compute the **number of days between two given dates**, the **day of the week**, and the **day of the year**. Use appropriate accessor functions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) `Dates.format` and Parsing:**

`Dates.format(dt, fmt_string)` converts a `Date`/`DateTime` into a formatted string, while `DateTime("string")` or `Date("string")` parses an ISO 8601 string back into a date object.

**Formatting token table:**

| Token | Meaning | Example Output |
|---|---|---|
| `yyyy` | 4-digit year | `2024` |
| `yy` | 2-digit year | `24` |
| `mm` | 2-digit month | `08` |
| `m` | month (no zero pad) | `8` |
| `dd` | 2-digit day | `05` |
| `d` | day (no zero pad) | `5` |
| `HH` | hour (24-h) | `14` |
| `MM` | minute | `30` |
| `SS` | second | `45` |
| `e` | short weekday | `Thu` |
| `E` | full weekday | `Thursday` |

```julia
julia> dt = DateTime(2024, 8, 15, 14, 30, 45);

julia> Dates.format(dt, "dd-mm-yyyy HH:MM")
"15-08-2024 14:30"

julia> Dates.format(dt, "E, d U yyyy")
"Thursday, 15 August 2024"
```

Parsing in reverse uses `DateTime("string", "format")` for non-ISO strings.

**(b) Program to compute days-between, day-of-week, day-of-year:**

```julia
using Dates

# Define two dates
d1 = Date(2024, 1, 1)
d2 = Date(2024, 8, 15)

# (i) Number of days between
delta = d2 - d1
num_days = Dates.value(delta)  # convert Period to integer
println("Days between: $num_days")

# (ii) Day of the week
dow = dayofweek(d2)
day_name = ["Mon","Tue","Wed","Thu","Fri","Sat","Sun"][dow]
println("Day of week: $day_name (numeric: $dow)")

# (iii) Day of the year
doy = dayofyear(d2)
println("Day of year: $doy")
```

**Expected output for `d2 = 2024-08-15`:**

```
Days between: 227
Day of week: Thu (numeric: 4)
Day of year: 228
```

(Recall 2024 is a leap year, hence 228.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Confusing `Date` and `DateTime`**: `Date` has NO time component; subtracting two `Date` objects returns `Day`, not `Millisecond`.
> 2. **Forgetting ISO 8601 order**: Julia uses `year-month-day`, NOT `month-day-year`. Writing `Date("08-15-2024")` will **throw an error**.
> 3. **`Dates.value()` is mandatory**: Just `dt2 - dt1` gives a `Period`, not a number. Students often write `dt2 - dt1` and expect an `Int`.
> 4. **Time zones**: The base `Dates` module does **NOT** support time zones. Mentioning `ZonedDateTime` requires `using TimeZones`.
> 5. **Month-end clamping surprise**: Forgetting that `Date(2024,1,31) + Month(1) = 2024-02-29`, not `2024-03-02`.
> 6. **Leap year in dayofyear**: Using 365 instead of 366 for leap years loses a mark.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **`Dates` module** is in Julia's **standard library** — no `Pkg.add` needed; just `using Dates`.
- **`Date`** = calendar day only; **`DateTime`** = instant with millisecond precision; **`Time`** = clock time only.
- Internal storage is **`Int64` milliseconds** since the proleptic epoch `0000-01-01T00:00:00`.
- Always use the **ISO 8601 string** `"yyyy-mm-dd"` for parsing — Julia rejects other formats by default.
- **`Period` types** (`Day`, `Month`, `Year`, `Hour`, `Minute`, `Second`, `Millisecond`) are **calendar-aware**.
- **`CompoundPeriod`** is auto-generated when arithmetic mixes period types.
- Use **`Dates.value(period)`** to extract the underlying integer (milliseconds for `DateTime` differences).
- **`dayofweek`** returns `1` for Monday, `7` for Sunday (ISO 8601 standard).
- **`isleapyear(y)`** returns `true` iff $(y \bmod 4 = 0) \land (y \bmod 100 \neq 0 \lor y \bmod 400 = 0)$.
- **Month-end clamping** occurs when `Month` arithmetic lands on a non-existent day (e.g., Feb 30).
- **`daysinmonth(dt)`** correctly handles leap years (29 for Feb in leap years, 28 otherwise).
- **`floor(dt, Day)`** truncates a `DateTime` to midnight; `ceil` rounds up; `round` rounds to nearest.
- Time zones require the **external package `TimeZones.jl`** — not in base.
- **1 day = 86,400,000 milliseconds** (always fixed, never varies).
- The **Unix epoch** (1970-01-01T00:00:00 UTC) corresponds to `DateTime` value **`-62135683200000`** ms relative to Julia's proleptic epoch.
- For business-day arithmetic: use **`Dates.tonext(d, dayofweek)`** and **`Dates.toprev`** to skip weekends.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Working with data frames
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET861 - DATA SCIENCE WITH JULIA
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Working with data frames

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — WORKING WITH DATA FRAMES

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> A **DataFrame** in Julia is a two-dimensional, labeled, mutable tabular data structure provided by the `DataFrames.jl` package. It is the canonical container for rectangular, heterogeneous datasets where each **column** is a `Vector` (with a single element type and a name) and each **row** is an observation indexed by an integer or custom index.

A `DataFrame` is best understood as an **in-memory spreadsheet on steroids**. Picture an Excel sheet:

- **Columns** in Excel → named vectors (each column has exactly one data type, e.g., `String`, `Float64`, `Int`).
- **Rows** in Excel → observations (one complete record per row).
- **Header row** in Excel → column names.

The difference is that Julia's `DataFrame` is **type-stable per column** (a column of age cannot accidentally contain the word "twenty"), it supports **vectorized operations** like R/Pandas, and it integrates seamlessly with the typed, fast Julia compiler.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Library Card Catalogue**
> Imagine a library's card catalogue. Each drawer is a **column** (Author, Title, Year, ISBN), and each card is a **row** (a single book). When you want every book published after 2010, you "filter rows" by sliding out cards based on the Year drawer. When you want to add a new column called "Genre", you simply insert a new drawer. A `DataFrame` in Julia behaves exactly like this catalogue — columns can be added, removed, renamed, and filtered independently of the rows.

### Why DataFrames matter in Data Science

| Feature | Engineering / Data-Science Utility |
|---|---|
| Heterogeneous columns | Real datasets mix numbers, text, dates, booleans |
| Labeled axes | Self-documenting code; no need to remember column indices |
| Vectorized ops | Fast bulk transformations without explicit loops |
| Missing-data support | Native `missing` type — no silent `NaN` confusion |
| Interop with CSV, Arrow, Parquet | I/O for the modern data lake ecosystem |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Installation Command (KTU Lab Setup)**
> ```julia
> using Pkg
> Pkg.add("DataFrames")
> using DataFrames
> ```
> In KTU lab examinations, **`using DataFrames`** is the first line of every script that touches tabular data.

### Construction — The Four Standard Entry Points

A `DataFrame` can be created from virtually any common Julia container. The four entry points every KTU student must memorize are shown below.

**1. From named keyword arguments (most common for small frames):**

```julia
df = DataFrame(Name = ["Anu", "Rahul", "Meera"],
               Age  = [21, 22, 20],
               GPA  = [8.9, 7.8, 9.2])
```

**2. From a `Dict` of equal-length vectors:**

```julia
d  = Dict("City" => ["Kochi", "Trivandrum", "Calicut"],
          "Pop"  => [677_381, 957_730, 609_224])
df = DataFrame(d)
```

**3. From a matrix (column types are auto-promoted to a common supertype):**

```julia
mat = [1 2 3;
       4 5 6;
       7 8 9]
df  = DataFrame(mat, :auto)              # default column names: x1, x2, x3
df2 = DataFrame(mat, ["A", "B", "C"])    # custom column names
```

**4. From an external file (CSV / TSV / Feather / Parquet):**

```julia
using CSV
df = CSV.read("students.csv", DataFrame)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **`Symbol` vs `String` Column Names**
> In Julia, `DataFrames` prefers **`Symbol`** column names internally (e.g., `:Name`, `:Age`). When you access a column, both `df.Name` and `df[!, :Name]` work, but the broadcasting form `df.Name` is idiomatic. Always quote a column name when it contains spaces: `df."Student Name"`.

### The Anatomy of a DataFrame

| Component | Type | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Column name | `Symbol` | `:Age` |
| Column data | `Vector{T}` | `[21, 22, 20]::Vector{Int64}` |
| Row index | `Vector{Int64}` (default) | `[1, 2, 3]` |
| Element | `Any` (column union type) | `21` |
| Whole table | `DataFrame` | `3×3 DataFrame` |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Check** — when you print a `DataFrame` in the REPL, Julia reports the schema at the bottom:
> ```
> 3 rows × 3 columns
> :Name  :Age  :GPA
> ```
> Reading this line tells you the **shape** and the **column dtypes** — examiners love this one-liner in viva.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Indexing Operators — `df.col`, `df[!, :col]`, `df[:, :col]`

KTU frequently tests the difference between **copy** and **view** semantics. Memorize the table below.

| Syntax | Returns | Mutates Original? | Memory |
|---|---|---|---|
| `df.col` | Column as **view** (alias) | ✅ Yes (write-back) | Shared |
| `df[!, :col]` | Column as **view** (alias) | ✅ Yes (write-back) | Shared |
| `df[:, :col]` | Column as **copy** | ❌ No | Independent |
| `df[!, [:c1, :c2]]` | New mini-DataFrame | ❌ No | Independent |
| `df[[1,3,5], :]` | New mini-DataFrame (rows) | ❌ No | Independent |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall** — Using `df[:, :Age]` in a hot loop copies the entire column every iteration. For element-wise updates, use `df[!, :Age]` to write back. Conversely, use `:` only when you intentionally want a copy (e.g., to free the original).

## 2.2 Selection — Picking Subsets of Columns

`select` keeps the specified columns (and may compute new ones); `select!` mutates in place.

$$
\text{select}(df, \text{cols}) \;\longrightarrow\; df_{\text{new}} \text{ with only the requested columns}
$$

```julia
select(df, :Name)                         # one column
select(df, [:Name, :GPA])                 # multiple columns
select(df, :Name => :StudentName)         # rename
select(df, :Age => x -> x .* 12)          # transform: months
select(df, :Age => ByRow(x -> x ≥ 21) => :IsAdult)
```

The general form is:

$$
\text{select}\!\left(df,\; \text{cols} \Rightarrow \text{new\_name},\; \text{operation}\right)
$$

where the operation may be:
- **Function on whole column**: `:Age => x -> x .* 12`
- **Row-wise function**: `:Age => ByRow(x -> x ≥ 21)`
- **Pair of multiple cols**: `[:A, :B] => (+) => :Sum`

## 2.3 Filtering — Row Subsetting

$$
df_{\text{filtered}} = \text{filter}(row \rightarrow \text{predicate}, df)
$$

```julia
filter(:Age => x -> x ≥ 21, df)                       # column predicate
filter(row -> row.GPA > 8.0, df)                      # row-wise predicate
subset(df, :Age => x -> x .≥ 21)                      # non-mutating, modern API
```

> [!NOTE]
> **`.≥` (dotted operator)** automatically broadcasts a scalar comparison across a vector — equivalent to `x -> x .>= 21`.

## 2.4 Sorting

$$
\text{sort}(df, \text{cols}; \text{rev} = \text{false})
$$

```julia
sort(df, :GPA)                       # ascending
sort(df, :GPA, rev = true)           # descending
sort(df, [:Dept, :GPA], rev = (false, true))   # mixed
```

## 2.5 Transformation — Column Arithmetic

`transform` and `transform!` add or replace columns while preserving row order and count.

$$
\text{transform}(df,\; :A \Rightarrow x \rightarrow x .- \text{mean}(x)) \longrightarrow df \cup \{:A_{\text{centered}}\}
$$

```julia
transform(df, :Age => (x -> x .- mean(x)) => :AgeCentered)
transform(df, [:Maths, :Physics] => (+) => :TotalScore)
```

## 2.6 Group-By / Split-Apply-Combine

The **`groupby`** + **`combine`** pair is Julia's analog of SQL's `GROUP BY`.

$$
\text{combine}(\text{groupby}(df, :key), \; :val \Rightarrow \text{agg}) \;\longrightarrow\; \text{aggregated}\; df
$$

```julia
gdf = groupby(df, :Department)
result = combine(gdf, :Salary => mean => :AvgSalary,
                       :Salary => length => :HeadCount)
```

The general mathematical signature of `combine`:

$$
\text{combine}(G, \; v_1 \Rightarrow \text{agg}_1 \Rightarrow n_1,\; v_2 \Rightarrow \text{agg}_2 \Rightarrow n_2)
$$

where $v_i$ are source columns and $n_i$ are the resulting column names.

## 2.7 Joins — Merging Two DataFrames

| Join Type | Symbol | Resulting Rows |
|---|---|---|
| Inner | `innerjoin` | Only matching keys (intersection) |
| Left  | `leftjoin`  | All left + matches from right |
| Right | `rightjoin` | All right + matches from left |
| Outer | `outerjoin` | Union of both sides, `missing` where no match |
| Semi  | `semijoin`  | Left rows **with** a match (no right columns) |
| Anti  | `antijoin`  | Left rows **without** a match |

Formally, for sets $L$ and $R$ of keys:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Inner}(L,R) &= L \cap R \\
\text{Left}(L,R)  &= L \\
\text{Right}(L,R) &= R \\
\text{Outer}(L,R) &= L \cup R \\
\text{Semi}(L,R)  &= L \cap R \quad (\text{left side only}) \\
\text{Anti}(L,R)  &= L \setminus R
\end{aligned}
$$

```julia
leftjoin(df1, df2, on = :ID)
innerjoin(df1, df2, on = [:Dept, :Year])     # compound key
```

## 2.8 Missing Data — The `missing` Type

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Julia has **three** null-like sentinels: `nothing`, `missing`, and `NaN`. **`DataFrames` exclusively uses `missing`** to denote absent values — never `NaN`. This is a frequent viva question.

| Function | Behavior |
|---|---|
| `ismissing(x)` | Returns `Bool`, true if `x === missing` |
| `coalesce(x, default)` | Returns `x` if non-missing, else `default` |
| `dropmissing(df)` | Drops any row containing at least one `missing` |
| `dropmissing(df, :col)` | Drops only if the column is `missing` |
| `allowmissing!(df, :col)` | Permits the column to hold `missing` |
| `disallowmissing!(df, :col)` | Forbids `missing`, replaces with type-appropriate default |
| `passmissing(f, x)` | `f(x)` but propagates `missing` instead of throwing |

## 2.9 Reshaping — Wide ↔ Long

| Function | Direction | Use-Case |
|---|---|---|
| `stack(df, ...)` | Wide → Long | Tidy data for plotting |
| `unstack(df, :key, :value)` | Long → Wide | Pivot / cross-tabulation |

**Tidy-Data Rule (Hadley / Wickham):**
$$
\text{Each variable} \rightarrow \text{column}, \quad \text{each observation} \rightarrow \text{row}, \quad \text{each value} \rightarrow \text{cell}.
$$

## 2.10 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> The table below is a **print-and-pin** reference for the KTU lab examination and the ESE module test. Every entry is fair game for either a 3-mark or 14-mark question.

| Operation | Function | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Create | `DataFrame(kwargs)` | `DataFrame(A=1:3, B=["x","y","z"])` |
| Read | `CSV.read(path, DataFrame)` | `CSV.read("a.csv", DataFrame)` |
| Write | `CSV.write(path, df)` | `CSV.write("out.csv", df)` |
| Shape | `size(df)` | `(nrows, ncols)` |
| Column names | `names(df)` | `[:A, :B, :C]` |
| Column types | `eltype.(eachcol(df))` | `[Int64, String]` |
| First *n* rows | `first(df, n)` | `first(df, 5)` |
| Last *n* rows | `last(df, n)` | `last(df, 5)` |
| Describe | `describe(df)` | mean, min, max, nmissing, eltype |
| Select cols | `select(df, cols)` | `select(df, :A)` |
| Mutate select | `select!(df, cols)` | `select!(df, :A)` |
| Add/replace cols | `transform(df, ...)` | `transform(df, :A => x->x.^2)` |
| Filter rows | `filter(pred, df)` | `filter(:A => >(5), df)` |
| Sort | `sort(df, cols; rev)` | `sort(df, :A, rev=true)` |
| Group | `groupby(df, :key)` | `groupby(df, :Dept)` |
| Aggregate | `combine(gdf, ...)` | `combine(gdf, :X => mean)` |
| Join | `leftjoin/innerjoin/...` | `leftjoin(a, b, on=:ID)` |
| Reshape wide→long | `stack(df, cols)` | `stack(df, [:M,:P])` |
| Reshape long→wide | `unstack(df, key, val)` | `unstack(df, :k, :v)` |
| Drop missing | `dropmissing(df)` | `dropmissing(df)` |
| Replace missing | `coalesce.(df.col, 0)` | `coalesce.(df.Age, 0)` |
| Unique rows | `unique(df)` | `unique(df, :ID)` |
| Push row | `push!(df, row)` | `push!(df, (1,"a"))` |
| Delete row | `delete!(df, i)` | `delete!(df, 3)` |
| Concatenate | `vcat(df1, df2)` / `hcat` | `vcat(df1, df2)` |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | DataFrame Task |
|---|---|
| Civil Engineering | Strain-gauge time-series → `groupby(:Sensor)` → mean deflection |
| Electrical Engineering | Load-shedding schedule → `innerjoin(:FeederID)` for redundancy check |
| Computer Science | Log analytics → `combine(:Latency => median)` per service |
| Biomedical | Patient vitals → `dropmissing` → `transform(:BP => zscore)` |
| Financial | Portfolio PnL → `leftjoin(:Ticker)` to attach benchmark |
