- **Adjusted $R^2$:** $\bar{R}^2 = 1 - (1-R^2)\dfrac{n-1}{n-p-1}$
- **F-statistic** for overall significance: $F = \dfrac{(SS_{tot} - SS_{res})/p}{SS_{res}/(n-p-1)}$

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **high-yield formulas** that the KTU 2024 examiner community expects candidates to reproduce *without* derivation marks lost.

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Time Series Decomposition | $Y_t = T_t + S_t + C_t + \varepsilon_t$ | Additive form |
| 2 | Multiplicative Form | $Y_t = T_t \cdot S_t \cdot C_t \cdot \varepsilon_t$ | Use when variance grows with level |
| 3 | Moving Average (order $m$) | $MA_t = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{j=0}^{m-1} Y_{t-j}$ | Smooths out seasonality when $m=$ period |
| 4 | Single Exponential Smoothing | $S_t = \alpha Y_t + (1-\alpha)S_{t-1}$ | $0 < \alpha < 1$ |
| 5 | Double Exponential (Holt) | $L_t = \alpha Y_t + (1-\alpha)(L_{t-1}+T_{t-1})$; $T_t = \beta(L_t - L_{t-1}) + (1-\beta)T_{t-1}$ | Captures trend |
| 6 | Autocovariance | $\gamma_k = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=k+1}^{n}(Y_t-\bar{Y})(Y_{t-k}-\bar{Y})$ | Lag $k$ |
| 7 | Autocorrelation (ACF) | $\rho_k = \gamma_k / \gamma_0$ | Bounded in $[-1, 1]$ |
| 8 | Partial ACF (PACF) | $\rho_k^{partial}$ via Durbin-Levinson | Determines AR order |
| 9 | OLS Estimator | $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{y}$ | Requires full rank $\mathbf{X}$ |
| 10 | Predicted Response | $\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \mathbf{X}\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = \mathbf{H}\mathbf{y}$ | $\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^\top$ is the *hat matrix* |
| 11 | Residual Sum of Squares | $RSS = \sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Minimisation target |
| 12 | Total Sum of Squares | $TSS = \sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2$ | Benchmark |
| 13 | $R^2$ | $R^2 = 1 - RSS/TSS$ | Proportion of explained variance |
| 14 | Adjusted $R^2$ | $\bar{R}^2 = 1 - (1-R^2)(n-1)/(n-p-1)$ | Penalises extra predictors |
| 15 | MSE Loss | $J(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \frac{1}{n}\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Used in gradient descent |
| 16 | Gradient Descent Update | $\beta_j \leftarrow \beta_j - \alpha \dfrac{\partial J}{\partial \beta_j}$ | $\alpha$ is learning rate |
| 17 | Ridge Penalty | $\lambda \sum \beta_j^2$ | L2 regularisation |
| 18 | Lasso Penalty | $\lambda \sum \vert \beta_j \vert$ | L1 regularisation, induces sparsity |
| 19 | ARIMA(p,d,q) | $\phi(B)(1-B)^d Y_t = \theta(B)\varepsilon_t$ | $B$ is backshift operator |
| 20 | Ljung-Box Statistic | $Q(m) = n(n+2)\sum_{k=1}^{m}\frac{\hat{\rho}_k^2}{n-k}$ | White-noise diagnostic |

### Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Time Series:** Used in *load forecasting* for power grids (Kerala State Electricity Board), *weather prediction* (IMD), *stock-market* algorithmic trading, *ECG* anomaly detection, and *IoT sensor* monitoring.
- **Regression Analysis:** Used in *structural health monitoring* (predicting bridge stress from vibration data), *process control* in chemical plants, *demand forecasting* in supply chains, and *predictive maintenance* of turbo-generators.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the OLS Estimator

We derive $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$ that minimises the **Residual Sum of Squares**.

### Step 1 — Define the Loss Function

$$RSS(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\bigl(y_i - \mathbf{x}_i^\top \boldsymbol{\beta}\bigr)^2$$

where $\mathbf{x}_i = [1, x_{i1}, x_{i2}, \ldots, x_{ip}]^\top$ is the augmented feature vector (with the leading 1 absorbing the intercept $\beta_0$).

### Step 2 — Rewrite in Matrix Form

$$\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} = \begin{bmatrix} y_1 - \mathbf{x}_1^\top\boldsymbol{\beta} \\ y_2 - \mathbf{x}_2^\top\boldsymbol{\beta} \\ \vdots \\ y_n - \mathbf{x}_n^\top\boldsymbol{\beta} \end{bmatrix}$$

So the scalar loss becomes:

$$RSS(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = (\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta})^\top(\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta})$$

### Step 3 — Expand the Quadratic Form

$$RSS = \mathbf{y}^\top\mathbf{y} - 2\boldsymbol{\beta}^\top\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{y} + \boldsymbol{\beta}^\top\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}$$

Explanation: The cross term expands to $-\mathbf{y}^\top\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} - \boldsymbol{\beta}^\top\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{y} = -2\boldsymbol{\beta}^\top\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{y}$ because both terms are scalar (transpose of a scalar is itself) and equal.

### Step 4 — Take the Gradient with Respect to $\boldsymbol{\beta}$

$$\frac{\partial\, RSS}{\partial \boldsymbol{\beta}} = -2\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{y} + 2\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}$$

We used the matrix calculus identities:
$\dfrac{\partial}{\partial\boldsymbol{\beta}}(\mathbf{a}^\top\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \mathbf{a}$ and $\dfrac{\partial}{\partial\boldsymbol{\beta}}(\boldsymbol{\beta}^\top\mathbf{A}\boldsymbol{\beta}) = 2\mathbf{A}\boldsymbol{\beta}$ (for symmetric $\mathbf{A}$, which $\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X}$ is).

### Step 5 — Set the Gradient to Zero (First-Order Optimality)

$$-2\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{y} + 2\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X}\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = \mathbf{0}$$

### Step 6 — Solve for $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$

$$\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X}\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = \mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{y}$$

Premultiplying both sides by $(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}$ (which exists when $\mathbf{X}$ has full column rank):

$$\boxed{\;\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_{\text{OLS}} = (\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{y}\;}$$

### Step 7 — Verify it's a Minimum (Second-Order Condition)

$$\frac{\partial^2 RSS}{\partial \boldsymbol{\beta}\,\partial \boldsymbol{\beta}^\top} = 2\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X}$$

Since $\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X}$ is **positive semi-definite** (and positive *definite* when $\mathbf{X}$ has full rank), $RSS$ is strictly convex in $\boldsymbol{\beta}$, confirming the stationary point is the *unique global minimum*.

> [!NOTE]
> The estimator is **unbiased**: $E[\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}] = \boldsymbol{\beta}$ provided the Gauss-Markov assumptions hold (linearity, strict exogeneity, no perfect multicollinearity, homoscedasticity, no autocorrelation).

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Coefficient of Determination $R^2$

### Step 1 — Define Sums of Squares

$$SS_{tot} = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \bar{y})^2, \quad SS_{res} = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2, \quad SS_{reg} = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(\hat{y}_i - \bar{y})^2$$

### Step 2 — Establish the Pythagorean Identity

A well-known algebraic identity (verifiable by direct expansion) is:

$$SS_{tot} = SS_{reg} + SS_{res}$$

### Step 3 — Form the Ratio

$$R^2 \equiv \frac{SS_{reg}}{SS_{tot}} = 1 - \frac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}}$$

### Step 4 — Bounds
- $R^2 = 1 \Leftrightarrow SS_{res} = 0$ (perfect fit).
- $R^2 = 0 \Leftrightarrow \hat{y}_i = \bar{y}$ for all $i$ (model no better than the mean).
- $R^2$ can be **negative** if the model is *worse* than the mean predictor (unusual but possible on out-of-sample test data).

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Single Exponential Smoothing Forecast

Given a smoothing constant $0 < \alpha < 1$, we want a recursive forecast $S_t$ for $Y_t$ such that past errors are exponentially decayed.

### Step 1 — Define the Recursion

$$S_t = \alpha Y_t + (1-\alpha)S_{t-1}$$

### Step 2 — Unroll the Recursion Back to $t = 0$

$$S_t = \alpha Y_t + (1-\alpha)\bigl[\alpha Y_{t-1} + (1-\alpha)S_{t-2}\bigr]$$

$$S_t = \alpha Y_t + \alpha(1-\alpha) Y_{t-1} + (1-\alpha)^2 S_{t-2}$$

Continuing this unrolling $k$ times:

$$S_t = \alpha\sum_{j=0}^{k-1}(1-\alpha)^j Y_{t-j} + (1-\alpha)^k S_{t-k}$$

### Step 3 — Take the Limit as $k \to \infty$

Since $0 < \alpha < 1$, the term $(1-\alpha)^k \to 0$, and the geometric series $\sum_{j=0}^{\infty}(1-\alpha)^j = \frac{1}{\alpha}$:

$$\boxed{\;S_t = \sum_{j=0}^{\infty} \alpha(1-\alpha)^j Y_{t-j}\;}$$

**Interpretation:** $S_t$ is a **weighted moving average** where the weight on $Y_{t-j}$ is $\alpha(1-\alpha)^j$, an exponentially decaying kernel. Recent observations receive more weight; very old observations contribute negligibly.

### Step 4 — Mean-Squared Error Minimisation (Optimal $\alpha$)

Differentiating $E[(Y_{t+1}-S_t)^2]$ with respect to $\alpha$ and setting to zero yields a transcendental equation:

$$\frac{d}{d\alpha}E[(Y_{t+1}-S_t)^2] = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{solved numerically by grid search in practice}$$

> [!TIP]
> In Julia, the `TimeSeries.jl` and `StateSpaceModels.jl` packages can perform this numerical optimisation automatically. The `Optim.jl` package's `optimize(...)` function with a custom loss is a typical workflow.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of the AR(1) Stationarity Condition

Consider the simplest AR model:

$$Y_t = \phi Y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t, \quad \varepsilon_t \sim WN(0, \sigma^2)$$

### Step 1 — Compute the Variance of Both Sides

$$\text{Var}(Y_t) = \phi^2 \text{Var}(Y_{t-1}) + \text{Var}(\varepsilon_t) = \phi^2 \gamma_0 + \sigma^2$$

### Step 2 — Apply Stationarity (Constant Variance)

Under stationarity, $\gamma_0 = \text{Var}(Y_t) = \text{Var}(Y_{t-1})$, so:

$$\gamma_0 = \phi^2 \gamma_0 + \sigma^2 \;\Longrightarrow\; \gamma_0(1-\phi^2) = \sigma^2 \;\Longrightarrow\; \gamma_0 = \frac{\sigma^2}{1-\phi^2}$$

### Step 3 — Existence Condition

The variance is **finite and positive** if and only if:

$$\boxed{\;\vert \phi \vert < 1\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **stationarity condition** for AR(1). When $\vert \phi \vert \geq 1$, the series is *non-stationary* (explosive or unit-root behaviour), and standard OLS inference is invalid.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Julia Packages for This Module

| Package | Purpose | Key Functions |
|---|---|---|
| `TimeSeries.jl` | Time-indexed data structures | `TimeArray`, `timestamp`, `values` |
| `TimeSeriesTools.jl` | Decomposition, smoothing | `decompose`, `moving_average` |
| `StateSpaceModels.jl` | ARIMA, exponential smoothing | `SARIMA`, `fit`, `forecast` |
| `GLM.jl` | Generalised linear models (incl. OLS) | `lm`, `glm`, `@formula` |
| `MLJ.jl` | Unified ML framework | `@load_model`, `machine`, `fit!` |
| `MLBase.jl` | Preprocessing, cross-validation | `train_test_split`, `cross_val_score` |
| `Statistics` (stdlib) | Descriptive statistics | `mean`, `median`, `cor`, `cov` |
| `DataFrames.jl` | Tabular data handling | `DataFrame`, `select`, `describe` |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Time Series Analysis Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Time Series Data Yt] --> B[Exploratory Analysis]
    B --> B1[Plot Series Yt vs t]
    B --> B2[Compute ACF and PACF]
    B --> C[Stationarity Tests]
    C --> C1[ADF Test]
    C --> C2[KPSS Test]
    C --> D{Is Series Stationary?}
    D -->|No| E[Differencing d times]
    E --> D
    D -->|Yes| F[Decomposition]
    F --> F1[Estimate Trend Tt]
    F --> F2[Estimate Seasonality St]
    F --> F3[Extract Residual epsilon]
    F3 --> G[Model Selection]
    G --> G1[ACF Cutoff suggests MA q]
    G --> G2[PACF Cutoff suggests AR p]
    G --> H[Fit ARIMA p d q Model]
    H --> I[Diagnostic Checks]
    I --> I1[Ljung Box on Residuals]
    I --> I2[Normality of Residuals]
    I --> J{Model Adequate?}
    J -->|No| G
    J -->|Yes| K[Forecast Future Values]
    K --> L[Confidence Intervals]

    style A fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F77B4,color:#000
    style L fill:#D5F5E3,stroke:#2ECC71,color:#000
    style D fill:#FFF3CD,stroke:#F39C12,color:#000
    style J fill:#FFF3CD,stroke:#F39C12,color:#000
```

**Figure 4.1 — Functional architecture of a classical time series forecasting pipeline. The two decision diamonds (stationarity and model adequacy) gate the iterative loop, reflecting the workflow implemented in `StateSpaceModels.jl` and `TimeSeriesTools.jl`.**

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set A]`
**Differentiate between the trend, seasonal, and cyclical components of a time series. Give one real-world example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — full marks allocation):**

A time series $Y_t$ can be decomposed as $Y_t = T_t + S_t + C_t + \varepsilon_t$.

- **Trend ($T_t$):** The smooth, long-term directional movement of the series, reflecting gradual growth or decline. **(1 mark)**
  *Example:* The steady year-on-year increase in India's GDP over the last two decades. **(½ mark)**
- **Seasonal ($S_t$):** A regular, periodic fluctuation tied to a *fixed calendar period*. **(1 mark)**
  *Example:* The monthly surge in air-conditioner sales every April–June in Kerala. **(½ mark)**
- **Cyclical ($C_t$):** Non-periodic, longer-wave oscillations typically driven by macroeconomic forces; period length is *variable and unknown*. **(1 mark — note: students often confuse cyclical with seasonal; explicitly stating the variable period is the key discriminator.)**

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]`
**State the Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) estimator for the coefficient vector $\boldsymbol{\beta}$ in linear regression. Under what condition on $\mathbf{X}$ does the estimator exist?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The OLS estimator that minimises the residual sum of squares $RSS(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = (\mathbf{y}-\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta})^\top(\mathbf{y}-\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta})$ is:

$$\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_{OLS} = (\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{y}$$

**(2 marks)** for the formula with correct notation. The estimator exists if and only if $\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X}$ is *invertible*, which is equivalent to $\mathbf{X}$ having **full column rank** (i.e., no perfect multicollinearity, no redundant features, $n > p$). **(1 mark)** for the condition.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### Question B-A `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Regular – Module 3 Choice 1]`

**(a)** Explain the classical additive decomposition model of a time series. With a labelled diagram, describe how trend, seasonality, cyclicity, and irregular components are extracted. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the OLS estimator $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$ for a multiple linear regression model from first principles. Clearly state all matrix calculus identities used. Also compute the predicted response and the residual vector. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for B-A(a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Decomposition equation (1 mark):**
The classical *additive* decomposition expresses a time series as the sum of four orthogonal components:
$$Y_t = T_t + S_t + C_t + \varepsilon_t$$
where $Y_t$ is the observed value at time $t$, $T_t$ is the trend, $S_t$ is the seasonal, $C_t$ is the cyclical, and $\varepsilon_t$ is the irregular (white-noise) component.

**Step 2 — Trend extraction (2 marks):**
The trend is estimated using a **centred moving average** of order equal to the seasonal period $m$:
$$\hat{T}_t = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{j=-(m-1)/2}^{(m-1)/2} Y_{t+j} \quad (m \text{ odd})$$
For even $m$, a 2×m centred moving average is used to ensure alignment with the original time index. Smoothing with this filter eliminates seasonal variation (since it spans exactly one seasonal cycle) and short-term noise, leaving the long-term trend.

**Step 3 — Seasonal extraction (2 marks):**
The detrended series is $D_t = Y_t - \hat{T}_t$. The seasonal index for season $j$ is the average of $D_t$ over all occurrences of season $j$, normalised so that seasonal indices sum to zero:
$$S_j = \frac{1}{n_j}\sum_{t:\,\text{season}(t)=j} D_t \quad\text{with}\quad \sum_{j=1}^{m} S_j = 0$$
Then $\hat{S}_t = S_{\text{season}(t)}$.

**Step 4 — Cyclical and irregular extraction (1 mark):**
The cyclical component is isolated by smoothing the detrended, deseasonalised series with a long moving average. The irregular component is the final residual:
$$\hat{\varepsilon}_t = Y_t - \hat{T}_t - \hat{S}_t - \hat{C}_t$$
and should ideally be white noise (validated by Ljung-Box test).

**Step 5 — Labelled diagram (1 mark):**
A clear block diagram showing $Y_t$ splitting into $T_t$, $S_t$, $C_t$, $\varepsilon_t$, with arrows indicating the additive combination, earns the remaining mark. (Reference: Figure 4.1 in Section 4.)

---

#### Model Solution for B-A(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Model and loss (1 mark):**
For the multiple linear regression model $\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$, the OLS objective is:
$$RSS(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \|\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}\|^2 = (\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta})^\top(\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta})$$

**Step 2 — Expansion (1 mark):**
$$RSS = \mathbf{y}^\top\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{y}^\top\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} - \boldsymbol{\beta}^\top\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{y} + \boldsymbol{\beta}^\top\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}$$
$$= \mathbf{y}^\top\mathbf{y} - 2\boldsymbol{\beta}^\top\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{y} + \boldsymbol{\beta}^\top\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}$$
since the two cross terms are scalars and equal.

**Step 3 — Gradient (1.5 marks):**
Using the identities (state them explicitly for ½ mark each):
- $\dfrac{\partial(\mathbf{a}^\top\boldsymbol{\beta})}{\partial\boldsymbol{\beta}} = \mathbf{a}$
- $\dfrac{\partial(\boldsymbol{\beta}^\top\mathbf{A}\boldsymbol{\beta})}{\partial\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{A} + \mathbf{A}^\top)\boldsymbol{\beta} = 2\mathbf{A}\boldsymbol{\beta}$ for symmetric $\mathbf{A}$.

Therefore:
$$\frac{\partial RSS}{\partial\boldsymbol{\beta}} = -2\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{y} + 2\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}$$

**Step 4 — Optimality condition (1.5 marks):**
Setting the gradient to zero:
$$-2\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{y} + 2\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X}\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = \mathbf{0} \;\Longrightarrow\; \mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X}\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = \mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{y}$$
Premultiplying by $(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}$ (which exists if $\mathbf{X}$ has full column rank):
$$\boxed{\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{y}}$$

**Step 5 — Predictions and residuals (1 mark):**
The predicted response vector and residual vector are:
$$\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \mathbf{X}\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = \mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{H}\mathbf{y}$$
$$\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{y} - \hat{\mathbf{y}} = (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H})\mathbf{y}$$
where $\mathbf{H}$ is the *hat matrix* that projects $\mathbf{y}$ onto the column space of $\mathbf{X}$. **½ mark** for the hat matrix identification, **½ mark** for the residual formula.

**Step 6 — Verification of minimum (1 mark):**
$$\frac{\partial^2 RSS}{\partial\boldsymbol{\beta}\partial\boldsymbol{\beta}^\top} = 2\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X}$$
Since $\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X}$ is positive definite (full-rank $\mathbf{X}$), the Hessian is positive definite, confirming the stationary point is a *global minimum*.

---

### Question B-B `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Regular – Module 3 Choice 2]`

**(a)** What is Exponential Smoothing? Derive the forecast equation for single exponential smoothing and show that it produces a weighted moving average with exponentially decaying weights. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a regression dataset with $n=5$ observations and a single predictor $x$, the design matrix and response are given below. Compute the OLS estimates $\hat{\beta}_0$ and $\hat{\beta}_1$ using the normal equations.

| $i$ | $x_i$ | $y_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | 2 |
| 2 | 2 | 3 |
| 3 | 3 | 5 |
| 4 | 4 | 4 |
| 5 | 5 | 6 |

**(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for B-B(a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Definition (1 mark):**
**Exponential Smoothing** is a forecasting technique that assigns exponentially decreasing weights to past observations, giving more importance to recent data. It is a recursive scheme with no requirement to store the entire history.

**Step 2 — Recursion (1 mark):**
For smoothing constant $\alpha \in (0,1)$:
$$S_t = \alpha Y_t + (1-\alpha)S_{t-1}, \quad S_0 = Y_1$$
The forecast for $Y_{t+1}$ is $\hat{Y}_{t+1} = S_t$.

**Step 3 — Unrolling the recursion (2 marks):**
Substituting the recursion into itself:
$$S_t = \alpha Y_t + (1-\alpha)\bigl[\alpha Y_{t-1} + (1-\alpha)S_{t-2}\bigr] = \alpha Y_t + \alpha(1-\alpha)Y_{t-1} + (1-\alpha)^2 S_{t-2}$$
Continuing $k$ steps:
$$S_t = \alpha\sum_{j=0}^{k-1}(1-\alpha)^j Y_{t-j} + (1-\alpha)^k S_{t-k}$$

**Step 4 — Limit as $k \to \infty$ (2 marks):**
Since $0 < (1-\alpha) < 1$, $(1-\alpha)^k \to 0$ as $k \to \infty$. The geometric series converges:
$$\sum_{j=0}^{\infty}(1-\alpha)^j = \frac{1}{1-(1-\alpha)} = \frac{1}{\alpha}$$
Therefore:
$$\boxed{S_t = \sum_{j=0}^{\infty}\alpha(1-\alpha)^j Y_{t-j}}$$
The weight on $Y_{t-j}$ is $w_j = \alpha(1-\alpha)^j$, which decays exponentially with age $j$. The sum of all weights is $\sum_{j=0}^{\infty}w_j = \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{\alpha} = 1$, so the weights form a valid probability distribution. **(1 mark)** for verifying the weights sum to 1.

**Step 5 — Interpretation (optional ½ mark):**
Larger $\alpha$ → faster decay → more reactive to recent changes (good for non-stationary series). Smaller $\alpha$ → slower decay → smoother (good for noisy stationary series).

---

#### Model Solution for B-B(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Construct the design matrix (1 mark):**
$$\mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \\ 1 & 3 \\ 1 & 4 \\ 1 & 5 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{y} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 3 \\ 5 \\ 4 \\ 6 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the required cross-products (2 marks):**
- $n = 5$
- $\sum x_i = 1+2+3+4+5 = 15$
- $\sum y_i = 2+3+5+4+6 = 20$
- $\sum x_i^2 = 1+4+9+16+25 = 55$
- $\sum x_i y_i = 1\cdot 2 + 2\cdot 3 + 3\cdot 5 + 4\cdot 4 + 5\cdot 6 = 2+6+15+16+30 = 69$

**Step 3 — Means (0.5 mark):**
- $\bar{x} = 15/5 = 3$
- $\bar{y} = 20/5 = 4$

**Step 4 — Slope estimate (1.5 marks):**
For simple linear regression (one predictor), the OLS slope is:
$$\hat{\beta}_1 = \frac{\sum(x_i-\bar{x})(y_i-\bar{y})}{\sum(x_i-\bar{x})^2} = \frac{\sum x_i y_i - n\bar{x}\bar{y}}{\sum x_i^2 - n\bar{x}^2}$$
Numerator: $69 - 5(3)(4) = 69 - 60 = 9$
Denominator: $55 - 5(3)^2 = 55 - 45 = 10$
$$\hat{\beta}_1 = \frac{9}{10} = 0.9$$

**Step 5 — Intercept estimate (1 mark):**
$$\hat{\beta}_0 = \bar{y} - \hat{\beta}_1 \bar{x} = 4 - (0.9)(3) = 4 - 2.7 = 1.3$$

**Step 6 — Fitted model and verification (1 mark):**
The fitted regression line is:
$$\hat{y} = 1.3 + 0.9 x$$
Verification using the normal equations $(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = \mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{y}$:
$$\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 15 \\ 15 & 55 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{y} = \begin{bmatrix} 20 \\ 69 \end{bmatrix}$$
Solving:
$$5\hat{\beta}_0 + 15\hat{\beta}_1 = 20 \;\Rightarrow\; \hat{\beta}_0 + 3\hat{\beta}_1 = 4 \;\Rightarrow\; \hat{\beta}_0 = 4 - 3(0.9) = 1.3 \;\checkmark$$
$$15\hat{\beta}_0 + 55\hat{\beta}_1 = 69 \;\Rightarrow\; 15(1.3) + 55(0.9) = 19.5 + 49.5 = 69 \;\checkmark$$
Both equations are satisfied, confirming the solution. **$R^2$** calculation: $SS_{reg} = \hat{\beta}_1^2 \sum(x_i-\bar{x})^2 = (0.9)^2(10) = 8.1$; $SS_{tot} = \sum(y_i-\bar{y})^2 = (2-4)^2+(3-4)^2+(5-4)^2+(4-4)^2+(6-4)^2 = 4+1+1+0+4 = 10$; $R^2 = 8.1/10 = 0.81$, so the model explains 81% of the variance.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing cyclical with seasonal:** Cyclical has *no fixed period*; seasonal is *calendar-bound*. Losing ½–1 mark per occurrence.
> 2. **Forgetting the constant column in $\mathbf{X}$:** Many students omit the leading 1's when writing the design matrix for OLS, leading to an incorrect normal equation and a wrong $\hat{\beta}_0$. **1 mark deduction.**
> 3. **Not stating the invertibility condition:** Writing $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{y}$ without mentioning that $\mathbf{X}$ must have *full column rank* is incomplete. **½ mark deduction.**
> 4. **Mixing up $R^2$ formulas:** $R^2 = 1 - SS_{res}/SS_{tot}$ is the universally correct form. The alternative $R^2 = (\text{Cor}(x,y))^2$ holds *only* for *simple* (one-predictor) linear regression.
> 5. **Skipping the verification step in numerical problems:** Examiners allocate explicit marks for the verification/normal-equation consistency check. Skipping it can cost 1 mark even when the answer is correct.
> 6. **Using Python code in Julia-based answers:** For PEIET861, all code snippets *must* be in Julia. Python loses 2 marks outright.
> 7. **Failing to mention Julia packages:** Naming `GLM.jl`, `TimeSeries.jl`, `StateSpaceModels.jl` shows contextual awareness and earns ½ bonus mark in the examiner's perception (and in some questions, explicit package-use marks).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (high-density summary for the night before the exam):**

- **Time Series Decomposition:** $Y_t = T_t + S_t + C_t + \varepsilon_t$ (additive) or $Y_t = T_t \cdot S_t \cdot C_t \cdot \varepsilon_t$ (multiplicative). Use multiplicative when variance grows with level.
- **Trend extraction:** centred moving average of order $m$ = seasonal period. Use 2×m for even $m$.
- **Seasonal extraction:** average detrended values for each season, then normalise to sum to zero.
- **Stationarity:** required for ARMA modelling. Test with Augmented Dickey-Fuller (ADF) and KPSS. Achieve via differencing.
- **ARIMA(p, d, q):** AR order $p$ from PACF cutoff; MA order $q$ from ACF cutoff; $d$ = differencing order.
- **Ljung-Box test:** validates white-noise residuals; $Q(m) = n(n+2)\sum_{k=1}^{m}\hat{\rho}_k^2/(n-k)$.
- **Exponential smoothing:** $S_t = \alpha Y_t + (1-\alpha)S_{t-1}$. Weights $\alpha(1-\alpha)^j$ sum to 1.
- **OLS estimator:** $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{y}$. Requires $\mathbf{X}$ to have full column rank.
- **Normal equations:** $\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X}\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = \mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{y}$. Always state this before solving.
- **Design matrix $\mathbf{X}$:** must include a leading column of 1's to estimate the intercept $\beta_0$.
- **Hat matrix:** $\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^\top$ — projects $\mathbf{y}$ onto column space of $\mathbf{X}$.
- **Residuals:** $\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{y} - \hat{\mathbf{y}} = (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H})\mathbf{y}$. Sum of residuals is exactly zero for OLS with intercept.
- **$R^2$:** $1 - SS_{res}/SS_{tot}$. Can be negative on out-of-sample test data.
- **Adjusted $R^2$:** $\bar{R}^2 = 1 - (1-R^2)(n-1)/(n-p-1)$. Always $\leq R^2$.
- **Gauss-Markov theorem:** OLS is BLUE (Best Linear Unbiased Estimator) under linearity, exogeneity, no multicollinearity, homoscedasticity, no autocorrelation.
- **Simple linear regression shortcuts:** $\hat{\beta}_1 = \dfrac{\sum(x_i-\bar{x})(y_i-\bar{y})}{\sum(x_i-\bar{x})^2}$, $\hat{\beta}_0 = \bar{y} - \hat{\beta}_1 \bar{x}$.
- **Julia packages for this module:** `TimeSeries.jl`, `TimeSeriesTools.jl`, `StateSpaceModels.jl`, `GLM.jl`, `MLJ.jl`, `DataFrames.jl`, `Statistics` (stdlib), `Optim.jl`.
- **Key Julia syntax for OLS:** `using GLM, DataFrames, CSV; model = lm(@formula(y ~ x1 + x2), data); coeftable(model)` — **memorise this line.**
- **Arima(1,0,0) stationarity condition:** $\vert \phi \vert < 1$. Outside this range, the series is *non-stationary*.
- **Common 3-mark question traps:** distinguish cyclical vs seasonal; explain why $R^2$ can be negative; state the role of the hat matrix; mention Julia packages by name.
- **Common 14-mark question traps:** derive OLS *from first principles* (not just state the formula); explicitly mention matrix calculus identities; verify final answer with normal equations; provide a labelled diagram for decomposition.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Day Strategy:** In Part A, write *one crisp sentence* per component and *one example*. In Part B (14 marks), allocate roughly 8 minutes per sub-part. Always include a labelled diagram wherever the question allows (decomposition, pipeline, model architecture) — it independently earns 1 mark and signals conceptual clarity to the examiner.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET861 - DATA SCIENCE WITH JULIA
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Classification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Classification — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Tone)**
> *Classification* is a **supervised machine learning paradigm** in which the model learns a mapping function $f: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ from a labeled training set $D = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$, where the target variable $y_i$ belongs to a **discrete, finite set of class labels** $\mathcal{Y} = \{C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_K\}$. The objective is to predict the categorical class of unseen observations with minimal expected loss.

In simpler words: Classification is the act of **putting things into boxes**. Given past examples that already have correct labels, the algorithm learns the rules of separation, and then applies those rules to new, unlabeled data.

---

## Conceptual Analogy — The Post Office Sorting Room

Imagine you walk into a massive Post Office sorting facility. There are **5 bins** at the end of a conveyor belt:
- **Local Mail** | **National Mail** | **International Mail** | **Express** | **Reject**

A human sorter has spent years (training data!) learning:
- If the stamp color is red and weight < 50g → **Local**
- If the address contains a foreign postcode → **International**

A *classification algorithm* is just this sorter, but in mathematical form. The "stamp color" and "weight" are the **features** $x$, and the **bin** is the **label** $y$. The sorter's "rulebook" is the trained model $f(x)$.

---

## Why Classification Matters in Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Classification sits at the heart of almost every modern intelligent system. For KTU 2024 outcomes, you must recognize that classification is **not regression** — the output is a *category*, not a *number on a line*.

| Engineering Domain | Classification Use Case |
|---|---|
| **Biomedical** | Tumor Malignant vs Benign |
| **Cybersecurity** | Intrusion Detection / Spam Filtering |
| **Civil / Structural** | Crack vs No-Crack on Concrete Surface |
| **Finance** | Loan Default vs No-Default |
| **NLP** | Sentiment: Positive vs Negative vs Neutral |

---

## Types of Classification Problems

1. **Binary Classification** — Only **2** classes. Example: Spam $\in \{0, 1\}$
2. **Multiclass Classification** — $K \geq 3$ mutually exclusive classes. Example: Digit recognition $\{0, 1, \ldots, 9\}$
3. **Multilabel Classification** — A sample can belong to **multiple** classes simultaneously. Example: A movie tagged as both "Action" and "Comedy"
4. **Imbalanced Classification** — Class frequencies are highly skewed. Example: Fraud detection (0.1% fraud)

---

## The Classification Pipeline (High-Level)

The standard KTU-endorsed workflow consists of **5 sequential stages**:

1. **Data Collection & Cleaning** — Handle missing values, outliers, noise.
2. **Feature Engineering & Scaling** — Standardization (Z-score), one-hot encoding of categoricals.
3. **Train/Test Split** — Typically 70:30 or 80:20, **stratified** to preserve class ratios.
4. **Model Training** — Fit the chosen classifier on $\mathcal{D}_{\text{train}}$.
5. **Evaluation & Tuning** — Confusion matrix, precision, recall, F1, ROC-AUC; cross-validation for hyperparameter search.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for KTU 2024:** The classifier never outputs a hard $0/1$ directly. It first computes a **probability** $P(y=C_k \mid x)$ and then applies a **decision rule** (usually threshold $\tau = 0.5$) to convert probability to label. This decoupling is what allows metrics like AUC and Log-Loss to exist.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mathematical Framework of Classification

A classifier is formally defined as a **decision function**:

$$h(x) = \arg\max_{C_k \in \mathcal{Y}} P(y = C_k \mid x)$$

This is the **Bayes Optimal Classifier** — the theoretically best possible classifier. In practice, we *estimate* the conditional probability $P(y \mid x)$ using one of several models.

---

## 2.2 Confusion Matrix — The Foundation of All Evaluation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU 2024, the **Confusion Matrix** is mandatory knowledge. Every other metric is derived from it.

For binary classification with classes $\{+1, -1\}$ (or $\{1, 0\}$):

| | **Predicted Positive (1)** | **Predicted Negative (0)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Positive (1)** | $TP$ (True Positive) | $FN$ (False Negative) — *Type II Error* |
| **Actual Negative (0)** | $FP$ (False Positive) — *Type I Error* | $TN$ (True Negative) |

> [!NOTE]
> A **Type I Error** is a *False Alarm* (predicting fraud when there is none). A **Type II Error** is a *Miss* (failing to catch actual fraud). In medical screening, Type II errors are far more dangerous.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **exam-critical**. Memorize the definitions; derivations follow in Section 3.

| Metric | Formula | Range | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Accuracy** | $\frac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | General-purpose; misleading on imbalanced data |
| **Error Rate** | $1 - \text{Accuracy} = \frac{FP+FN}{N}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Complement of accuracy |
| **Precision (PPV)** | $\frac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | $[0, 1]$ | When False Positives are costly (e.g., spam filter) |
| **Recall (Sensitivity, TPR)** | $\frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | When False Negatives are costly (e.g., cancer detection) |
| **Specificity (TNR)** | $\frac{TN}{TN+FP}$ | $[0, 1]$ | True negative rate |
| **F1-Score** | $2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Harmonic mean of P and R |
| **MCC (Matthews CC)** | $\frac{TP \cdot TN - FP \cdot FN}{\sqrt{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)(TN+FP)(TN+FN)}}$ | $[-1, 1]$ | Robust for imbalanced data |
| **Log-Loss (Cross-Entropy)** | $-\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\left[y_i \log(\hat{p}_i) + (1-y_i)\log(1-\hat{p}_i)\right]$ | $[0, \infty)$ | Penalizes confident wrong predictions |
| **ROC-AUC** | $\int_{0}^{1} \text{TPR}(\text{FPR}^{-1}(t))\, dt$ | $[0.5, 1]$ | Threshold-independent ranking quality |
| **Cohen's Kappa** | $\frac{p_o - p_e}{1 - p_e}$ | $[-1, 1]$ | Agreement adjusted for chance |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Symbol Rule:** Note that absolute-value bars have been written as $\vert \cdot \vert$ in equations to comply with markdown table safety. In handwritten KTU scripts, plain $\mid x \mid$ is acceptable.

---

## 2.4 The Sigmoid (Logistic) Function

The cornerstone of **Logistic Regression**. It squashes any real number to the open interval $(0, 1)$, making it a perfect probability estimator:

$$\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}} = \frac{e^{z}}{1 + e^{z}}$$

For a linear input $z = w^T x + b$:

$$P(y=1 \mid x; w, b) = \sigma(w^T x + b) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(w^T x + b)}}$$

**Key properties of $\sigma(z)$:**
- $\sigma(0) = 0.5$ — the decision boundary in probability space
- $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1 - \sigma(z))$ — clean derivative, used in gradient descent
- $\sigma(-z) = 1 - \sigma(z)$ — symmetry, useful for implementation
- As $z \to \infty$, $\sigma(z) \to 1$; as $z \to -\infty$, $\sigma(z) \to 0$

---

## 2.5 The Log-Odds (Logit) Transformation

Taking the natural log of the odds ratio:

$$\text{logit}(p) = \ln\left(\frac{p}{1-p}\right) = w^T x + b$$

This shows that **Logistic Regression is essentially a linear model in log-odds space**. The linearity in log-odds is the *defining geometric property* — it means the decision boundary $w^T x + b = 0$ is a **hyperplane** in feature space.

---

## 2.6 Why Cross-Entropy and Not MSE?

> [!TIP]
> A frequent KTU question: *"Why not use Mean Squared Error for classification?"*
> **Answer:** The loss surface of MSE + sigmoid is **non-convex** in classification (because of the $\sigma(1-\sigma)$ coupling), leading to multiple local minima. Cross-entropy yields a **convex** loss, guaranteeing convergence to the global optimum.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

In production systems, classification metrics drive **business decisions**:
- A loan approval model with **Recall = 0.95** means we catch 95% of defaulters — banks calibrate this to risk appetite.
- An **AUC of 0.87** in a manufacturing defect detector means 87% chance a random defective unit is ranked above a random good unit.
- **Precision @ Top-K** is used in recommendation ranking — among the top 10 recommendations, how many were clicked?

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section satisfies the KTU 2024 mandate for **exhaustive derivation with no step-skipping**. Every algebraic transition is shown.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cross-Entropy (Log-Loss) from Maximum Likelihood

**Goal:** Derive the cross-entropy loss used in Logistic Regression starting from first principles.

### Step 1 — Model the Likelihood

For a single training sample $(x_i, y_i)$ where $y_i \in \{0, 1\}$, the model gives:

$$P(y_i = 1 \mid x_i) = \hat{p}_i, \quad P(y_i = 0 \mid x_i) = 1 - \hat{p}_i$$

These two cases can be unified into a single expression using the **indicator trick**:

$$P(y_i \mid x_i) = \hat{p}_i^{\,y_i} \cdot (1 - \hat{p}_i)^{\,1 - y_i}$$

> **Logic:** If $y_i = 1$, the second factor becomes $(1-\hat{p}_i)^0 = 1$, leaving $\hat{p}_i$. If $y_i = 0$, the first factor becomes $\hat{p}_i^0 = 1$, leaving $(1-\hat{p}_i)$. Both cases correctly covered.

### Step 2 — Form the Likelihood over the Whole Dataset

Assuming i.i.d. samples, the joint likelihood is the product:

$$\mathcal{L}(w) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} \hat{p}_i^{\,y_i} \cdot (1 - \hat{p}_i)^{\,1 - y_i}$$

### Step 3 — Take the Logarithm (Log-Likelihood)

Logarithm converts the product into a sum, which is differentiable and numerically stable:

$$\ell(w) = \ln \mathcal{L}(w) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ y_i \ln \hat{p}_i + (1 - y_i) \ln (1 - \hat{p}_i) \right]$$

### Step 4 — Convert Maximization to Minimization (Negate and Average)

The standard convention in ML is to **minimize** a loss. We negate and average across $N$:

$$J(w) = -\frac{1}{N} \ell(w) = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ y_i \ln \hat{p}_i + (1 - y_i) \ln (1 - \hat{p}_i) \right]$$

> **This is the Binary Cross-Entropy (Log-Loss) function.**

### Step 5 — Substitute the Sigmoid Prediction

Replacing $\hat{p}_i = \sigma(z_i) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z_i}}$ where $z_i = w^T x_i + b$:

$$J(w, b) = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ y_i \ln \sigma(z_i) + (1 - y_i) \ln (1 - \sigma(z_i)) \right]$$

### Step 6 — Gradient Descent Update Rule

Differentiating $J$ w.r.t. $w_j$ and using the identity $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial w_j} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( \sigma(z_i) - y_i \right) x_{i,j}$$

**Update rule:** $w_j \leftarrow w_j - \alpha \cdot \frac{\partial J}{\partial w_j}$ where $\alpha$ is the **learning rate**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice the elegant form $\left(\hat{p}_i - y_i\right) x_{i,j}$ — this is identical in shape to the LMS rule in linear regression! This is the foundation of the **Generalized Linear Model (GLM)** family.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Information Gain & Entropy for Decision Trees

**Goal:** Derive the splitting criterion used in ID3 / C4.5 decision trees.

### Step 1 — Shannon Entropy

For a discrete random variable $Y$ with $K$ classes and probability distribution $P(Y=C_k) = p_k$:

$$H(Y) = -\sum_{k=1}^{K} p_k \log_2 p_k$$

> **Logic:** Entropy measures **impurity** or **uncertainty**. $H=0$ means pure node (all one class); $H=\log_2 K$ means maximum chaos (uniform distribution).

### Step 2 — Conditional Entropy After Split

If we split the dataset $S$ on feature $A$ into subsets $S_v$ for each value $v$ of $A$:

$$H(Y \mid A) = \sum_{v \in \text{Values}(A)} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(Y \mid S_v)$$

> **Logic:** Weighted average of entropies of the child nodes, weighted by their size.

### Step 3 — Information Gain

$$\text{IG}(S, A) = H(Y) - H(Y \mid A) = H(S) - \sum_{v} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$$

> **Decision Rule:** Choose the feature $A^*$ that maximizes Information Gain: $A^* = \arg\max_A \text{IG}(S, A)$.

### Step 4 — Numerical Example (KTU Favorite)

Consider a dataset with 14 samples: 9 of class "+" and 5 of class "−". Split on feature $A$ gives:
- Left child: 6 samples, 4 "+", 2 "−"
- Right child: 8 samples, 5 "+", 3 "−"

**Parent Entropy:**

$$H(\text{parent}) = -\frac{9}{14}\log_2\frac{9}{14} - \frac{5}{14}\log_2\frac{5}{14}$$

$$= -0.643 \times (-0.637) - 0.357 \times (-1.485)$$

$$= 0.410 + 0.530 = 0.940 \text{ bits}$$

**Child Entropies:**

$$H(\text{left}) = -\frac{4}{6}\log_2\frac{4}{6} - \frac{2}{6}\log_2\frac{2}{6} = 0.918 \text{ bits}$$

$$H(\text{right}) = -\frac{5}{8}\log_2\frac{5}{8} - \frac{3}{8}\log_2\frac{3}{8} = 0.954 \text{ bits}$$

**Weighted Conditional Entropy:**

$$H(Y \mid A) = \frac{6}{14}(0.918) + \frac{8}{14}(0.954) = 0.393 + 0.545 = 0.938 \text{ bits}$$

**Information Gain:**

$$\text{IG}(S, A) = 0.940 - 0.938 = 0.002 \text{ bits}$$

> **Interpretation:** A near-zero IG means this split is *useless* — the child nodes are almost as impure as the parent. The algorithm will reject this feature.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Naive Bayes Posterior

**Goal:** Derive the classification rule for Naive Bayes using Bayes' Theorem.

### Step 1 — Apply Bayes' Theorem

$$P(y = C_k \mid x) = \frac{P(x \mid y = C_k) \cdot P(y = C_k)}{P(x)}$$

### Step 2 — The "Naive" Conditional Independence Assumption

Assume all features $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_d$ are conditionally independent given the class:

$$P(x \mid y = C_k) = \prod_{j=1}^{d} P(x_j \mid y = C_k)$$

> **Logic:** This is the "naive" assumption — features are treated as independent contributors. In practice, this is often *violated*, but the classifier still performs surprisingly well.

### Step 3 — Form the Decision Rule

Since $P(x)$ is constant across all classes, we can drop it for the argmax:

$$\hat{y} = \arg\max_{C_k} P(y = C_k) \cdot \prod_{j=1}^{d} P(x_j \mid y = C_k)$$

### Step 4 — Work in Log-Space (Numerical Stability)

Products of many small probabilities underflow to 0. Taking log converts products to sums:

$$\hat{y} = \arg\max_{C_k} \left[ \ln P(y = C_k) + \sum_{j=1}^{d} \ln P(x_j \mid y = C_k) \right]$$

### Step 5 — Laplace Smoothing (Zero-Frequency Fix)

If a feature value never co-occurs with a class in training, $P(x_j \mid C_k) = 0$ and the entire product collapses. Add-1 (Laplace) smoothing:

$$\hat{P}(x_j = v \mid C_k) = \frac{\text{count}(x_j = v, C_k) + 1}{\text{count}(C_k) + V_j}$$

where $V_j$ is the number of possible values of feature $j$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — KNN Distance Metrics

For K-Nearest Neighbors, the prediction is the **majority vote** of the $K$ closest training points:

$$\hat{y} = \text{mode}\left( \{ y_i : x_i \in \mathcal{N}_K(x) \} \right)$$

where $\mathcal{N}_K(x)$ is the set of $K$ nearest neighbors of $x$ under a chosen distance $d(x, x_i)$:

| Distance | Formula | When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Euclidean (L2)** | $\sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{d}(x_j - x'_j)^2}$ | Continuous, scaled features |
| **Manhattan (L1)** | $\sum_{j=1}^{d} \vert x_j - x'_j \vert$ | High-dimensional sparse data |
| **Minkowski (Lp)** | $\left(\sum_{j=1}^{d} \vert x_j - x'_j \vert^p\right)^{1/p}$ | Generalized; L1 and L2 are special cases |
| **Cosine** | $1 - \frac{x \cdot x'}{\vert\vert x \vert\vert \cdot \vert\vert x' \vert\vert}$ | Text / TF-IDF vectors |

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Algorithm Comparison

| Algorithm | Type | Training Style | Key Hyperparameter | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Logistic Regression** | Probabilistic / Linear | Gradient Descent | Learning rate $\alpha$, regularization $\lambda$ | Interpretable, fast, probabilistic output | Linear boundary only |
| **Decision Tree** | Non-parametric | Greedy (top-down) | Max depth, min samples split | Handles mixed data types, no scaling needed | High variance, overfits |
| **KNN** | Instance-based / Lazy | None (memorizes) | $K$ (number of neighbors), distance metric | No training phase, simple | Slow at prediction, curse of dimensionality |
| **Naive Bayes** | Probabilistic | Closed-form (counts) | Smoothing $\alpha$ | Extremely fast, works with small data | Independence assumption often false |
| **SVM** | Geometric | Quadratic Programming | $C$ (regularization), kernel, $\gamma$ | Excellent in high-dim, kernelizable | Slow on large $N$, no probabilistic output |
| **Random Forest** | Ensemble (Bagging) | Bootstrap + averaging | $n_{\text{trees}}$, max features | Robust, handles missing data | Less interpretable, larger model |

```mermaid
flowchart LR
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[Input Feature Vector x of size d] --> C2[Compute Linear Score z equals w transpose x plus b]
    C2 --> C3[Apply Sigmoid Function sigma of z]
    C3 --> C4[Probability Estimate p hat between 0 and 1]
    C4 --> C5{Threshold Tau equals 0.5}
    C5 -- p hat GE 0.5 --> C6[Predict Class 1]
    C5 -- p hat LT 0.5 --> C7[Predict Class 0]
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D1[Root Node with All Samples] --> D2[Test Feature A1 Information Gain]
    D2 -- MAX GAIN --> D3[Split on A1 less than threshold]
    D2 -- NOT MAX --> D4[Test Feature A2 Information Gain]
    D4 --> D3
    D3 --> D5[Left Child Node Subset L1]
    D3 --> D6[Right Child Node Subset L2]
    D5 --> D7{Pure Node or Max Depth}
    D6 --> D8{Pure Node or Max Depth}
    D7 -- YES --> D9[Leaf Label Class Ck]
    D7 -- NO --> D10[Recurse Further Splitting]
    D8 -- YES --> D11[Leaf Label Class Cj]
    D8 -- NO --> D10
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E1[Sort Predictions by Descending Score] --> E2[Sweep Threshold Tau from 1 to 0]
    E2 --> E3[Compute TPR and FPR at Each Threshold]
    E3 --> E4[Plot TPR on Y axis vs FPR on X axis]
    E4 --> E5[Compute Area Under Curve AUC]
    E5 --> E6{Interpret AUC}
    E6 -- AUC equals 0.5 --> E7[Random Guessing]
    E6 -- AUC equals 1.0 --> E8[Perfect Classifier]

```
