## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]`
**Differentiate between Precision and Recall. In a medical diagnosis system for cancer detection, which metric is more critical and why?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Precision | Recall |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | $\frac{TP}{TP+FP}$ — Of all predicted positives, how many are correct? | $\frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ — Of all actual positives, how many did we catch? |
| **Focus** | Quality of positive predictions | Coverage of actual positives |
| **Trade-off** | Increases when FP decreases | Increases when FN decreases |

**In cancer detection, Recall is more critical (1 mark).** Missing an actual cancer case (FN) can be life-threatening, while a false alarm (FP) only leads to a follow-up test. Hence, medical systems are tuned to **maximize Recall** (often at the cost of some Precision). The principle *"better safe than sorry"* applies — Type II errors are far more dangerous than Type I errors in this domain. **(1 mark for medical justification + 1 mark for correct definitions)**

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]`
**Explain the role of the Sigmoid function in Logistic Regression. Why is it preferred over a step function?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Sigmoid function $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ **(1 mark)** maps any real-valued input $z = w^Tx + b$ to the open interval $(0, 1)$, allowing the output to be interpreted as a **probability** $P(y=1 \mid x)$. **(1 mark)**

It is preferred over a step (Heaviside) function because:
1. The sigmoid is **continuous and differentiable everywhere**, enabling gradient-based optimization (gradient descent).
2. Its derivative has a clean closed form: $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$.
3. The step function is non-differentiable at the threshold, making optimization mathematically intractable. **(1 mark for comparison)**

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**A classifier is trained on a medical dataset to detect a rare disease. The test set confusion matrix is given below. Compute and interpret all major evaluation metrics. Also discuss the class imbalance problem.**

| | **Predicted Disease** | **Predicted Healthy** |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Disease** | 50 | 10 |
| **Actual Healthy** | 5 | 935 |

#### **Part (a) — Compute all metrics [7 Marks]**

**Solution:**

From the confusion matrix:
- $TP = 50$, $FN = 10$, $FP = 5$, $TN = 935$, Total $N = 1000$

**Step 1 — Accuracy:**

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{N} = \frac{50 + 935}{1000} = \frac{985}{1000} = 0.985$$

**Step 2 — Precision (PPV):**

$$\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{50}{50 + 5} = \frac{50}{55} \approx 0.909$$

**Step 3 — Recall (Sensitivity, TPR):**

$$\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{50}{50 + 10} = \frac{50}{60} \approx 0.833$$

**Step 4 — Specificity (TNR):**

$$\text{Specificity} = \frac{TN}{TN + FP} = \frac{935}{935 + 5} = \frac{935}{940} \approx 0.995$$

**Step 5 — F1-Score:**

$$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.909 \times 0.833}{0.909 + 0.833} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.757}{1.742} \approx 0.869$$

**Step 6 — False Positive Rate (FPR):**

$$\text{FPR} = 1 - \text{Specificity} = 1 - 0.995 = 0.005$$

**Step 7 — Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC):**

$$\text{MCC} = \frac{TP \cdot TN - FP \cdot FN}{\sqrt{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)(TN+FP)(TN+FN)}}$$

$$\text{MCC} = \frac{(50)(935) - (5)(10)}{\sqrt{(55)(60)(940)(945)}} = \frac{46750 - 50}{\sqrt{2,930,460,000}}$$

$$\text{MCC} = \frac{46700}{\sqrt{2.930 \times 10^9}} = \frac{46700}{54133.5} \approx 0.863$$

> **Summary of Metrics [7 marks — 1 mark each, with the last one being bonus integration]:**
> Accuracy = 0.985, Precision = 0.909, Recall = 0.833, Specificity = 0.995, F1 = 0.869, FPR = 0.005, MCC = 0.863.

#### **Part (b) — Discuss the class imbalance problem and remedies [7 Marks]**

**Solution:**

**The Imbalance Problem:** The dataset has 60 diseased vs 940 healthy — a ratio of **1:15.67**. A naive classifier that predicts "Healthy" for everyone would achieve 94% accuracy while being *completely useless*. This is the **accuracy paradox**. **(2 marks)**

**Remedies — Three families of solutions:**

1. **Data-Level Resampling [2 marks]**
   - **Oversampling minority:** SMOTE (Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique) creates synthetic samples along the line segments joining k nearest minority neighbors.
   - **Undersampling majority:** Randomly drop healthy samples; risk of information loss.
   - **Hybrid:** Combination of both.

2. **Algorithm-Level Modifications [2 marks]**
   - Use **class weights** inversely proportional to frequency: $w_k = N / (K \cdot n_k)$.
   - Use **cost-sensitive learning** with asymmetric misclassification costs.
   - Switch from accuracy to **balanced accuracy** $\frac{1}{2}(\text{TPR} + \text{TNR})$ or **F1-score**.

3. **Evaluation Adjustments [1 mark]**
   - Always use **Precision-Recall curves** instead of ROC when classes are heavily skewed.
   - Use **PR-AUC** which is more sensitive to performance on the rare class.

**Final Recommendation:** In this medical context, **SMOTE + class-weighted Logistic Regression** combined with **Recall** as the primary metric would be the optimal engineering choice. **(Final integration — bonus)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`

**Consider a Logistic Regression model trained on a 2-D dataset. Derive the decision boundary equation, explain how it is used for classification, and show the gradient descent update rule for the weights.**

#### **Part (a) — Derive the decision boundary [7 Marks]**

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — The Logistic Regression Model [2 marks]**

For input $x = (x_1, x_2)$ and parameters $w = (w_1, w_2)$, $b$:

$$P(y=1 \mid x; w, b) = \sigma(w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + b) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + b)}}$$

**Step 2 — Decision Rule [2 marks]**

Predict class 1 if $P(y=1 \mid x) \geq 0.5$, else class 0:

$$\hat{y} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \sigma(z) \geq 0.5 \\ 0 & \text{if } \sigma(z) < 0.5 \end{cases}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to Linear Boundary [3 marks]**

Since $\sigma(z) = 0.5 \iff z = 0$ (because $\sigma$ is monotonically increasing and $\sigma(0) = 0.5$):

$$w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + b = 0$$

$$\boxed{x_2 = -\frac{w_1}{w_2} x_1 - \frac{b}{w_2}}$$

> **Geometric Interpretation:** This is the equation of a **straight line** in the $(x_1, x_2)$ plane. The slope is $-w_1/w_2$ and the $x_2$-intercept is $-b/w_2$. Logistic Regression can only produce **linear** decision boundaries — this is its primary limitation. For non-linear boundaries, we must use kernel methods, decision trees, or neural networks.

#### **Part (b) — Derive the gradient descent update rule [7 Marks]**

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Loss Function (Binary Cross-Entropy) [2 marks]**

$$J(w, b) = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ y_i \ln \hat{p}_i + (1 - y_i) \ln (1 - \hat{p}_i) \right]$$

where $\hat{p}_i = \sigma(z_i) = \sigma(w_1 x_{1,i} + w_2 x_{2,i} + b)$.

**Step 2 — Partial Derivative w.r.t. $w_j$ [3 marks]**

Using the chain rule and the identity $\frac{d\sigma}{dz} = \sigma(z)(1 - \sigma(z)) = \hat{p}(1-\hat{p})$:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial w_j} = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ y_i \cdot \frac{1}{\hat{p}_i} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{p}_i}{\partial w_j} + (1 - y_i) \cdot \frac{1}{1 - \hat{p}_i} \cdot \left(-\frac{\partial \hat{p}_i}{\partial w_j}\right) \right]$$

Now $\frac{\partial \hat{p}_i}{\partial w_j} = \hat{p}_i (1 - \hat{p}_i) x_{j,i}$. Substituting:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial w_j} = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ y_i (1 - \hat{p}_i) x_{j,i} - (1 - y_i) \hat{p}_i x_{j,i} \right]$$

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial w_j} = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ y_i - \hat{p}_i \right] x_{j,i} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( \hat{p}_i - y_i \right) x_{j,i}$$

**Step 3 — Update Rule [2 marks]**

$$w_j^{\text{new}} = w_j^{\text{old}} - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( \hat{p}_i - y_i \right) x_{j,i}$$

where $\alpha$ is the learning rate (typical value $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-1}$).

> **Convergence Note:** The cross-entropy loss in Logistic Regression is **convex** with respect to $w$ and $b$, guaranteeing that gradient descent converges to the **global minimum** given an appropriate $\alpha$. This is a key advantage over MSE + sigmoid combinations.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing $TP$ and $TN$ positions in the confusion matrix** — always label the rows as "Actual" and columns as "Predicted" (or vice versa, but be consistent).
> 2. **Forgetting to negate** when converting log-likelihood to a loss function. Cross-entropy is the *negative* log-likelihood.
> 3. **Writing $P(y=1 \mid x)$ as a step function** — Logistic Regression outputs a *probability*, not a hard class. The classification decision is made *after* thresholding.
> 4. **Claiming Logistic Regression can solve XOR** — it cannot, because its decision boundary is linear. Many students make this claim in viva. The correct answer requires a non-linear model or feature transformation.
> 5. **Confusing Type I and Type II errors** — Type I = False Positive (false alarm); Type II = False Negative (miss).
> 6. **Not stratifying the train/test split** on imbalanced data — leads to a test set with zero minority samples and undefined recall.
> 7. **Reporting only accuracy** on imbalanced datasets — this is the most common mistake; use F1, MCC, or PR-AUC.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE**

- [x] **Classification is supervised learning with discrete output** $y \in \{C_1, \ldots, C_K\}$ — never confuse it with regression (continuous output).
- [x] **Confusion Matrix** is the foundation: rows are *Actual*, columns are *Predicted* (or vice versa — be consistent).
- [x] **TP, FP, TN, FN** are the four cardinal numbers; every metric is a function of them.
- [x] **Accuracy** is misleading on imbalanced data — always pair it with **Precision, Recall, F1**.
- [x] **Precision = TP/(TP+FP)** — minimize false alarms.
- [x] **Recall = TP/(TP+FN)** — minimize missed detections.
- [x] **F1 = 2PR/(P+R)** — harmonic mean, robust single-number summary for imbalanced classes.
- [x] **Sigmoid function** $\sigma(z) = 1/(1+e^{-z})$ squashes to $(0,1)$; derivative is $\sigma(1-\sigma)$.
- [x] **Logistic Regression** is linear in log-odds space; decision boundary is a hyperplane.
- [x] **Cross-Entropy Loss** is derived from **Maximum Likelihood Estimation** under a Bernoulli model.
- [x] **MSE + Sigmoid is non-convex**; **Cross-Entropy + Sigmoid is convex** — always prefer cross-entropy for classification.
- [x] **Decision Trees** use **Information Gain** (ID3) or **Gini Impurity** (CART) as splitting criteria.
- [x] **Entropy** $H(Y) = -\sum p_k \log_2 p_k$ ranges in $[0, \log_2 K]$.
- [x] **Naive Bayes** assumes conditional independence of features given the class; use **Laplace smoothing** to avoid zero probabilities.
- [x] **KNN** is a *lazy learner* with no training phase; prediction uses **majority vote** of K nearest neighbors.
- [x] **Distance metrics** in KNN: Euclidean (L2), Manhattan (L1), Minkowski (Lp), Cosine.
- [x] **ROC-AUC** measures threshold-independent ranking quality; **PR-AUC** is preferred for imbalanced data.
- [x] **Type I Error** = False Positive; **Type II Error** = False Negative.
- [x] In **medical diagnosis**, prioritize **Recall**; in **spam filtering**, prioritize **Precision**.
- [x] Always **stratify** train/test splits when classes are imbalanced.
- [x] **SMOTE** is a synthetic oversampling technique used to balance class distributions.
- [x] **Logistic Regression cannot solve XOR** — it is fundamentally a linear classifier.
- [x] Gradient descent update for Logistic Regression: $w_j \leftarrow w_j - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{N}\sum(\hat{p}_i - y_i) x_{j,i}$.
- [x] Cross-entropy loss for Logistic Regression is **convex** — guarantees global optimum.
- [x] Always apply **feature scaling** (StandardScaler / MinMaxScaler) before KNN and SVM.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Clustering and dimensionality reduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET861 - DATA SCIENCE WITH JULIA
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Clustering and dimensionality reduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Clustering — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Clustering (Unsupervised Learning Definition):**
> Clustering is the process of partitioning an unlabeled dataset $X = \{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n\}$ where each $x_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$ into $k$ homogeneous groups (clusters) $C = \{C_1, C_2, \dots, C_k\}$ such that **intra-cluster similarity is maximized** and **inter-cluster similarity is minimized**, without relying on pre-existing class labels.

Mathematically, clustering seeks to optimize a criterion function $J(C)$ subject to:

$$C_1 \cup C_2 \cup \dots \cup C_k = X \quad \text{and} \quad C_i \cap C_j = \varnothing \; \text{for} \; i \neq j$$

## 1.2 Dimensionality Reduction — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dimensionality Reduction (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> Dimensionality reduction is the transformation of a high-dimensional dataset $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$ into a lower-dimensional representation $Z \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times k}$ where $k \ll d$, while preserving as much of the **statistical structure**, **variance**, or **neighborhood relationships** of the original data as possible.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Library Sorting Room"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:**
> Imagine a librarian receives **10,000 unsorted books** dumped in a hall (this is your raw dataset). 
> 1. **Clustering** is like grouping them into shelves by genre — action, romance, science — so related books stay together. The librarian uses similarity in topics (features) to form groups.
> 2. **Dimensionality Reduction** is like realizing that 50 different sub-categories (space-opera, cyberpunk, hard-SF, etc.) can be summarized using just 3 mega-categories (Fiction, Non-Fiction, Academic). The librarian compresses information without losing meaning.

This two-step pipeline is the **heart of unsupervised data exploration** and is heavily tested in KTU ESE Module 3.

## 1.4 Why These Techniques Matter in Engineering

| Engineering Domain | Clustering Use-Case | Dimensionality Reduction Use-Case |
|---|---|---|
| **IoT / Sensor Networks** | Grouping sensors by behaviour patterns | Compressing high-frequency telemetry |
| **Biomedical Signal Processing** | Patient stratification from ECG/EEG | Feature extraction from MRI voxels |
| **Computer Vision** | Image segmentation | Face recognition (Eigenfaces via PCA) |
| **Recommender Systems** | User segmentation | Collaborative filtering embeddings |
| **Structural Engineering** | Damage pattern clustering | Modal analysis dimensionality trimming |

## 1.5 Key Standard Metrics (KTU High-Yield)

- **Inertia (Within-Cluster Sum of Squares — WCSS):** $J = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{x \in C_i} \vert\vert x - \mu_i \vert\vert^2$
- **Silhouette Score:** range $[-1, 1]$, **higher is better**
- **Explained Variance Ratio (PCA):** $\eta_j = \dfrac{\lambda_j}{\sum_{i=1}^{d} \lambda_i}$
- **Epsilon-Neighborhood (DBSCAN):** $N_{\epsilon}(p) = \{q \in D \mid \text{dist}(p,q) \leq \epsilon\}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Clustering Family — KTU 2024 Taxonomy

### 2.1.1 K-Means Clustering (Partition-Based)

**Algorithm Logic Steps:**
1. **Initialization:** Randomly select $k$ initial centroids $\{\mu_1^{(0)}, \mu_2^{(0)}, \dots, \mu_k^{(0)}\}$ — usually via **Forgy** or **K-Means++** initialization.
2. **Assignment Step:** For every data point $x_i$, assign it to the cluster whose centroid is nearest (Euclidean distance).
3. **Update Step:** Recompute each centroid as the **mean** of all points assigned to its cluster.
4. **Convergence Check:** Stop when centroid positions stabilize or maximum iterations reached.

> [!NOTE]
> **Convergence Guarantee:** K-means is guaranteed to converge to a **local minimum** (not global) of the inertia $J$. Multiple random restarts are recommended.

### 2.1.2 Hierarchical Clustering

- **Agglomerative (Bottom-Up):** Start with $n$ singleton clusters → iteratively merge the two closest clusters.
- **Divisive (Top-Down):** Start with one cluster containing all points → recursively split.
- **Linkage Criteria** (CRITICAL for KTU):

| Linkage Type | Formula | Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| **Single** | $d(C_i, C_j) = \min_{x \in C_i, y \in C_j} d(x,y)$ | Tends to produce **chained** clusters |
| **Complete** | $d(C_i, C_j) = \max_{x \in C_i, y \in C_j} d(x,y)$ | Produces **compact, balanced** clusters |
| **Average** | $d(C_i, C_j) = \frac{1}{\vert C_i \vert \vert C_j \vert}\sum_{x,y} d(x,y)$ | Compromise between single & complete |
| **Ward's** | Minimizes increase in **total WCSS** | Best for **spherical, equal-variance** clusters |

### 2.1.3 DBSCAN (Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise)

- **Core Point:** A point $p$ is a core point if $\vert N_{\epsilon}(p) \vert \geq \text{MinPts}$.
- **Border Point:** Not a core point, but lies within $\epsilon$ of a core point.
- **Noise Point (Outlier):** Neither core nor border.
- **Density-Reachability** is the key transitive property.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> DBSCAN **does NOT require pre-specifying $k$**, can discover clusters of **arbitrary shape**, and is **robust to outliers** — three features KTU examiners love to test.

## 2.2 The Dimensionality Reduction Family

### 2.2.1 Principal Component Analysis (PCA) — Linear Method

**The 'Why' Behind PCA:**
High-dimensional data lies on (or near) a lower-dimensional **linear subspace**. PCA finds orthogonal axes (principal components) along which the data has **maximum variance**.

**Step-by-Step Concept:**
1. **Center the data:** $x_i^{\text{centered}} = x_i - \bar{x}$
2. **Compute Covariance Matrix:** $S = \frac{1}{n-1} X_c^T X_c$
3. **Eigendecomposition:** $S v_j = \lambda_j v_j$
4. **Sort eigenvalues** in descending order: $\lambda_1 \geq \lambda_2 \geq \dots \geq \lambda_d$
5. **Project:** $Z = X_c W_k$ where $W_k = [v_1, v_2, \dots, v_k]$

### 2.2.2 t-SNE (t-Distributed Stochastic Neighbor Embedding) — Non-Linear

- Converts high-dimensional Euclidean distances into **conditional probabilities** $p_{j \mid i}$.
- In low-dim space, uses **Student t-distribution** (heavy tails) to avoid the **crowding problem**.
- Excellent for **visualization** (2D/3D), poor for general-purpose DR.

### 2.2.3 UMAP (Uniform Manifold Approximation and Projection)

- Based on **Riemannian geometry** and **topological data analysis**.
- Faster than t-SNE, preserves more global structure.
- Increasingly tested in modern KTU syllabi.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Concept | Formula | KTU Mark Weight |
|---|---|---|
| **Euclidean Distance** | $d(x,y) = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{d}(x_j - y_j)^2}$ | ★★★ |
| **Manhattan Distance** | $d(x,y) = \sum_{j=1}^{d} \vert x_j - y_j \vert$ | ★★★ |
| **Minkowski (General)** | $d_p(x,y) = \left(\sum_{j=1}^{d} \vert x_j - y_j \vert^p\right)^{1/p}$ | ★★ |
| **Cosine Similarity** | $\cos \theta = \frac{x \cdot y}{\vert\vert x \vert\vert \, \vert\vert y \vert\vert}$ | ★★ |
| **WCSS / Inertia** | $J = \sum_{i=1}^{k}\sum_{x \in C_i} \vert\vert x - \mu_i \vert\vert^2$ | ★★★ |
| **Silhouette Coefficient** | $s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i), b(i)\}}$ | ★★★ |
| **Covariance Matrix** | $S = \frac{1}{n-1} X_c^T X_c$ | ★★★ |
| **PCA Objective** | $\max_{v} \, v^T S v$ subject to $v^T v = 1$ | ★★★ |
| **Explained Variance** | $\eta_j = \frac{\lambda_j}{\sum_{i=1}^{d} \lambda_i}$ | ★★★ |
| **Cumulative Variance** | $V_k = \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{k}\lambda_j}{\sum_{i=1}^{d}\lambda_i}$ | ★★ |
| **DBSCAN Epsilon Ball** | $N_{\epsilon}(p) = \{q \in D \mid \text{dist}(p,q) \leq \epsilon\}$ | ★★★ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** PCA is used in **face recognition (Eigenfaces)**, **gene expression analysis**, and **signal compression in 5G OFDM systems**. K-Means powers **vector quantization** in image compression. DBSCAN is the workhorse for **anomaly detection** in network security.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Complete Derivation of PCA (Eigenvalue Problem)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PCA Derivation Mandate:** Every algebraic transition is shown explicitly. No steps are skipped, as required by KTU's 14-mark model answer expectations.

### Step 1 — Problem Formulation

We want to find a unit direction vector $v \in \mathbb{R}^d$ such that the **projected variance** of centered data $X_c$ onto $v$ is maximized.

$$\text{Projected variance} = \frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(v^T x_i^{(c)})^2 = v^T S v$$

where $x_i^{(c)} = x_i - \bar{x}$ is the $i$-th centered sample, and $S$ is the $d \times d$ sample covariance matrix.

### Step 2 — Constrained Optimization (Why We Need Lagrange Multipliers)

Since $v$ can be scaled arbitrarily to make the variance infinite, we constrain $v$ to be a unit vector:

$$\max_{v} \, v^T S v \quad \text{subject to} \quad v^T v = 1$$

### Step 3 — Lagrangian Construction

Construct the Lagrangian:

$$\mathcal{L}(v, \lambda) = v^T S v - \lambda(v^T v - 1)$$

### Step 4 — Take the Gradient and Set to Zero

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial v} = 2Sv - 2\lambda v = 0$$

$$\boxed{Sv = \lambda v}$$

This is the **eigenequation**. The direction $v$ is an **eigenvector** of $S$, and the projected variance equals the **eigenvalue** $\lambda$.

### Step 5 — Sort Eigenvalues

To maximize variance, pick the eigenvector corresponding to the **largest eigenvalue** $\lambda_1$. The top-$k$ eigenvectors form the projection matrix $W_k$.

### Step 6 — Reconstruction Error Bound

The **total variance preserved** when keeping $k$ components:

$$
V_k = \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{k} \lambda_j}{\sum_{j=1}^{d} \lambda_j}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 14-Mark Tip:** Examiners expect students to state the **Lagrangian form**, show the **derivative step**, and conclude that the problem reduces to an **eigenvalue problem**. Skipping Step 3–4 loses 4–5 marks.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Manual PCA on 2D Data

**Given Data (already centered):**
$$X_c = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 3 & 2 \\ 1 & -1 \\ 4 & 3 \end{bmatrix}, \quad n = 4, \quad d = 2$$

### Step A — Compute Covariance Matrix

$$X_c^T X_c = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 3 & 1 & 4 \\ 1 & 2 & -1 & 3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 3 & 2 \\ 1 & -1 \\ 4 & 3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 4+9+1+16 & 2+6-1+12 \\ 2+6-1+12 & 1+4+1+9 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$
= \begin{bmatrix} 30 & 19 \\ 19 & 15 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$S = \frac{1}{n-1}X_c^T X_c = \frac{1}{3}\begin{bmatrix} 30 & 19 \\ 19 & 15 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 10 & 6.333 \\ 6.333 & 5 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step B — Eigenvalue Equation

$$\det(S - \lambda I) = 0$$

$$
(10 - \lambda)(5 - \lambda) - (6.333)^2 = 0
$$

$$
\lambda^2 - 15\lambda + 50 - 40.111 = 0
$$

$$
\lambda^2 - 15\lambda + 9.889 = 0
$$

Applying the quadratic formula:

$$
\lambda = \frac{15 \pm \sqrt{225 - 39.556}}{2} = \frac{15 \pm \sqrt{185.444}}{2} = \frac{15 \pm 13.617}{2}
$$

$$
\lambda_1 = 14.308, \quad \lambda_2 = 0.692
$$

### Step C — Explained Variance

$$
V_1 = \frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_1 + \lambda_2} = \frac{14.308}{15.000} \approx 0.954 = 95.4\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** A single principal component captures **95.4%** of the data's variance — we can compress 2D → 1D with minimal information loss.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of K-Means Update Rule

The centroid $\mu_i$ of cluster $C_i$ minimizing $\sum_{x \in C_i} \vert\vert x - \mu_i \vert\vert^2$ is the **mean**:

$$
\mu_i = \frac{1}{\vert C_i \vert}\sum_{x \in C_i} x
$$

**Proof Sketch:**
Take the partial derivative of the cluster objective and set to zero:

$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial \mu_i}\sum_{x \in C_i} \vert\vert x - \mu_i \vert\vert^2 = -2\sum_{x \in C_i}(x - \mu_i) = 0
$$

$$
\sum_{x \in C_i} x - \vert C_i \vert \mu_i = 0 \implies \mu_i = \frac{1}{\vert C_i \vert}\sum_{x \in C_i} x
$$

---

## 3.4 Silhouette Coefficient Derivation

For point $i$ in cluster $C_I$:

- **$a(i)$** = mean distance from $i$ to other points in $C_I$ (cohesion).
- **$b(i)$** = min mean distance from $i$ to points in any other cluster (separation).

$$
s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i), b(i)\}} \in [-1, +1]
$$

- $s(i) \approx +1$: Well-clustered.
- $s(i) \approx 0$: On cluster boundary.
- $s(i) \approx -1$: Possibly misclassified.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — K-Means Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Input dataset X and k] --> B[Initialize k centroids using K-Means++]
    B --> C[Assignment Step: Assign each point to nearest centroid]
    C --> D[Update Step: Recompute centroids as cluster means]
    D --> E{Convergence check: Centroids stable?}
    E -- No --> C
    E -- Yes --> F[Output: Final cluster labels C1 to Ck]
    F --> G[End]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — PCA Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw High-Dim Data X] --> B[Mean Centering X minus mu]
    B --> C[Covariance Matrix S]
    C --> D[Eigendecomposition S v equals lambda v]
    D --> E[Sort Eigenvalues descending]
    E --> F[Select Top k Eigenvectors as Wk]
    F --> G[Project Z equals Xc Wk]
    G --> H[Lower-Dim Representation Z in R k]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — DBSCAN Point Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[For each point p in D] --> B{Number of points in epsilon neighborhood of p greater than or equal to MinPts}
    B -- Yes --> C[Mark p as CORE POINT]
    B -- No --> D{Is p in epsilon neighborhood of any core point}
    D -- Yes --> E[Mark p as BORDER POINT]
    D -- No --> F[Mark p as NOISE OUTLIER]
    C --> G[Form cluster via density reachability]
    E --> G
    F --> H[Add to noise set]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Architecture — End-to-End Unsupervised Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Dataset n samples by d features] --> B[Step 1: Data Preprocessing]
    B --> B1[Handle missing values]
    B --> B2[Feature scaling StandardScaler]
    B --> B3[Encoding for categorical vars]
    B1 --> C
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C[Step 2: Dimensionality Reduction]
    C --> C1[Linear: PCA / LDA]
    C --> C2[Non-Linear: t-SNE / UMAP]
    C1 --> D
    C2 --> D[Step 3: Clustering]
    D --> D1[Partition: K-Means]
    D --> D2[Hierarchical: Agglomerative]
    D --> D3[Density: DBSCAN]
    D1 --> E
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E[Step 4: Evaluation]
    E --> E1[Internal: Silhouette / Davies-Bouldin]
    E --> E2[External if labels exist: ARI / NMI]
    E1 --> F[Final Cluster Assignments]
    E2 --> F
```

## 4.5 Conceptual Map — When to Use Which Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Choose Clustering Algorithm] --> B{K is known?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Data is linearly separable?}
    C -- Yes --> D[K-Means]
    C -- No --> E[Spectral Clustering]
    B -- No --> F{Need to detect noise?}
    F -- Yes --> G[DBSCAN]
    F -- No --> H{Hierarchical structure required?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Agglomerative Clustering]
    H -- No --> J[Mean Shift]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP]`
**Define clustering. List any four applications of clustering in engineering domains.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
**Definition (1.5 marks):** Clustering is an unsupervised machine learning technique that partitions $n$ unlabeled data points into $k$ groups based on similarity, such that intra-cluster similarity is high and inter-cluster similarity is low.

**Applications (1.5 marks — any four):**
1. **Image segmentation** in computer vision
2. **Customer segmentation** in marketing analytics
3. **Anomaly detection** in network intrusion detection systems
4. **Document clustering** for topic modeling in NLP
5. **Gene expression analysis** in bioinformatics
6. **Sensor fault grouping** in IoT systems

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3]`
**What is the curse of dimensionality? How does dimensionality reduction help overcome it?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
**Curse of Dimensionality (1.5 marks):** As the number of features $d$ increases, the volume of the feature space grows exponentially, causing data points to become **sparsely distributed**. Distances between points become **less meaningful** (all pairwise distances converge), and the amount of data required to maintain statistical significance grows exponentially with $d$.

**How DR Helps (1.5 marks):**
1. **Reduces computational cost** for downstream algorithms
2. **Mitigates overfitting** by removing noisy/irrelevant features
3. **Enables visualization** of high-D data in 2D/3D
4. **Resolves multicollinearity** (e.g., PCA creates orthogonal components)
5. **Improves clustering performance** by removing noise dimensions

---

## 📘 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **K-Means clustering algorithm** with its objective function. Derive the centroid update rule and discuss the **K-Means++ initialization** strategy. Mention any two limitations of K-Means.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a suitable block diagram, explain the steps of **Principal Component Analysis (PCA)**. Derive the **eigenvalue formulation** from the constrained optimization problem. The covariance matrix of a 2D dataset (after centering) is given as $S = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 2 \\ 2 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$. Compute its eigenvalues and the explained variance ratio for the first principal component.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare **K-Means** and **DBSCAN** clustering algorithms across at least five parameters. Explain the concepts of **core points, border points, and noise points** in DBSCAN with suitable diagrams. Discuss why DBSCAN is preferred for datasets with arbitrary-shaped clusters.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **t-SNE algorithm** in detail. How does it differ from PCA? Discuss the role of the **perplexity hyperparameter** and the **Student t-distribution** in t-SNE.

---

## 5.1 Model Solutions — Question A

### Solution to A(a) — K-Means Algorithm

**Algorithm Steps (3 marks):**
1. **Input:** Dataset $X = \{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n\}$, number of clusters $k$.
2. **Initialize** $k$ centroids $\{\mu_1, \mu_2, \dots, \mu_k\}$ — preferably using K-Means++.
3. **Assignment Step:** For each $x_i$, assign it to the cluster with the nearest centroid:
$$
C_i^{(t)} = \arg\min_{j} \vert\vert x_i - \mu_j^{(t)} \vert\vert^2
$$
4. **Update Step:** Recompute centroids as cluster means:
$$
\mu_j^{(t+1)} = \frac{1}{\vert C_j^{(t)} \vert}\sum_{x_i \in C_j^{(t)}} x_i
$$
5. **Repeat** Steps 3–4 until convergence.

**Objective Function (1.5 marks):**
$$
J(C) = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \sum_{x_i \in C_j} \vert\vert x_i - \mu_j \vert\vert^2
$$

**Centroid Update Derivation (1 mark):** Setting $\frac{\partial J}{\partial \mu_j} = 0$ gives $\mu_j = \frac{1}{\vert C_j \vert}\sum_{x_i \in C_j} x_i$ (shown in Section 3.3).

**K-Means++ Initialization (1 mark):**
- Pick first centroid uniformly at random.
- For each subsequent centroid, pick a point $x_i$ with probability proportional to its squared distance from the nearest already-chosen centroid:
$$
P(x_i) = \frac{D(x_i)^2}{\sum_j D(x_j)^2}
$$
This ensures **better starting centroids**, reducing convergence to poor local minima.

**Limitations (1 mark — any two):**
1. Must pre-specify $k$ (number of clusters).
2. Sensitive to outlier and noise points.
3. Assumes **spherical, equal-variance** clusters — fails for elongated/irregular shapes.
4. Sensitive to initialization (different runs → different clusters).
5. Does not work well with **non-linear** cluster boundaries.

---

### Solution to A(b) — PCA with Numerical

**Block Diagram (2 marks):** Refer to Section 4.2 Mermaid block diagram.

**Steps (1.5 marks):**
1. Mean-center the data.
2. Compute the covariance matrix $S$.
3. Solve the eigenvalue problem $Sv = \lambda v$.
4. Sort eigenvalues in descending order.
5. Project data onto the top $k$ eigenvectors.

**Eigenvalue Derivation (1.5 marks):** As shown in Section 3.1, the constrained optimization:
$$
\max_v \, v^T S v \quad \text{subject to} \quad v^T v = 1
$$
via Lagrangian $\mathcal{L} = v^T S v - \lambda(v^T v - 1)$ gives the eigenvalue equation $Sv = \lambda v$.

**Numerical (2 marks):**

Given $S = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 2 \\ 2 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$:

Eigenvalue equation:
$$
\det(S - \lambda I) = 0 \implies (4-\lambda)(3-\lambda) - 4 = 0
$$
$$
\lambda^2 - 7\lambda + 12 - 4 = 0 \implies \lambda^2 - 7\lambda + 8 = 0
$$

Using the quadratic formula:
$$
\lambda = \frac{7 \pm \sqrt{49 - 32}}{2} = \frac{7 \pm \sqrt{17}}{2}
$$

$$
\lambda_1 = \frac{7 + 4.123}{2} = 5.561, \quad \lambda_2 = \frac{7 - 4.123}{2} = 1.439
$$

**Explained Variance Ratio for PC1:**
$$
\eta_1 = \frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_1 + \lambda_2} = \frac{5.561}{5.561 + 1.439} = \frac{5.561}{7.000} \approx 0.794 = 79.4\%
$$

**Conclusion:** The first principal component captures **79.4%** of the total variance.

---

## 5.2 Model Solutions — Question B

### Solution to B(a) — K-Means vs DBSCAN

**Comparison Table (3.5 marks):**

| Parameter | K-Means | DBSCAN |
|---|---|---|
| **Cluster Shape** | Spherical / convex | Arbitrary |
| **Number of Clusters** | Must be pre-specified | Determined automatically |
| **Outlier Handling** | Sensitive — outliers pull centroids | Robust — explicitly marked as noise |
| **Parameter Sensitivity** | Sensitive to initialization & $k$ | Sensitive to $\epsilon$ and MinPts |
| **Scalability** | $O(nkdi)$ — highly scalable | $O(n \log n)$ with spatial index, otherwise $O(n^2)$ |
| **Density Assumption** | Assumes equal cluster density | Works with varying densities |
| **Cluster Size** | Tends to produce equal-size clusters | Naturally finds uneven clusters |

**DBSCAN Concepts (2.5 marks):**
- **Core Point:** Has at least **MinPts** points (including itself) within $\epsilon$-radius. Forms the cluster's dense interior.
- **Border Point:** Lies within $\epsilon$ of a core point but has fewer than MinPts neighbors itself. Lies on cluster's fringe.
- **Noise Point:** Neither core nor border. Treated as **outlier**.

**Why DBSCAN for Arbitrary Shapes (1 mark):** DBSCAN groups points based on **density reachability** — any chain of points within $\epsilon$ of each other forms a cluster. This is shape-agnostic, unlike K-Means which uses Euclidean distance to centroids (forcing convex shapes).

---

### Solution to B(b) — t-SNE

**Algorithm (3 marks):**
1. Compute pairwise similarities in high-D space using a **Gaussian kernel** centered on each point:
$$
p_{j \mid i} = \frac{\exp(-\vert\vert x_i - x_j \vert\vert^2 / 2\sigma_i^2)}{\sum_{k \neq i}\exp(-\vert\vert x_i - x_k \vert\vert^2 / 2\sigma_i^2)}
$$
2. Symmetrize: $p_{ij} = \frac{p_{j \mid i} + p_{i \mid j}}{2n}$
3. Initialize low-D points $y_i$ randomly (usually from $\mathcal{N}(0, 10^{-4}I)$).
4. Compute low-D similarities using **Student t-distribution with 1 degree of freedom**:
$$
q_{ij} = \frac{(1 + \vert\vert y_i - y_j \vert\vert^2)^{-1}}{\sum_{k \neq l}(1 + \vert\vert y_k - y_l \vert\vert^2)^{-1}}
$$
5. Minimize the **Kullback–Leibler (KL) divergence** $KL(P \vert\vert Q) = \sum_{ij} p_{ij} \log \frac{p_{ij}}{q_{ij}}$ using **gradient descent**.

**PCA vs t-SNE (2 marks):**

| Aspect | PCA | t-SNE |
|---|---|---|
| **Linearity** | Linear transformation | Non-linear |
| **Goal** | Maximize **global variance** | Preserve **local neighborhoods** |
| **Output Use** | General DR, features for ML | Primarily **2D/3D visualization** |
| **Computational Cost** | $O(d^3 + nd^2)$ | $O(n^2)$ — slow for large $n$ |
| **Determinism** | Deterministic | Stochastic (different runs → different maps) |
| **Interpretability of Axes** | Components have meaning | Axes have **no meaning** |

**Perplexity (1 mark):** $\text{Perp}(P_i) = 2^{H(P_i)}$ where $H$ is Shannon entropy. It loosely corresponds to the **effective number of neighbors** for each point. Typical range: 5–50. Controls the **bandwidth $\sigma_i$** of the Gaussian in high-D.

**Student t-Distribution (1 mark):** In low-D, t-SNE uses a **heavy-tailed t-distribution** with 1 d.o.f. (Cauchy). This solves the **crowding problem** — moderate distances in high-D can be modeled as much larger distances in low-D without penalty, allowing clusters to spread out naturally.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **❌ Skipping the Lagrangian step in PCA derivation** → Lose 4 marks. Always write the constrained optimization and Lagrangian explicitly.
> 2. **❌ Confusing explained variance ratio with eigenvalue** → They are **different**. Eigenvalue = variance along PC; ratio = proportion of total variance.
> 3. **❌ Forgetting to mean-center data before PCA** → This is the **#1 mistake** in numerical PCA problems. Always center first.
> 4. **❌ Confusing DBSCAN's MinPts with $k$ in K-Means** → MinPts is the **minimum neighborhood size**, not the number of clusters.
> 5. **❌ Writing K-Means algorithm without specifying the stopping criterion** → Examiners deduct 1 mark.
> 6. **❌ In t-SNE questions, omitting the KL divergence objective** → Always state the loss function explicitly.
> 7. **❌ In hierarchical clustering, not specifying the linkage type** → Single, complete, average, and Ward's are **not interchangeable**.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 3 Rapid Revision Checklist — Clustering and Dimensionality Reduction**

### 🔹 Clustering Essentials
- ✅ Clustering is **unsupervised** — no labels used during training.
- ✅ K-Means minimizes **WCSS / Inertia** $J = \sum_i \sum_{x \in C_i} \vert\vert x - \mu_i \vert\vert^2$.
- ✅ K-Means++ initialization improves convergence to good local minima.
- ✅ K-Means assumes **spherical, equal-variance clusters** — its biggest limitation.
- ✅ **Elbow method** plots WCSS vs $k$ — choose the "elbow" point.
- ✅ **Silhouette score** is the most reliable internal metric for $k$ selection.
- ✅ Hierarchical clustering produces a **dendrogram** — cut at desired height to get clusters.
- ✅ **Ward's linkage** minimizes WCSS increase — best for compact, spherical clusters.
- ✅ DBSCAN uses **$\epsilon$ (neighborhood radius)** and **MinPts** — does NOT need $k$.
- ✅ DBSCAN handles **arbitrary shapes** and **outliers** — major advantage.
- ✅ Core, Border, and Noise are the three DBSCAN point types.
- ✅ Distance metrics: Euclidean, Manhattan, Minkowski, Cosine — know formulas.

### 🔹 Dimensionality Reduction Essentials
- ✅ **Curse of dimensionality** → sparsity, distance concentration, overfitting.
- ✅ PCA finds orthogonal directions of **maximum variance** (linear method).
- ✅ PCA steps: Center → Covariance → Eigendecomposition → Sort → Project.
- ✅ PCA objective: $\max_v v^T S v$ subject to $v^T v = 1$ → $Sv = \lambda v$.
- ✅ **Explained variance ratio** $\eta_j = \frac{\lambda_j}{\sum_i \lambda_i}$ — sum top-$k$ ratios to get total variance preserved.
- ✅ Always **mean-center** before applying PCA (mandatory step).
- ✅ **t-SNE** is non-linear, preserves **local** structure, ideal for visualization.
- ✅ **t-SNE uses KL divergence** as loss; perplexity controls neighborhood size.
- ✅ **UMAP** is faster than t-SNE and preserves more global structure.
- ✅ LDA is **supervised** (uses class labels) — do not confuse with PCA.
- ✅ Reconstruction error: $\text{Error} = \sum_{i=k+1}^{d} \lambda_i$.

### 🔹 Evaluation & Selection
- ✅ Use **Silhouette, Davies-Bouldin, Calinski-Harabasz** for internal validation.
- ✅ Use **ARI, NMI, Fowlkes-Mallows** for external validation (when labels exist).
- ✅ Always **scale features** (StandardScaler / MinMax) before K-Means and PCA.

> **🎯 Final KTU Exam Tip:** For 14-mark questions, the formula is simple — **Definition (2) + Algorithm/Diagram (4) + Mathematical Derivation (4) + Numerical/Comparison (4)**. Hit all four quadrants and full marks are guaranteed.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solving linear programming problems, mixed integer programming and vehicle routing problems, solving non-linear and mixed integer non-linear programming problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET861 - DATA SCIENCE WITH JULIA
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Solving linear programming problems, mixed integer programming and vehicle routing problems, solving non-linear and mixed integer non-linear programming problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Mathematical Optimization in Julia?

**Mathematical Optimization** (also called Mathematical Programming) is the process of selecting the best element (with regard to some criterion) from a set of available alternatives. In the context of data science and operations research, this is expressed as the minimization (or maximization) of an **objective function** subject to a set of **constraints** defined over a set of **decision variables**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Canonical Form):**
> $$\min_{x \in \mathbb{R}^n} f(x) \quad \text{subject to} \quad g_i(x) \leq 0, \;\; i = 1, \dots, m$$
> $$\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad h_j(x) = 0, \;\; j = 1, \dots, p$$
> Here, $x$ is the vector of decision variables, $f$ is the objective function, $g_i$ are inequality constraints, and $h_j$ are equality constraints.

### Conceptual Analogy: The "Compass and Map" of Decision Making

Imagine you are planning a road trip from **Kochi to Delhi** with a fixed budget of ₹15,000, 4 days of vacation, and a fuel-efficient car. You have hundreds of possible routes. Each route has a *cost* (fuel + toll), a *time*, and a *scenery rating* (your personal objective). 

- The **map** represents the **feasible region** (all routes satisfying budget and time constraints).
- The **compass** is the **objective function** (you want to maximize scenery OR minimize cost).
- The **destination pinpoint** is the **optimal solution** — the single best route that lies on the map and aligns with your compass.

In Julia, the **JuMP (Julia for Mathematical Programming)** framework is the "GPS system" that finds this pinpoint for problems with millions of variables and constraints.

---

## 1.2 Taxonomy of Optimization Problems

| Problem Class | Objective & Constraints | Decision Variables | Solver Family in Julia |
|---------------|------------------------|-------------------|----------------------|
| **LP** (Linear Programming) | All linear | Continuous | GLPK, HiGHS, CLP |
| **MIP** (Mixed-Integer Programming) | All linear | Mixed (continuous + integer) | HiGHS, CBC, Gurobi |
| **NLP** (Non-Linear Programming) | Non-linear or non-linear constraints | Continuous | Ipopt, KNITRO |
| **MINLP** (Mixed-Integer Non-Linear) | Non-linear | Mixed | SCIP, Juniper, KNITRO |
| **VRP** (Vehicle Routing) | Mixed (discrete + continuous) | Mixed | Specialized OR-Tools, JuMP + CBC |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A problem is classified as **linear** ONLY if the objective function and ALL constraints are linear in the decision variables. The moment any one of them is non-linear (e.g., $x^2$, $\sin(x)$, $x_1 x_2$), the problem graduates to the **non-linear (NLP)** category.

---

## 1.3 The JuMP Modeling Workflow

JuMP is a domain-specific modeling language embedded in Julia for mathematical optimization. It decouples the *modeling* of a problem from the *solving* of it.

### Conceptual Analogy: "The Universal Remote Control"

Think of JuMP as a universal remote control:

- You (the user) press buttons in a standardized way: `model = Model()`, `@variable`, `@constraint`, `@objective`.
- The remote emits **infrared signals** that work with **any TV brand** (GLPK, Gurobi, CPLEX, Ipopt) plugged in via `set_optimizer(model, solver)`.
- You never have to learn the proprietary language of each TV manufacturer.

This separation is the **defining power** of JuMP and is heavily tested in KTU examinations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why JuMP over Python's PuLP or Pyomo?**
> - **Performance:** Julia's JIT compilation makes JuMP up to **10x–100x faster** for large-scale problems.
> - **Differentiability:** Seamless integration with automatic differentiation (via `ForwardDiff.jl`).
> - **Composability:** Tight coupling with the Julia data ecosystem (DataFrames.jl, Plots.jl).
> - **Solver agnosticism:** Switch solvers with a single line of code.

---

## 1.4 High-Yield Vocabulary for KTU Exams

| Term | Definition | Example |
|------|------------|---------|
| **Decision Variable** | The unknowns we control | $x$ = number of cars produced |
| **Objective Function** | The quantity to optimize | Maximize profit: $\max 30x_1 + 45x_2$ |
| **Constraint** | A restriction on variables | $2x_1 + 3x_2 \leq 100$ (labor hours) |
| **Feasible Region** | The set of all points satisfying all constraints | Polygon in 2D |
| **Optimal Solution** | The best feasible point | Vertex with highest profit |
| **Slack Variable** | Converts $\leq$ to $=$ | $2x_1 + 3x_2 + s = 100$ |
| **Surplus Variable** | Converts $\geq$ to $=$ | $2x_1 + 3x_2 - s = 100$ |
| **Binary Variable** | Variable restricted to $\{0, 1\}$ | $y = 1$ if truck is used, else $0$ |
| **Integer Variable** | Variable restricted to $\mathbb{Z}$ | $n$ = number of machines (whole) |
| **Big-M** | Penalty constant for logical constraints | $M \approx 10^6$ |
| **Feasibility** | Whether a solution satisfies all constraints | `termination_status(model)` |
| **Optimality** | Whether no better solution exists | `primal_status(model) == FEASIBLE_POINT` |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Linear Programming (LP) — The Foundation

A **Linear Program** is an optimization problem where the objective function and ALL constraints are linear in the decision variables.

### Standard Form (KTU Exam Critical)

$$\min_{x \in \mathbb{R}^n} c^T x$$

$$\text{subject to} \quad A x \leq b$$

$$\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad x \geq 0$$

where $c \in \mathbb{R}^n$, $A \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$, $b \in \mathbb{R}^m$.

### Key Properties of LP

1. **The feasible region is a convex polyhedron** (or empty, or unbounded).
2. **If an optimal solution exists, it exists at a vertex (basic feasible solution)** — this is the *Fundamental Theorem of LP*.
3. **The Simplex Method** (Dantzig, 1947) walks along edges of the polyhedron from vertex to vertex, always improving the objective.
4. **Interior Point Methods** (Karmarkar, 1984) cut through the interior using logarithmic barriers.

### LP Solvers in Julia

| Solver | Type | Open Source? | Speed | Commercial? |
|--------|------|--------------|-------|-------------|
| **GLPK** | Simplex + Interior Point | ✅ Yes | Medium | No |
| **HiGHS** | Simplex + IPM | ✅ Yes | Fast | No |
| **Clp** | Simplex | ✅ Yes | Fast | No |
| **Gurobi** | Simplex + IPM + Parallel | ❌ No | **Very Fast** | Yes (free academic) |
| **CPLEX** | Simplex + IPM | ❌ No | Very Fast | Yes (free academic) |

---

## 2.2 Mixed-Integer Programming (MIP)

A **Mixed-Integer Program** is an LP where some variables are required to be **integers** or **binary**.

$$\min c^T x + d^T y$$

$$\text{subject to} \quad A x + B y \leq b$$

$$\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad x \geq 0, \quad y \in \mathbb{Z}^p, \quad y_i \in \{0, 1\} \text{ for } i \in \mathcal{B}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** MIP is **NP-hard** in general. Solvers use **Branch-and-Bound** combined with **LP relaxation** at each node. A common pattern is to first solve the LP relaxation (ignore integrality), then branch on fractional variables.

### The Big-M Method

Logical constraints like "either $x \leq 5$ **OR** $x \geq 10$" are encoded using binary variables $y \in \{0,1\}$ and a large constant $M$:

$$x \leq 5 + M(1 - y)$$

$$x \geq 10 - M y$$

$$y \in \{0, 1\}$$

When $M$ is "big enough" to not bind on the feasible region, this linearization is exact.

---

## 2.3 Vehicle Routing Problems (VRP)

The **Capacitated Vehicle Routing Problem (CVRP)** is the canonical logistics optimization: serve a set of customers using a fleet of vehicles of limited capacity, minimizing total distance.

### Mathematical Formulation

Given:
- A **depot** (node 0) and $n$ customers (nodes $1, \dots, n$).
- Distance matrix $d_{ij}$ between nodes $i$ and $j$.
- Vehicle capacity $Q$ and identical fleet of $K$ vehicles.

**Decision Variables:**
- $x_{ijk} \in \{0, 1\}$: 1 if vehicle $k$ travels directly from $i$ to $j$, else 0.
- $u_{ik} \geq 0$: load of vehicle $k$ after visiting node $i$ (for subtour elimination).

**Formulation:**

$$\min \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{i=0}^{n} \sum_{j=0}^{n} d_{ij} x_{ijk}$$

$$\sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{j=1}^{n} x_{0jk} \leq K \quad \text{(each vehicle leaves depot at most once)}$$

$$\sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{i=0}^{n} x_{ijk} = 1 \quad \forall j = 1, \dots, n \quad \text{(each customer visited exactly once)}$$

$$\sum_{i=0}^{n} x_{ijk} = \sum_{i=0}^{n} x_{jik} \quad \forall j = 0, \dots, n, \; \forall k \quad \text{(flow conservation)}$$

$$u_{ik} + q_j \leq u_{jk} + Q(1 - x_{ijk}) \quad \text{(capacity + subtour elimination, MTZ constraint)}$$

$$x_{ijk} \in \{0, 1\}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why MTZ Constraints?** Without the Miller-Tucker-Zemlin constraints, solvers can produce disconnected sub-tours (e.g., 3 vehicles each doing a 2-customer loop). The MTZ constraints force a single tour per vehicle.

---

## 2.4 Non-Linear Programming (NLP)

A **Non-Linear Program** has at least one non-linear term in the objective or constraints:

$$\min_{x} f(x) \quad \text{subject to} \quad g(x) \leq 0, \;\; h(x) = 0, \;\; x \in \mathbb{R}^n$$

### Why is NLP Harder than LP?

1. **Feasible region is non-convex** in general → multiple local optima exist.
2. **No vertex structure** → Simplex Method does not apply.
3. **Gradients and Hessians** must be computed → automatic differentiation is critical.
4. **KKT conditions** are *necessary* but not *sufficient* for global optimality.

### KKT Conditions (First-Order Optimality)

For $\min f(x)$ subject to $g_i(x) \leq 0$:

1. **Stationarity:** $\nabla f(x^*) + \sum_i \lambda_i \nabla g_i(x^*) = 0$
2. **Primal feasibility:** $g_i(x^*) \leq 0$
3. **Dual feasibility:** $\lambda_i \geq 0$
4. **Complementary slackness:** $\lambda_i \cdot g_i(x^*) = 0$

### NLP Solvers in Julia

| Solver | Algorithm | Open Source? | Key Use |
|--------|-----------|--------------|---------|
| **Ipopt** | Interior Point | ✅ Yes | Large-scale smooth NLP |
| **KNITRO** | Active Set + Interior | ❌ Commercial | Industrial NLP |
| **NLopt** | Many (SQP, COBYLA) | ✅ Yes | Black-box, small problems |
| **Optim.jl** | Gradient-free + BFGS | ✅ Yes | Unconstrained |

---

## 2.5 Mixed-Integer Non-Linear Programming (MINLP)

The "beast" of optimization: non-linear objective/constraints **AND** integer variables.

$$\min f(x, y) \quad \text{s.t.} \quad g(x, y) \leq 0, \;\; x \in \mathbb{R}^n, \;\; y \in \mathbb{Z}^p$$

### Solution Strategies

| Strategy | Idea | Julia Package |
|----------|------|---------------|
| **Branch-and-Bound on NLP** | Solve NLP at each node | Juniper.jl |
| **Outer Approximation** | Iteratively add linear cuts | SCIP.jl |
| **Generalized Benders** | Dual-based decomposition | Custom |
| **Extended Cutting Plane** | Add KKT cuts | ECP.jl |

---

## 2.6 The KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Bookmark this table — it covers 90% of KTU numerical questions on Module 4.**

| Concept | Formula / Condition | Engineering Application |
|---------|-------------------|------------------------|
| **LP Canonical Form** | $\min c^T x$ s.t. $Ax \leq b, \; x \geq 0$ | Production planning, diet problem |
| **LP Dual (Primal = min)** | $\max b^T u$ s.t. $A^T u \leq c, \; u \geq 0$ | Shadow prices, sensitivity |
| **Strong Duality** | $c^T x^* = b^T u^*$ | Optimality certificate |
| **MIP LP Relaxation** | Remove $y \in \mathbb{Z}$ constraint | Lower bound for branch-and-bound |
| **Big-M Disjunction** | $x \leq a + M(1-y); \; x \geq b - My; \; y \in \{0,1\}$ | Either-or constraints |
| **CVRP Capacity** | $\sum_{i} q_i x_{ijk} \leq Q \; \forall k$ | Truck loading |
| **MTZ Subtour Elim.** | $u_{ik} - u_{jk} + Q \cdot x_{ijk} \leq Q - q_j$ | VRP/TSP |
| **KKT Stationarity** | $\nabla f(x^*) + \sum \lambda_i \nabla g_i(x^*) = 0$ | NLP optimality |
| **Complementary Slackness** | $\lambda_i g_i(x^*) = 0$ | Active vs. inactive constraints |
| **Lagrangian** | $\mathcal{L}(x, \lambda) = f(x) + \sum \lambda_i g_i(x)$ | Dual bounds for NLP |
| **Newton Step (Interior Point)** | $\Delta x = -[\nabla^2 \mathcal{L}]^{-1} \nabla \mathcal{L}$ | NLP solver core |
| **MINLP Master Problem** | $\min \mu$ s.t. linearizations $\mu \geq f(x^{(k)}) + \nabla f^T (x - x^{(k)})$ | Outer approximation |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Domain | Problem Type | Impact |
|--------|--------------|--------|
| **Supply Chain Logistics** | VRP | Save 10–25% fuel in last-mile delivery (Amazon, Flipkart) |
| **Power Grid Dispatch** | LP / MIP | Unit commitment, economic dispatch |
| **Chip Design** | MIP | VLSI placement, routing |
| **Finance** | LP | Portfolio optimization (Markowitz) |
| **Chemical Engineering** | NLP / MINLP | Process optimization, reactor design |
| **Telecommunications** | MIP | Network flow, 5G resource allocation |
| **Aerospace** | NLP | Trajectory optimization (SpaceX landing burns) |
| **Agriculture (KTU Context)** | LP | Crop planning in Kerala — coconut, paddy, rubber mix |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Lagrangian Dual of an LP

We start with a primal LP in canonical form:

$$\min c^T x \quad \text{s.t.} \quad Ax \geq b, \;\; x \geq 0$$

### Step 1: Form the Lagrangian

Introduce a multiplier $\lambda \geq 0$ for each inequality constraint $Ax \geq b$:

$$\mathcal{L}(x, \lambda) = c^T x - \lambda^T (Ax - b) = c^T x - \lambda^T A x + \lambda^T b$$

### Step 2: Group the $x$ terms

Rearrange the Lagrangian by factoring out $x$:

$$\mathcal{L}(x, \lambda) = (c - A^T \lambda)^T x + \lambda^T b$$

### Step 3: Take the infimum over $x \geq 0$

The dual function is $g(\lambda) = \inf_{x \geq 0} \mathcal{L}(x, \lambda)$. 

For the infimum to be finite (not $-\infty$), we need the coefficient of $x$ to be non-negative:

$$c - A^T \lambda \geq 0 \quad \iff \quad A^T \lambda \leq c$$

If this holds, the infimum is achieved at $x = 0$, giving $g(\lambda) = \lambda^T b$.

### Step 4: State the Dual

$$\max_{\lambda} b^T \lambda \quad \text{s.t.} \quad A^T \lambda \leq c, \;\; \lambda \geq 0$$

> **Conversion logic:** Each primal constraint gives a dual variable; each primal variable gives a dual constraint. The "min" becomes "max" with the right-hand side $b$ as the new objective.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: KKT Conditions for a Constrained NLP

Consider:

$$\min f(x) \quad \text{s.t.} \quad g_i(x) \leq 0, \; i = 1, \dots, m$$

### Step 1: Define the Lagrangian

$$\mathcal{L}(x, \lambda) = f(x) + \sum_{i=1}^{m} \lambda_i g_i(x)$$

### Step 2: Take the Partial Derivatives

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x_j} = \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_j} + \sum_{i=1}^{m} \lambda_i \frac{\partial g_i}{\partial x_j} = 0 \quad \forall j$$

In vector form:

$$\nabla_x \mathcal{L} = \nabla f(x) + \sum_{i=1}^{m} \lambda_i \nabla g_i(x) = 0$$

This is the **stationarity** condition.

### Step 3: Apply Complementary Slackness

If a constraint is *strictly inactive* ($g_i(x^*) < 0$), then $\lambda_i = 0$ (the multiplier must be zero because increasing the constraint bound doesn't help). Conversely, if a constraint is *active* ($g_i(x^*) = 0$), $\lambda_i$ may be positive.

$$\lambda_i \cdot g_i(x^*) = 0 \quad \forall i$$

### Step 4: Combine All Four Conditions

The complete KKT system:

$$\nabla f(x^*) + \sum_{i} \lambda_i^* \nabla g_i(x^*) = 0$$

$$g_i(x^*) \leq 0, \;\; \lambda_i^* \geq 0, \;\; \lambda_i^* g_i(x^*) = 0$$

> **Conversion logic:** KKT conditions are *necessary* for any local optimum in a smooth problem with constraint qualifications (e.g., LICQ). They become *sufficient* if $f$ is convex and the feasible set is convex.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: The MTZ Subtour-Elimination Constraint

This is a critical KTU derivation. Consider a TSP with 4 cities and a single vehicle. The solution $x_{12} = x_{23} = x_{34} = x_{41} = 1$ is a *sub-tour* (disconnected from the depot).

### Step 1: Load Variables

Let $u_i$ be the cumulative load/visit order when the vehicle reaches city $i$. For TSP, set $q_j = 1$ (each city visited once) and $Q = n$ (max possible).

### Step 2: Construct the MTZ Constraint

For every arc $(i, j)$ with $i \neq 0, j \neq 0$:

$$u_i - u_j + Q \cdot x_{ij} \leq Q - q_j \quad \forall i, j \neq 0$$

### Step 3: Verify It Eliminates Sub-Tours

Suppose $i = 1, j = 2$ and $x_{12} = 1$. Then:

$$u_1 - u_2 + Q \leq Q - 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad u_2 \geq u_1 + 1$$

This forces the load to *strictly increase* along any arc, so the tour cannot return to a previous city.

### Step 4: Combine with the Tour-Return Constraint

Also require $u_0 = 0$ (depot load) and $q_i \leq u_i \leq Q$ for all customers.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: LP Relaxation as a Lower Bound for MIP

Given a MIP: $\min c^T x + d^T y$ s.t. $Ax + By \leq b$, $x \geq 0$, $y \in \{0,1\}^p$.

### Step 1: Relax Integrality

Define the **LP relaxation** $P_{LP}$:

$$\min c^T x + d^T y \quad \text{s.t.} \quad Ax + By \leq b, \;\; x \geq 0, \;\; 0 \leq y \leq 1$$

### Step 2: Bound the MIP Optimum

Let $z^*_{MIP}$ be the optimal MIP value and $z^*_{LP}$ be the optimal LP relaxation value. Since $P_{LP}$ contains $P_{MIP}$ as a subset (we are enlarging the feasible set), and both minimize the same objective:

$$z^*_{LP} \leq z^*_{MIP}$$

The LP relaxation is a **lower bound** for the MIP.

### Step 3: Branch-and-Bound Logic

At each branch node, the solver solves an LP relaxation. If the solution has $y_j \notin \{0,1\}$, it creates two child nodes with $y_j = 0$ and $y_j = 1$ added as constraints. The global lower bound is the minimum of all open-node LP values; the global upper bound is the minimum of all integer-feasible solutions found so far. The algorithm terminates when lower = upper.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example (KTU 14-Mark Style): Production Planning LP

> **A KTU electronics firm in Kochi produces two products A and B.**
> - Product A: profit ₹40/unit, requires 2 hours assembly + 1 hour testing.
> - Product B: profit ₹30/unit, requires 1 hour assembly + 2 hours testing.
> - Available: 100 hours assembly, 80 hours testing per week.
> - Demand constraint: At most 60 units of A can be sold.
> 
> **Formulate, solve, and interpret.**

### Step 1: Define Variables

Let $x_A$ = units of A, $x_B$ = units of B.

### Step 2: Write the LP

$$\max Z = 40 x_A + 30 x_B$$

$$\text{s.t.} \quad 2 x_A + x_B \leq 100 \quad \text{(assembly hours)}$$

$$x_A + 2 x_B \leq 80 \quad \text{(testing hours)}$$

$$x_A \leq 60 \quad \text{(demand)}$$

$$x_A, x_B \geq 0$$

### Step 3: Convert to JuMP Code (Mental Mapping)

```
model = Model(HiGHS.Optimizer)
@variable(model, xA >= 0)
@variable(model, xB >= 0)
@objective(model, Max, 40*xA + 30*xB)
@constraint(model, 2*xA + xB <= 100)
@constraint(model, xA + 2*xB <= 80)
@constraint(model, xA <= 60)
optimize!(model)
```

### Step 4: Solve Graphically (KTU Exam Method)

Corner points of feasible region:
- $(0, 0) \to Z = 0$
- $(50, 0) \to Z = 2000$ (assembly binding)
- $(40, 20) \to Z = 2200$ (assembly + testing binding)
- $(20, 30) \to Z = 1700$ (testing binding)
- $(0, 40) \to Z = 1200$

### Step 5: Optimal Solution

$$x_A^* = 40, \; x_B^* = 20, \; Z^* = ₹2200$$

> **Conversion logic:** The optimal mix produces 40 units of A and 20 units of B weekly, exhausting both assembly (100 hrs) and testing (80 hrs) capacities. The demand constraint is not binding.

---

## 3.6 Worked Example: VRP with 1 Depot and 4 Customers

> **Kerala courier company. Depot in Ernakulam. 4 customers in nearby towns. 1 vehicle, capacity 100 kg. Demands: c1=30, c2=20, c3=40, c4=10 kg. Distance matrix (km):**
> 
> | | Depot | c1 | c2 | c3 | c4 |
> |-|-|-|-|-|-|
> | Depot | 0 | 10 | 15 | 20 | 25 |
> | c1 | 10 | 0 | 12 | 18 | 22 |
> | c2 | 15 | 12 | 0 | 8 | 17 |
> | c3 | 20 | 18 | 8 | 0 | 10 |
> | c4 | 25 | 22 | 17 | 10 | 0 |

### Step 1: JuMP Formulation

```
using JuMP, HiGHS
n = 4
Q = 100
q = [30, 20, 40, 10]
d = [0 10 15 20 25; 10 0 12 18 22; 15 12 0 8 17; 20 18 8 0 10; 25 22 17 10 0]

model = Model(HiGHS.Optimizer)
@variable(model, x[0:n, 0:n], Bin)        # arc usage
@variable(model, u[0:n] >= 0)              # load after node i

@objective(model, Min, sum(d[i,j] * x[i,j] for i in 0:n, j in 0:n))

# Each customer visited once
for j in 1:n
    @constraint(model, sum(x[i,j] for i in 0:n) == 1)
end

# Flow conservation
for k in 1:n
    @constraint(model, sum(x[i,k] for i in 0:n) == sum(x[k,j] for j in 0:n))
end

# Leave depot once
@constraint(model, sum(x[0,j] for j in 1:n) == 1)

# MTZ constraints
for i in 1:n, j in 1:n
    if i != j
        @constraint(model, u[i] - u[j] + Q*x[i,j] <= Q - q[j])
    end
end

@constraint(model, u[0] == 0)
for i in 1:n
    @constraint(model, u[i] >= q[i])
    @constraint(model, u[i] <= Q)
end

optimize!(model)
```

### Step 2: Solution

Best route: **Depot → c1 → c2 → c3 → c4 → Depot** with total distance $10 + 12 + 8 + 10 + 25 = 65$ km.

---

## 3.7 NLP Worked Example: Portfolio Optimization (Markowitz)

$$\min w^T \Sigma w \quad \text{s.t.} \quad \mu^T w \geq r_{\text{target}}, \;\; \sum_i w_i = 1, \;\; w_i \geq 0$$

This is a **Quadratic Program (QP)** — a special case of NLP. In JuMP:

```
using JuMP, Ipopt
model = Model(Ipopt.Optimizer)
@variable(model, w[1:n] >= 0)
@objective(model, Min, w' * Σ * w)
@constraint(model, sum(w) == 1)
@constraint(model, μ' * w >= r_target)
optimize!(model)
```

> **Conversion logic:** $w^T \Sigma w$ is non-linear (a quadratic form), so this is an NLP even though the constraints are linear. Ipopt (Interior Point OPTimizer) handles it efficiently.

## 4.1 JuMP Modeling-Solving Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Defines Problem] --> B[Create Model in JuMP]
    B --> C[Declare Decision Variables]
    C --> D[Add Objective Function]
    D --> E[Add Constraints]
    E --> F[Select Solver]
    F --> G{Problem Type?}
    G -->|LP| H[GLPK or HiGHS]
    G -->|MIP| I[HiGHS or Gurobi]
    G -->|NLP| J[Ipopt or KNITRO]
    G -->|MINLP| K[Juniper or SCIP]
    H --> L[Call optimize!]
    I --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M[Query Solution]
    M --> N[value variable]
    M --> O[objective value]
    M --> P[termination status]
```

## 4.2 Optimization Problem Taxonomy Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Mathematical Optimization Problem] --> B{All functions linear?}
    B -->|Yes| C{Any integer variables?}
    B -->|No| D{Any integer variables?}
    C -->|No| E[LP: Linear Programming]
    C -->|Yes| F[MIP: Mixed Integer Programming]
    D -->|No| G[NLP: Non-Linear Programming]
    D -->|Yes| H[MINLP: Mixed Integer NLP]
    E --> I[Solvers: GLPK, HiGHS, Clp]
    F --> J[Solvers: HiGHS, CBC, Gurobi]
    G --> K[Solvers: Ipopt, KNITRO]
    H --> L[Solvers: Juniper, SCIP, Bonmin]
```

## 4.3 Branch-and-Bound Tree for MIP

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Root Node: LP Relaxation] --> B[Branch on y1 fractional]
    A --> C[Branch on y1 fractional]
    B --> D[y1 equals 0]
    C --> E[y1 equals 1]
    D --> F[LP at y1=0]
    E --> G[LP at y1=1]
    F --> H{All integer?}
    G --> I{All integer?}
    H -->|Yes| J[Integer Feasible: Update Upper Bound]
    H -->|No| K[Branch on y2]
    I -->|Yes| L[Integer Feasible: Update Upper Bound]
    I -->|No| M[Branch on y2]
    K --> N[Node y2 equals 0]
    K --> O[Node y2 equals 1]
    M --> P[Node y2 equals 0]
    M --> Q[Node y2 equals 1]
    N --> R[LP Feasible: Update Lower Bound]
    O --> S[Infeasible: Fathom]
    P --> T[LP Feasible: Update Lower Bound]
    Q --> U[Bound worse than incumbent: Fathom]
```

## 4.4 VRP Solution Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Data: Customers, Depots, Vehicles] --> B[Distance Matrix Computation]
    B --> C[Demand Aggregation]
    C --> D[JuMP Model Construction]
    D --> E[Arc Variables: x subscript ijk]
    D --> F[Load Variables: u subscript ik]
    D --> G[Objective: Minimize Total Distance]
    D --> H[Constraints: Assignment]
    D --> I[Constraints: Capacity]
    D --> J[Constraints: MTZ Subtour Elim]
    H --> K[HiGHS or Gurobi Solver]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    G --> K
    K --> L[Solution Extraction]

```

## 4.5 Interior Point Method Flow (Ipopt)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initialize x superscript 0, lambda superscript 0] --> B[Compute KKT Residual]
    B --> C{Converged?}
    C -->|Yes| Z[Return Optimal Solution]
    C -->|No| D[Compute Hessian of Lagrangian]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Linear Programming Problem. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Model Answer:**

A **Linear Programming Problem (LPP)** is an optimization problem in which the objective function and all the constraints are linear functions of the decision variables. It is expressed in canonical form as:

$$\min c^T x \quad \text{s.t.} \quad Ax \leq b, \; x \geq 0$$

**Key Properties:**
1. The feasible region is a convex polyhedron.
2. The objective is linear, ensuring that local optima are global optima.
3. If a finite optimum exists, it occurs at an extreme point (vertex) of the feasible region.

**Example:** A product-mix problem: $\max 5x_1 + 4x_2$ s.t. $6x_1 + 4x_2 \leq 24, \; x_1 + 2x_2 \leq 6, \; x_1, x_2 \geq 0$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking:** Definition (1M), canonical form equation (1M), example (1M).

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between LP and NLP with a suitable example. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | LP | NLP |
|--------|-----|------|
| **Objective function** | Linear in variables | Non-linear in variables |
| **Constraints** | All linear | At least one non-linear |
| **Feasible region** | Convex polyhedron | Generally non-convex |
| **Solution method** | Simplex, Interior Point | KKT, SQP, Interior Point |
| **Guarantee of global optimum** | Yes (under boundedness) | No (may have local optima) |
| **Example** | $\max 3x_1 + 2x_2$ s.t. linear | $\min x^2 + y^2$ s.t. $x + y \geq 5$ |

In the NLP example, the objective $x^2 + y^2$ is a non-linear (quadratic) function, and the feasible region is non-convex due to the curved boundary, making it harder to solve.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking:** Tabular difference (2M), one example distinguishing both (1M).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Format

### Question A: Production Planning Using JuMP [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]

A furniture manufacturer in Thrissur produces **chairs** and **tables**. Each chair requires 3 hours of carpentry and 1 hour of finishing, yielding a profit of ₹25. Each table requires 2 hours of carpentry and 2 hours of finishing, yielding a profit of ₹40. The workshop has 90 hours of carpentry and 60 hours of finishing available per week. Due to market demand, at most 30 chairs can be sold. Formulate this as an LP, solve using JuMP, and find the optimal production mix.

**(a) Formulate the LP mathematically and write the equivalent JuMP code. (7 Marks)**

**(b) Solve the LP using the graphical method and verify with JuMP output. Interpret the shadow prices. (7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

**Step 1: Decision Variables**

Let $x_1$ = number of chairs, $x_2$ = number of tables.

**Step 2: Objective Function**

$$\max Z = 25 x_1 + 40 x_2$$

**Step 3: Constraints**

- Carpentry: $3x_1 + 2x_2 \leq 90$
- Finishing: $x_1 + 2x_2 \leq 60$
- Demand: $x_1 \leq 30$
- Non-negativity: $x_1, x_2 \geq 0$

**Step 4: JuMP Code**

```julia
using JuMP, HiGHS

model = Model(HiGHS.Optimizer)
@variable(model, x1 >= 0)
@variable(model, x2 >= 0)
@objective(model, Max, 25*x1 + 40*x2)
@constraint(model, carpentry, 3*x1 + 2*x2 <= 90)
@constraint(model, finishing, x1 + 2*x2 <= 60)
@constraint(model, demand, x1 <= 30)
optimize!(model)
println("Chairs: ", value(x1))
println("Tables: ", value(x2))
println("Profit: ", objective_value(model))
println("Shadow price (carpentry): ", dual(carpenstry))
```

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**Step 1: Graphical Method**

Corner points of feasible region:
- $(0, 0) \to Z = 0$
- $(30, 0) \to Z = 750$ (demand binding)
- $(30, 15) \to Z = 750 + 600 = 1350$ (demand + finishing binding)
- Intersection of carpentry and finishing: $3x_1 + 2x_2 = 90$ and $x_1 + 2x_2 = 60$ → subtract: $2x_1 = 30 \Rightarrow x_1 = 15, x_2 = 22.5$
- $(15, 22.5) \to Z = 25(15) + 40(22.5) = 375 + 900 = 1275$

**Step 2: Compare Corner Points**

| Corner | $Z$ |
|--------|-----|
| $(0, 0)$ | 0 |
| $(30, 0)$ | 750 |
| $(30, 15)$ | **1350** ← Optimal |
| $(15, 22.5)$ | 1275 |

**Step 3: Optimal Solution**

$$x_1^* = 30, \; x_2^* = 15, \; Z^* = ₹1350$$

**Step 4: Shadow Price Interpretation**

The dual of carpentry (binding) is the marginal worth of one additional carpentry hour. If carpentry increased to 91 hours, profit would rise by approximately the shadow price. Similarly, the finishing constraint is binding at optimum, so an extra finishing hour is valuable.

---

### Question B: Vehicle Routing Problem for Kerala Logistics [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]

A logistics company based in **Kozhikode** operates 2 identical delivery trucks, each with a maximum capacity of **150 kg**. There are 5 customers to be served, with the following demands: $c_1 = 40$ kg, $c_2 = 30$ kg, $c_3 = 50$ kg, $c_4 = 35$ kg, $c_5 = 45$ kg. The distance matrix (km) is given below:

| | Depot | $c_1$ | $c_2$ | $c_3$ | $c_4$ | $c_5$ |
|-|-------|-------|-------|-------|-------|-------|
| Depot | 0 | 12 | 18 | 25 | 30 | 22 |
| $c_1$ | 12 | 0 | 8 | 15 | 20 | 17 |
| $c_2$ | 18 | 8 | 0 | 10 | 14 | 9 |
| $c_3$ | 25 | 15 | 10 | 0 | 6 | 11 |
| $c_4$ | 30 | 20 | 14 | 6 | 0 | 8 |
| $c_5$ | 22 | 17 | 9 | 11 | 8 | 0 |

**(a) Formulate the CVRP as a MIP and write the JuMP code with MTZ constraints. (7 Marks)**

**(b) Solve and identify the optimal routes. Compute the total distance and verify capacity feasibility. (7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

**Step 1: Decision Variables**

- $x_{ijk} \in \{0,1\}$: 1 if truck $k$ travels from $i$ to $j$, else 0.
- $u_{ik} \geq 0$: cumulative load on truck $k$ after visiting $i$.
- $y_k \in \{0,1\}$: 1 if truck $k$ is used, else 0.

**Step 2: Objective**

$$\min \sum_{k=1}^{2} \sum_{i=0}^{5} \sum_{j=0}^{5} d_{ij} x_{ijk}$$

**Step 3: Constraints**

- Each customer served exactly once: $\sum_{k} \sum_{i} x_{ijk} = 1 \; \forall j = 1, \dots, 5$
- Flow conservation: $\sum_{i} x_{ijk} = \sum_{i} x_{jik} \; \forall j, k$
- Vehicle leaves depot at most once: $\sum_{j} x_{0jk} = y_k$
- Capacity: $u_{ik} \leq 150 \cdot y_k$
- MTZ: $u_{ik} - u_{jk} + 150 x_{ijk} \leq 150 - q_j \; \forall i, j \neq 0, k$

**Step 4: JuMP Code (Skeleton)**

```julia
using JuMP, HiGHS
n, K, Q = 5, 2, 150
q = [40, 30, 50, 35, 45]
d = [0 12 18 25 30 22; 12 0 8 15 20 17; 18 8 0 10 14 9;
     25 15 10 0 6 11; 30 20 14 6 0 8; 22 17 9 11 8 0]
model = Model(HiGHS.Optimizer)
@variable(model, x[0:n, 0:n, 1:K], Bin)
@variable(model, u[0:n, 1:K] >= 0)
@variable(model, y[1:K], Bin)
@objective(model, Min, sum(d[i,j]*x[i,j,k] for i in 0:n, j in 0:n, k in 1:K))
for j in 1:n
    @constraint(model, sum(x[i,j,k] for i in 0:n, k in 1:K) == 1)
end
for k in 1:K
    for j in 1:n
        @constraint(model, sum(x[i,j,k] for i in 0:n) == sum(x[j,i,k] for i in 0:n))
    end
end
# MTZ + capacity constraints here...
optimize!(model)
```

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**Step 1: Optimal Routing**

Solving the MIP yields two routes:

- **Truck 1:** Depot → $c_1$ → $c_2$ → $c_5$ → Depot  
  Distance: $12 + 8 + 9 + 22 = 51$ km  
  Load: $40 + 30 + 45 = 115$ kg $\leq 150$ ✓
  
- **Truck 2:** Depot → $c_3$ → $c_4$ → Depot  
  Distance: $25 + 6 + 30 = 61$ km  
  Load: $50 + 35 = 85$ kg $\leq 150$ ✓

**Step 2: Total Distance**

$$Z^* = 51 + 61 = 112 \text{ km}$$

**Step 3: Capacity Verification**

Both trucks operate well within the 150 kg limit. The MTZ constraints ensure no sub-tours exist.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the non-negativity constraints** ($x \geq 0$): Costs 1 mark in most cases.
> 2. **Mixing up Big-M constraints:** If $M$ is too small, the formulation is incorrect. If too large, numerical instability occurs. Always use $M \geq \text{upper bound of } x$.
> 3. **MTZ constraint direction:** The inequality is $u_i - u_j + Qx_{ij} \leq Q - q_j$, NOT $\geq$. Reversing this gives infeasibility.
> 4. **NLP vs. LP confusion:** Any product of variables ($x_1 x_2$) makes a problem non-linear. Writing $x_1 x_2$ inside an LP block is a guaranteed **zero** for that sub-question.
> 5. **Shadow price interpretation:** Shadow price is the *change in objective per unit increase in RHS*, valid only for the current basis and within a range (sensitivity analysis).
> 6. **Forgetting to call `optimize!(model)` in JuMP:** Code compiles but no solution is computed. Always check `termination_status`.
> 7. **Binary vs. integer confusion:** $y \in \{0, 1\}$ is binary (MIP); $y \in \mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$ is general integer. Different solver handling.
> 8. **MTZ works only for symmetric/asymmetric TSP/CVRP:** It does NOT generalize to VRP with time windows (use different constraints).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Module 4:**

- [ ] **LP canonical form:** $\min c^T x$ s.t. $Ax \leq b, \; x \geq 0$ — must know by heart.
- [ ] **LP solvers in Julia:** GLPK, HiGHS (open-source defaults).
- [ ] **MIP = LP + integrality:** Use `@variable(model, y, Int)` or `Bin` in JuMP.
- [ ] **Big-M method:** For either-or constraints; requires $M$ to be sufficiently large.
- [ ] **VRP = MIP with capacity:** Formulate with arc variables + load variables.
- [ ] **MTZ subtour elimination:** $u_i - u_j + Q x_{ij} \leq Q - q_j$ for all customer pairs.
- [ ] **NLP definition:** At least one non-linear element in objective or constraints.
- [ ] **KKT conditions:** Stationarity + Feasibility + Dual Feasibility + Complementary Slackness.
- [ ] **NLP solvers:** Ipopt (open-source), KNITRO (commercial).
- [ ] **MINLP solvers:** Juniper.jl, SCIP.jl, Bonmin — branch-and-bound on NLP relaxations.
- [ ] **JuMP macro cheats:** `@variable`, `@objective`, `@constraint`, `@expression`.
- [ ] **Post-solve queries:** `value(x)`, `objective_value(model)`, `dual(constraint)`, `termination_status(model)`.
- [ ] **Duality:** Primal (min, $\leq$) ↔ Dual (max, $\geq$); $c^T x^* = b^T u^*$ at optimum.
- [ ] **LP relaxation = lower bound** for MIP — fundamental for branch-and-bound.
- [ ] **Portfolio optimization (Markowitz)** is the canonical QP example in NLP.
